[H10] SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES -- H10 covers semiconductor devices, which are electronic components made from semiconductor materials such as silicon or germanium that control, amplify, or switch electric current. The class includes diodes, transistors, thyristors, integrated circuits and related structures, as well as their packaging and interconnection methods.
[H10B] ELECTRONIC MEMORY DEVICES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memory devices consisting of multiple semiconductor or solid-state components. This includes the multiple memory cells constituting the memory core region and logic components within the immediate peripheral region surrounding the memory core region. The memory cells have storage components therein, which may be accessed by selection components.  This includes the following kind of devices:  Volatile Memory Devices:  Non-Volatile Memory Devices:  Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one memory device of this subclass.  Processes and apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of such devices.  **Glossary:** - chip: a piece of a wafer or a substrate that has been processed to contain devices therein or thereon. The expression "diced chip" refers to the result of dicing a wafer or a substrate into a plurality of chips, whereas "undiced chip" refers to a chip before dicing or with no dicing. - device: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.); (depending on the context) can also refer to an integrated device (e.g. CMOS-IC, DRAM device, etc.). A device may be in the form of a bare or packaged chip. - dopant: the atoms or compounds added to a material during doping - doping: the intentional addition of a small quantity of atoms or compounds into a material to achieve a desired characteristic, e.g. to produce an n-type or p-type material - individual: refers to: an electric circuit element not being an integrated device; or a component of an integrated device. Examples of individual devices include: diodes, transistors, photovoltaic cells, Josephson-junction devices, light-emitting diodes [LED], organic LEDs or a single LED component within an integrated device. - integrated device: a device consisting of a plurality of semiconductor or other solid-state electric circuit elements formed in or on a common substrate - integrated circuit: an integrated device where all the electric circuit elements (e.g. diodes, transistors, LEDs, etc.) are formed in or on a common substrate, including interconnections between the elements - component: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.) that is one of a plurality of elements formed in or on a common substrate, e.g. in an integrated device - wafer: it can be one of the following: (a) a slice of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material. For example: a slice of silicon; a slice of a semiconducting compound, e.g. gallium nitride [GaN]; a slice of lithium tantalate [LiTaO 3 ] for superconductor applications. (b) A multilayered laminate, having at least one layer of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material, the layer being meant to be processed into devices. For example: silicon-on-insulator [SOI]; silicon-on-glass [SOG]; silicon-on-sapphire [SOS]; a composite wafer comprising silicon carbide [SiC] on polycrystalline silicon [Si] support; a layer of semiconducting nanowires on glass. A wafer is typically processed by (e.g.) deposition, etching, doping or diffusion, and is then typically diced into chips. - body: the region of semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) within which, or at the surface of which, the physical effects that are characteristic of the device occur, and any bordering semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) that are contiguous with this region. Examples: in a field-effect transistor [FET], the physical effects occur in the channel region between the source and the drain. The semiconductor body includes the channel region, the source and drain regions, and any contiguous semiconductor material; in a light-emitting diode [LED], the physical effects occur at a junction of active semiconductor layers. The semiconductor body includes these active semiconductor layers and any contiguous semiconductor layers, such as buffer layers, possibly a growth substrate, etc., that are between the cathode and anode electrodes; in a thermoelectric device, the solid-state body includes all solid-state materials in the path of current between the electrodes. - electrode: a conductive region in or on the semiconductor body or solid-state body of a device (and other than the body itself) which exerts an electrical influence on the body, irrespective of whether or not an external electrical connection is made thereto. The term covers metallic regions which exert electrical influence on the body through an insulating region (e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling), or inductive coupling arrangements. In a capacitive coupling arrangement, the dielectric region is regarded as part of the electrode. The overall conductive wiring may comprise multiple portions. In such a case, only the wiring portions that exert an electrical influence on the body are considered portions of the electrode. Examples: conductive layer(s) in direct physical contact with the body; conductive region(s) exerting an inductive coupling onto the body; a multilayer structure which exerts influence on the body through an insulating region, e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling. - interconnection: a conductive arrangement for conducting electric current from an electrode of a circuit element to another part of the circuit. Examples include metal wirings. - container: a solid construction in which (one or more) devices are placed, or which is formed around the devices, for forming packaged devices. A container requires a partial or total enclosure and it may also comprise a filling. - encapsulation: an enclosure consisting of (one or more) layers, e.g. comprising organic polymers, which at least partially enclose the (one or more) devices, thereby protecting them. An encapsulation is often used to hermetically seal devices. - field-effect: refers to semiconductor technology wherein a voltage applied to a gate electrode creates an electric field that allows for control of current near the interface of the gate and the body, e.g. to create an inversion channel between the source and drain of a MOSFET - package: the collection of all elements, which are external to the chip, that protect the chip or connect it to another object. Package therefore covers encapsulations, containers, package substrates, interposers, heatsinks or the like. Package does not include objects at a higher system level, like circuit boards and beyond, e.g. a housing in which the circuit board is enclosed. - unipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that primarily involves one type only of charge carrier, i.e. it involves either holes or electrons but not both - bipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that involves multi-carrier-type operation, i.e. which simultaneously uses both electrons and holes as charge carriers - MIS: metal-insulator-semiconductor - MOS: metal-oxide-semiconductor - FET: field-effect transistor - MISFET: metal-insulator-semiconductor field-effect transistor - TFT: thin-film transistor - programming: setting a desired state of a memory cell - writing, erasing: changing the state of a memory cell, in a memory cell wherein programming can occur as many times as desired - core, core region: the portion of a memory cell having storage components, select components, or data lines such as bit lines and word lines. The core also includes devices for local accessing (e.g. reading, writing or erasing) of the storage elements, for example, select transistors of NAND strings or read/write ports of SRAM. - peripheral region, periphery: the portion of a memory device outside the core region having devices or parts for global accessing (e.g. reading, writing, erasing) of the devices of the core region. It includes, e.g. word line drivers, multiplexers or sense amplifiers. - boundary region between the core region and peripheral circuit region: the portion of a memory device that contains neither core devices (e.g. storage components or select components) nor peripheral devices (e.g. word line drivers or multiplexers), typically comprising structural parts such as bit line fan-outs between the core region and the peripheral region, or dummy elements or staircase structures for 3D NAND
[H10B 10/00] Volatile memory devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memory devices having multiple volatile memory cells, wherein, in each cell, the logic state is stored in one of two stable states of a cross-coupled inverter. SRAM cells commonly have four or more transistors.
[H10B 10/10] SRAM devices comprising bipolar components -- H10B 10/10 covers static random-access memory (SRAM) cells that incorporate bipolar transistors or other bipolar components alongside MOS structures. The classification is used for patents describing hybrid SRAM designs that combine bipolar amplification with volatile semiconductor memory functionality.
[H10B 10/12] comprising a MOSFET load element -- The CPC subclass H10B 10/12 relates to volatile semiconductor memory devices that incorporate a MOSFET as the load element, a configuration commonly used in DRAM and SRAM cells to provide the pull-down resistance needed for read and write operations. This arrangement enhances switching speed and power efficiency within the memory circuit.
[H10B 10/125] the MOSFET being a thin film transistor [TFT] -- This CPC subclass covers MOSFETs implemented as thin-film transistors, used in volatile memory applications. It pertains to semiconductor devices where a MOSFET structure is fabricated as a TFT on a substrate, enabling integration in display backplanes and memory arrays.
[H10B 10/15] comprising a resistor load element -- The CPC subclass H10B 10/15 covers volatile electronic memory devices, such as DRAM cells, that incorporate a resistor as the load element to create the necessary voltage levels for storing data. This resistor load is used to define the high-or-low state of each memory cell.
[H10B 10/18] Peripheral circuit regions -- This subclass covers the layout and design of circuit regions that surround the core memory array in volatile semiconductor memory devices, including address decoders, sense amplifiers, control logic and I/O buffers, and relates to the peripheral circuitry that interfaces the memory cells with external signals and power supplies.
[H10B 12/00] Dynamic random access memory [DRAM] devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dynamic memory devices having multiple volatile memory cells, wherein, each cell has:
[H10B 12/01] Manufacture or treatment -- H10B 12/01 relates to the manufacturing and post-fabrication treatment of dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) chips. The subclass covers processes such as wafer preparation, cell formation, doping, dielectric deposition, interconnect formation, packaging and testing of DRAM devices.
[H10B 12/02] for one transistor one-capacitor [1T-1C] memory cells -- The H10B 12/02 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers DRAM devices that use a one-transistor-one-capacitor architecture for each memory cell. This design stores data as charge on a capacitor accessed by a single transistor, providing high density and low power consumption in semiconductor memory technologies.
[H10B 12/03] Making the capacitor or connections thereto -- The entry H10B 12/03 covers processes for forming the storage capacitor and its electrical connections in dynamic RAM chips. It includes techniques such as dielectric layer deposition, electrode patterning, and interconnect formation that integrate the capacitor onto the semiconductor die, ensuring reliable charge storage and access within the memory cell.
[H10B 12/033] the capacitor extending over the transistor -- The H10B 12/033 entry covers DRAM cells in which the storage capacitor is formed over the transistor, creating a stacked or surface-mounted capacitor structure that shares the same silicon area as the transistor. This arrangement reduces cell size and improves density by integrating the capacitor directly above the transistor within the memory array.
[H10B 12/0335] Making a connection between the transistor and the capacitor, e.g. plug -- The H10B 12/0335 entry covers methods for electrically linking a transistor to its storage capacitor in DRAM cells, such as using a plug-in or similar interconnection structure. It addresses the physical arrangement and fabrication steps that create a reliable connection between the two components within the memory array.
[H10B 12/036] the capacitor extending under the transistor -- The CPC entry H10B 12/036 covers DRAM devices in which the storage capacitor extends beneath the transistor. It addresses semiconductor memory designs that integrate the capacitor under the active transistor to increase cell density and improve performance.
[H10B 12/038] the capacitor being in a trench in the substrate -- H10B 12/038 covers DRAM devices whose storage capacitor is formed by etching a trench into the semiconductor substrate, with the capacitor electrode placed within that trench to increase capacitance while saving surface area. This trench-capacitor structure is used to improve cell scalability and performance.
[H10B 12/0383] wherein the transistor is vertical -- The H10B 12/0383 subclass covers dynamic random-access memory cells in which the access transistor is formed in a vertical orientation, standing perpendicular to the substrate plane. This vertical transistor structure enables higher packing density and improved performance in semiconductor memory devices.
[H10B 12/0385] Making a connection between the transistor and the capacitor, e.g. buried strap -- The classification H10B 12/0385 covers methods for electrically linking the access transistor to its storage capacitor in DRAM structures, notably techniques such as buried-strap connections that are formed within the semiconductor substrate to reduce cell size, improve parasitic resistance, and enhance overall memory performance.
[H10B 12/0387] Making the trench -- The classification H10B 12/0387 covers processes for forming trenches in semiconductor structures used in dynamic random-access-memory chips, such as etching or filling narrow grooves that define capacitor plates or isolation regions. It addresses the technical steps and materials required to create these recessed features during DRAM fabrication.
[H10B 12/05] Making the transistor -- The H10B 12/05 subclass covers processes for fabricating the transistor that forms the active element of a DRAM cell, such as doping, lithography, etching and the creation of source, drain and gate structures. It details manufacturing steps that build the charge-storage device itself.
[H10B 12/053] the transistor being at least partially in a trench in the substrate  vertical transistor in combination with a capacitor formed in a substrate trench  H10B12/0383 -- H10B 12/053 pertains to dynamic random access memory (DRAM) devices where the transistor is at least partially integrated into a trench within the substrate, specifically featuring a vertical transistor combined with a capacitor also formed in a substrate trench. This classification highlights advanced DRAM structures designed for enhanced performance and density.
[H10B 12/056] the transistor being a FinFET -- The H10B 12/056 sub-class covers dynamic random-access memory devices that employ FinFET transistors. It specifies DRAM architectures where the active switching element is a fin-shaped field-effect transistor, taking advantage of the three-dimensional structure for reduced leakage, higher drive current and improved scaling.
[H10B 12/09] with simultaneous manufacture of the peripheral circuit region and memory cells -- The H10B 12/09 subclass classifies dynamic random-access-memory (DRAM) devices in which the peripheral circuit region and the memory cells are fabricated together in a single manufacturing step, integrating sense amplifiers, address decoders and other support circuitry simultaneously with the cell array.
[H10B 12/10] DRAM devices comprising bipolar components -- Dynamic random-access memory devices that store data in cells built from bipolar transistors rather than MOSFETs. The classification includes DRAM architectures where the bipolar components serve as the active switching or storage element within the memory array, covering circuit designs, fabrication methods and related bipolar-based cell structures.
[H10B 12/20] DRAM devices comprising floating-body transistors, e.g. floating-body cells -- The classification code H10B 12/20 pertains to Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) devices that utilize floating-body transistors, including those with floating-body cells, which are designed for enhanced performance and efficiency in semiconductor memory applications.
[H10B 12/30] DRAM devices comprising one-transistor - one-capacitor [1T-1C] memory cells -- This subclass covers dynamic random-access memory devices that use a one-transistor-one-capacitor (1T-1C) cell architecture. It includes the design, operation and manufacturing of DRAM chips where each bit is stored in a single capacitor accessed by a single transistor.
[H10B 12/31] having a storage electrode stacked over the transistor -- The H10B 12/31 subclass covers dynamic random-access memory cells in which the storage electrode is formed above the transistor, creating a vertical stack that simplifies layout and improves integration density. It falls under electric semiconductor devices, specifically electronic memory devices within the DRAM family.
[H10B 12/312] with a bit line higher than the capacitor -- H10B 12/312 refers to DRAM devices in which the bit line is situated above the storage capacitor. This vertical arrangement of the bit line relative to the capacitor is used to optimize circuit layout, improve signal integrity, or facilitate manufacturing in semiconductor memory structures.
[H10B 12/315] with the capacitor higher than a bit line -- The CPC class H10B 12/315 covers DRAM devices whose storage capacitor is positioned higher than the bit line, often by stacking the capacitor above the access transistor or using a trench structure. This arrangement allows a more compact cell layout and can improve capacitance without expanding the cell footprint, supporting higher memory densities.
[H10B 12/318] the storage electrode having multiple segments -- H10B 12/318 pertains to DRAM cells in which the storage electrode of the capacitor is formed from several discrete segments rather than a single continuous plate. The segmented electrode can reduce parasitic capacitance, improve charge distribution, and facilitate scaling to smaller feature sizes. This subclass covers designs and manufacturing methods that implement such multi-segment storage electrodes.
[H10B 12/33] the capacitor extending under the transistor -- H10B 12/33 covers DRAM cell designs in which the storage capacitor is formed beneath the transistor, typically using trench or stacked-capacitor structures that integrate the capacitor under the active device to increase capacitance while minimizing cell footprint.
[H10B 12/34] the transistor being at least partially in a trench in the substrate -- The classification code H10B 12/34 pertains to dynamic random access memory (DRAM) devices where the transistor is at least partially embedded in a trench within the semiconductor substrate, enhancing integration density and performance.
[H10B 12/36] the transistor being a FinFET -- H10B 12/36 covers semiconductor memory cells that employ a FinFET transistor as the active element, defining the geometric fin structure, gate wrapping, and related layout features used in dynamic random-access memory. It classifies patents describing the design, fabrication, and operation of FinFET-based memory transistors.
[H10B 12/37] the capacitor being at least partially in a trench in the substrate -- This subclass covers dynamic random access memory cells whose storage capacitor is formed at least partially in a trench etched into the semiconductor substrate. The trench-capacitor arrangement reduces cell area and improves charge storage, enabling higher integration densities in DRAM designs.
[H10B 12/373] the capacitor extending under or around the transistor -- H10B 12/373 covers DRAM cells where the storage capacitor is formed under-or-around the transistor, often using stacked or trench structures to reduce area and enhance performance.
[H10B 12/377] having a storage electrode extension located over the transistor -- The CPC entry H10B 12/377 classifies DRAM memory cells in which the storage-electrode is extended to lie over the access transistor, a layout that increases capacitance while allowing a compact cell geometry. It pertains to semiconductor memory devices that integrate the capacitor electrode directly above the transistor structure.
[H10B 12/39] the capacitor and the transistor being in a same trench -- H10B 12/39 covers DRAM cells where the storage capacitor and the access transistor are formed in a common trench of the substrate, creating a compact three-dimensional structure. The trench contains both the transistor channel and a dielectric-filled section that serves as the capacitor, enabling higher memory density.
[H10B 12/395] the transistor being vertical -- H10B 12/395 classifies DRAM devices that use a vertical transistor architecture, where the transistor channel is oriented perpendicular to the substrate, allowing higher cell density and a smaller footprint.
[H10B 12/48] Data lines or contacts therefor -- H10B 12/48 pertains to the design and configuration of data lines or contacts that connect dynamic random-access memory cells to their surrounding circuitry, covering routing, spacing and interface structures used to transmit read-write signals within DRAM devices.
[H10B 12/482] Bit lines -- The H10B 12/482 class covers the conductive pathways that link individual DRAM memory cells to peripheral circuitry, enabling read, write and refresh signals to travel across the array. These bit lines transmit data between the cell matrix and sense amplifiers or word-line drivers.
[H10B 12/485] Bit line contacts -- Bit line contacts are the conductive structures that connect a DRAM cell's bit lines to peripheral circuitry, enabling charge sensing and data transfer during read and write operations. The CPC entry H10B 12/485 covers designs, materials and manufacturing methods for these contacts.
[H10B 12/488] Word lines -- Word lines are the conductive tracks within a DRAM array that transmit data bits from each memory cell to the peripheral circuitry during read and write operations. They form part of the addressing structure, linking rows of cells to sense amplifiers and enabling selective access to stored information. This element is classified under H10B 12/488.
[H10B 12/50] Peripheral circuit region structures -- H10B 12/50 classifies the layout and design of circuit regions that lie outside the core memory array of DRAM chips, such as sense amplifiers, address drivers, and I/O buffers. It covers the geometric arrangement, interconnections and supporting structures that enable signal access and control for the memory device.
[H10B 20/00] Non-volatile memory devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memory devices wherein data is stored even when power is removed (non-volatile), wherein the cells have components (e.g. fuses or anti-fuses) that are irreversibly changed during programming.  The group H10B20/00 itself covers memory devices wherein, after manufacturing, the operations are limited to read-only (e.g. mask ROM), because the programming thereof was performed during manufacturing. The groups H10B20/20 - H10B20/25 cover programmable ROM [PROM] wherein the memory may be programmed after manufacturing by a user.  ROM and PROM devices are typically programmable only once (though in some situations, a cell may have more than one element that may be irreversibly changed, so a very limited amount of "corrective" programming may occur). This is contrasted with EPROM and EEPROM devices, which can be writable or erasable many (e.g. millions of) times.
[H10B 20/10] ROM devices comprising bipolar components -- H10B 20/10 designates read-only memory devices whose storage cells are built from bipolar semiconductor components, placing them in the non-volatile memory sub-category of electronic memory within the broader fields of semiconductor and electrical technology.
[H10B 20/20] Programmable ROM [PROM] devices comprising field-effect components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile ROM devices comprising multiple memory cells that can be programmed after their manufacture ("programmable ROM" or "PROM"), wherein the memory cell comprises field-effect components as either the access component or the storage component.  The group H10B20/20 itself covers PROM wherein individual memory cells may each be programmed more than once, e.g. a "corrective programming" may occur because the cells have more than one element that may be irreversibly-changeable element. The groups indented under H10B20/20 cover PROM wherein each cell may only be programmed once.  Illustrative example for this group: a cell is constructed from an anti-fuse programming transistor M0, an electronic fuse EF, and a control transistor MN1, such that the normal programming of M0 by breaking down its gate-source insulation layer (which decreases its initial large resistance) can be followed by "correction programming" by fusing the effuse (increasing the resistance).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - ROM devices comprising bipolar components -> H10B20/10
[H10B 20/25] One-time programmable ROM [OTPROM] devices, e.g. using electrically-fusible links -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile programmable ROM (PROM) devices comprising multiple memory cells wherein, after their manufacture, each cell can only be programmed once (e.g. by permanently connecting or disconnecting a fuse or an anti-fuse).
[H10B 20/27] ROM only -- H10B 20/27 identifies ROM-only devices within non-volatile electronic memory, a sub-class of semiconductor devices for memory under the electricity field. It covers read-only memory circuits that store data permanently and are not reprogrammable.
[H10B 20/30] having the source region and the drain region on the same level, e.g. lateral transistors -- This CPC subclass covers semiconductor non-volatile memory cells in which the source and drain regions are coplanar, forming a lateral transistor geometry. Such structures are used to implement memory functions where charge storage occurs without vertical stacking of the active regions.
[H10B 20/34] Source electrode or drain electrode programmed -- The entry H10B 20/34 covers non-volatile semiconductor memory cells in which the source or drain electrode itself is used as the programmable element, allowing data to be stored by modifying the electrical state of that electrode. It includes methods such as charge trapping, phase-change or other mechanisms that program the source or drain to represent stored bits.
[H10B 20/36] Gate programmed, e.g. different gate material or no gate -- H10B 20/36 covers non-volatile memory cells whose operation is set by the gate structure itself, such as using a different gate material or eliminating the gate altogether. The classification falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → electronic memory devices → non-volatile memory devices. It pertains to gate-programmed technologies that define the data state through the physical gate composition.
[H10B 20/363] Gate conductor programmed -- The H10B 20/363 class covers gate conductors in non-volatile memory devices that are deliberately programmed to store charge or state information, such as floating-gate or charge-trap structures. It addresses methods and circuit designs where the gate electrode itself functions as the programmed storage element in semiconductor memory technologies.
[H10B 20/367] Gate dielectric programmed, e.g. different thickness -- The CPC class H10B 20/367 covers semiconductor memory devices whose gate dielectric is deliberately programmed, for example by using regions of different thickness. This relates to non-volatile memories such as flash, where the dielectric variation controls charge storage and threshold voltage. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → electronic memory devices → non-volatile memory devices.
[H10B 20/38] Doping programmed, e.g. mask ROM -- This CPC subclass covers non-volatile memory cells that are programmed by permanently altering the semiconductor doping pattern, such as mask ROM structures where the logical data are defined during fabrication by doped regions. It pertains to electronic memory devices in the hierarchy electricity → semiconductor devices → electronic memory devices → non-volatile memory devices.
[H10B 20/383] Channel doping programmed -- H10B 20/383 relates to methods of intentionally introducing dopants into the channel region of non-volatile memory cells to set their electrical characteristics. It covers programmed doping processes such as ion implantation, diffusion or laser-induced doping that define threshold voltage or charge-storage behavior in devices like flash or EEPROM.
[H10B 20/387] Source region or drain region doping programmed -- The subclass covers semiconductor memory cells whose source or drain regions are doped in a programmed manner to set or modify their electrical characteristics, enabling data storage in non-volatile devices. It includes techniques for adjusting impurity concentrations to define threshold voltages or conductance states of the memory cell.
[H10B 20/40] having the source region and drain region on different levels, e.g. vertical channel -- H10B 20/40 covers non-volatile memory devices in which the source and drain regions are formed on separate vertical levels, creating a vertical channel structure. This arrangement enables stacked or three-dimensional architectures that improve density and performance compared with planar layouts.
[H10B 20/50] having transistors on different levels, e.g. 3D ROM -- H10B 20/50 pertains to non-volatile memory devices that incorporate transistors on multiple vertical levels, enabling three-dimensional structures such as 3D ROM. This arrangement allows stacked arrays of memory cells to increase density while maintaining the ability to retain data without power. It falls under the CPC hierarchy ELECTRICITY → SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES → ELECTRONIC MEMORY DEVICES.
[H10B 20/60] Peripheral circuit regions -- H10B 20/60 classifies the design and arrangement of peripheral circuit regions in non-volatile memory devices, including the layout of drivers, sense amplifiers, control and address circuitry that lie outside the memory cell array. It is used for patents covering the architecture and interconnections of these supporting circuits in semiconductor memory chips.
[H10B 20/65] of memory structures of the ROM only type -- H10B 20/65 covers semiconductor memory structures that provide only read-only functionality, i.e., ROM-type devices where data are permanently programmed and cannot be altered by the user. It includes designs of mask-programmed, fuse, antifuse and other non-volatile ROM architectures and related manufacturing methods.
[H10B 41/00] Electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM [EEPROM] devices comprising floating gates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is stored as charge on a floating gate of a transistor. The memory cell can be electrically erased and reprogrammed.
[H10B 41/10] characterised by the top-view layout -- H10B 41/10 covers electrically erasable and programmable ROM devices that use floating-gate cells and are defined by the arrangement of those cells as seen from a top-view layout, such as the geometric pattern of the memory array, routing and bonding pads. This subclass distinguishes designs based on how the cell architecture is organized in the plane of the chip rather than by circuit function or process steps.
[H10B 41/20] characterised by three-dimensional [3D] arrangements, e.g. with cells on different height levels -- H10B 41/20 covers electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM devices with floating-gate structures that employ three-dimensional architectures, such as stacking memory cells on different height levels, to increase storage density within the semiconductor-device class.
[H10B 41/23] with source and drain on different levels, e.g. with sloping channels -- This CPC subclass H10B 41/23 covers EEPROM devices with floating-gate transistors whose source and drain are placed on different vertical levels, for example using sloping channel geometries to connect them. The design enables three-dimensional integration of the MOSFET channel within the memory cell.
[H10B 41/27] the channels comprising vertical portions, e.g. U-shaped channels -- This subclass covers structures in floating-gate EEPROMs where the conductive channel includes a vertical segment, such as a U-shaped or inverted-U configuration, used to control charge flow and improve scaling. It pertains to the geometry of the channel within the memory cell.
[H10B 41/30] characterised by the memory core region -- H10B 41/30 classifies electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM devices that use floating-gate structures and are defined by particular features of the memory core region, such as the layout, material composition or geometry of the floating-gate area that stores charge. This subclass falls under electronic memory devices within semiconductor technology.
[H10B 41/35] with a cell select transistor, e.g. NAND -- The CPC subclass H10B 41/35 refers to electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM devices that incorporate a cell-select transistor, as found in NAND-type memory arrays where each floating-gate cell is accessed through its own select transistor. It sits under electricity, semiconductor devices, electronic memory devices, and EEPROM devices comprising floating gates.
[H10B 41/40] characterised by the peripheral circuit region -- H10B 41/40 classifies floating-gate EEPROM devices whose design is defined by a peripheral circuit region that houses address decoding, control logic and read/write circuitry separate from the memory array. The classification highlights the arrangement of supporting circuitry around the array rather than within it.
[H10B 41/41] of a memory region comprising a cell select transistor, e.g. NAND -- H10B 41/41 covers electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM devices that use floating-gate structures and define a memory region that includes a cell-select transistor, typically arranged in a NAND architecture. This subclass refines the broader group for semiconductor memory devices by specifying the integrated transistor used to address individual cells in the array.
[H10B 41/42] Simultaneous manufacture of periphery and memory cells -- H10B 41/42 refers to processes that produce the peripheral circuitry and the floating-gate EEPROM memory cells at the same time, integrating both parts in a single manufacturing step to improve device compactness and lower production cost. This subclass falls under Electricity → Semiconductor Devices → Electronic Memory Devices → Electrically Erasable-and-Programmable ROM devices with floating gates.
[H10B 41/43] comprising only one type of peripheral transistor -- This CPC subclass covers EEPROM devices that rely on floating-gate structures and use only one kind of peripheral transistor for read and write operations. It applies to semiconductor memory chips where the peripheral circuitry is homogeneous, simplifying design and fabrication of electrically erasable and programmable read-only memory.
[H10B 41/44] with a control gate layer also being used as part of the peripheral transistor -- The classification code H10B 41/44 pertains to EEPROM devices that feature a control gate layer serving a dual role, integrating functionality for both memory operation and peripheral transistors. This design optimizes space and performance in semiconductor memory devices.
[H10B 41/46] with an inter-gate dielectric layer also being used as part of the peripheral transistor -- H10B 41/46 covers electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM cells that use the inter-gate dielectric not only to isolate the floating gate but also as a functional part of the surrounding peripheral transistor. By sharing this dielectric layer, the design simplifies fabrication, reduces area, and improves integration of the memory array with its control circuitry.
[H10B 41/47] with a floating-gate layer also being used as part of the peripheral transistor -- This subclass covers electrically erasable programmable read-only memory cells where the floating-gate structure that stores charge is also integrated into the surrounding transistor circuitry, serving as part of the peripheral transistor. It applies to EEPROM devices that share the floating-gate layer between the memory cell and its driver elements.
[H10B 41/48] with a tunnel dielectric layer also being used as part of the peripheral transistor -- This subclass concerns floating-gate EEPROM cells in which the tunnel dielectric that forms the insulating barrier for charge storage is also used as the gate dielectric of the peripheral transistor, allowing the same insulating layer to serve both the memory element and the adjacent control transistor.
[H10B 41/49] comprising different types of peripheral transistor -- H10B 41/49 covers EEPROM memory devices that use floating-gate cells and include different types of peripheral transistors, such as varying conductivity types or threshold characteristics, to manage the read, write or erase functions.
[H10B 41/50] characterised by the boundary region between the core region and the peripheral circuit region -- H10B 41/50 covers EEPROM devices with a floating-gate memory cell where the defining feature is the boundary region separating the core storage area from the peripheral circuitry. The classification pertains to semiconductor memory structures where this interface is specially designed or characterized.
[H10B 41/60] the control gate being a doped region, e.g. single-poly memory cell -- H10B 41/60 covers EEPROM memory cells in which the control gate is formed as a doped semiconductor region rather than a polysilicon layer, as in single-poly floating-gate structures. The classification applies to devices that use a floating gate for charge storage and employ a doped control gate to program and erase data electrically.
[H10B 41/70] the floating gate being an electrode shared by two or more components -- The subclass concerns electrically erasable-and-programmable ROM devices in which a single floating-gate electrode is used by two or more components, such as transistors or other circuit elements, to share charge storage for programming and erasing. It defines the structural arrangement where the floating gate serves as a common electrode across multiple parts of the memory array.
[H10B 43/00] EEPROM devices comprising charge-trapping gate insulators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is stored as charge on a charge-trapping gate dielectric. The memory cell can be electrically erased and reprogrammed.
[H10B 43/10] characterised by the top-view layout -- The H10B 43/10 subclass classifies EEPROM devices that use charge-trapping gate insulators and are defined primarily by their top-view layout of memory cells and interconnections. It focuses on the planar arrangement of cell arrays as seen from above, distinguishing designs based on the geometric pattern of rows, columns and access circuitry.
[H10B 43/20] characterised by three-dimensional [3D] arrangements, e.g. with cells on different height levels -- H10B 43/20 covers EEPROM memory structures where the charge-trapping gate insulators are arranged in a three-dimensional layout, such as stacked or vertically displaced cells, permitting memory cells to occupy multiple height levels within the same device.
[H10B 43/23] with source and drain on different levels, e.g. with sloping channels -- The subclass H10B 43/23 covers EEPROM devices that use charge-trapping gate insulators and have the source and drain formed on different vertical levels, such as through sloping channel designs, to achieve specific electrical or manufacturing advantages.
[H10B 43/27] the channels comprising vertical portions, e.g. U-shaped channels -- The CPC entry H10B 43/27 covers vertical, often U-shaped, channel structures used in EEPROM devices that incorporate charge-trapping gate insulators. These channels form the conductive path in three-dimensional memory cells, enabling charge storage and retrieval via the trapped charges in the surrounding dielectric.
[H10B 43/30] characterised by the memory core region -- H10B 43/30 refers to EEPROM devices that employ charge-trapping gate insulators and are distinguished by features of the memory core region, such as its layout, materials or geometry. The subclass focuses on how the core area stores and retains charge within semiconductor memory structures.
[H10B 43/35] with cell select transistors, e.g. NAND -- The H10B 43/35 class covers EEPROM memory structures that employ charge-trapping gate insulators and incorporate cell-select transistors, such as those used in NAND-type arrays. It pertains to semiconductor devices where the select transistors enable addressing individual memory cells within the array.
[H10B 43/40] characterised by the peripheral circuit region -- H10B 43/40 covers electrically programmable read-only memory (EEPROM) devices that employ charge-trapping gate insulators and are distinguished by having the peripheral circuit placed in a separate region surrounding the memory array.
[H10B 43/50] characterised by the boundary region between the core and peripheral circuit regions -- A subclass of EEPROMs that employ charge-trapping gate insulators and are defined by the design of the boundary region separating the memory core from the peripheral circuit areas, where the interface properties influence coupling, leakage and access performance.
[H10B 51/00] Ferroelectric RAM [FeRAM] devices comprising ferroelectric memory transistors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is stored as a polarisation state of a ferroelectric material within a transistor, e.g. in a ferroelectric gate dielectric. The memory cell can be electrically erased and reprogrammed.
[H10B 51/10] characterised by the top-view layout -- H10B 51/10 classifies ferroelectric RAM structures where the device layout is defined by a top-view perspective, indicating the planar arrangement of memory cells, transistors and interconnections as seen from above. The classification is used for patents describing layout-specific features of FeRAM with ferroelectric transistors.
[H10B 51/20] characterised by the three-dimensional [3D] arrangements, e.g. with cells on different height levels -- This CPC subclass covers ferroelectric RAM devices whose memory cells are arranged in a three-dimensional structure, such as stacked or vertically offset cells that occupy different height levels within the chip. It resides under electricity, semiconductor devices and electronic memory devices.
[H10B 51/30] characterised by the memory core region -- This subclass covers ferroelectric RAM devices that employ ferroelectric memory transistors and are defined by the design of the memory core region where the ferroelectric material stores data, including any specific configurations or modifications of that core region to achieve the storage function.
[H10B 51/40] characterised by the peripheral circuit region -- This CPC subgroup covers ferroelectric RAM devices that include ferroelectric memory transistors and are defined by features of the peripheral circuit region, such as the arrangement of drivers, sense amplifiers, timing or control circuitry that lie outside the cell array.
[H10B 51/50] characterised by the boundary region between the core and peripheral circuit regions -- The H10B 51/50 subclass covers ferroelectric RAM devices in which the interface between the memory core and the surrounding peripheral circuitry is specially defined or engineered, affecting how signals and power are transferred across that boundary.
[H10B 53/00] Ferroelectric RAM [FeRAM] devices comprising ferroelectric memory capacitors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is stored as a polarisation state of a ferroelectric material within a capacitor, e.g. in a ferroelectric capacitor dielectric. The cell is accessed by a selection component and can be electrically erased and reprogrammed.
[H10B 53/10] characterised by the top-view layout -- H10B 53/10 covers ferroelectric RAM (FeRAM) devices that use ferroelectric memory capacitors and are defined by their top-view layout, i.e., the arrangement of cells and interconnects as seen from above, within the CPC subgroup for electronic memory devices.
[H10B 53/20] characterised by the three-dimensional [3D] arrangements, e.g. with cells on different height levels -- The H10B 53/20 subclass refers to ferroelectric RAM devices whose memory cells are organized in a three-dimensional architecture, for example by stacking cells at different height levels to increase density. This arrangement is a specific feature of FeRAM within the broader hierarchy of electronic memory devices.
[H10B 53/30] characterised by the memory core region -- This CPC subclass H10B 53/30 relates to ferroelectric RAM (FeRAM) devices and is defined by the characteristics of the memory core region, describing the structure and arrangement of ferroelectric memory capacitors within that core. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → electronic memory devices.
[H10B 53/40] characterised by the peripheral circuit region -- This subclass covers ferroelectric RAM devices where the defining feature is the design or arrangement of the peripheral circuit region that interfaces the ferroelectric memory capacitors with external logic or power. It includes structures, interconnections and circuit layouts surrounding the memory cell array that are essential to the device's operation.
[H10B 53/50] characterised by the boundary region between the core and peripheral circuit regions -- The H10B 53/50 subclass covers ferroelectric RAM (FeRAM) devices that are defined by the characteristics of the boundary region separating the ferroelectric memory core from the peripheral circuit areas. It includes designs where this interface region influences signal routing, isolation, or integration of the memory cells with read-write circuitry. This classification is used for patents describing structural or functional aspects of that core-periphery boundary in FeRAMs.
[H10B 61/00] Magnetic memory devices, e.g. magnetoresistive RAM [MRAM] devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is stored in magnetic domains of magnetic layers in a storage component. The memory cell can be electrically erased and reprogrammed. Examples include MTJ-based memory and STT-MRAM.
[H10B 61/10] comprising components having two electrodes, e.g. diodes or MIM elements -- H10B 61/10 covers magnetic memory devices that incorporate components with two electrodes, such as diodes or metal-insulator-metal structures. These elements are used to read or write magnetic states in MRAM cells.
[H10B 61/20] comprising components having three or more electrodes, e.g. transistors -- H10B 61/20 covers magnetic memory devices, such as MRAM, that incorporate components with three or more electrodes, for example transistors where separate electrodes serve write, read, and control functions, enabling distinct pathways for magnetoresistive operation.
[H10B 61/22] of the field-effect transistor [FET] type
[H10B 63/00] Resistance change memory devices, e.g. resistive RAM [ReRAM] devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is stored as a high or low resistance state. The memory cell can be electrically erased and reprogrammed. Examples include:
[H10B 63/10] Phase change RAM [PCRAM, PRAM] devices -- Phase-change RAM (PCRAM or PRAM) devices store data by switching a material between amorphous and crystalline states, each having distinct electrical resistance. The rapid, reversible phase transition is induced by heating with electrical pulses, allowing non-volatile memory operation with fast read/write speeds and high endurance. This technology falls under CPC H10B 63/10 for resistance-change memory devices.
[H10B 63/20] comprising selection components having two electrodes, e.g. diodes -- The code H10B 63/20 covers resistive memory cells that incorporate a two-terminal selection element, typically a diode, to control current flow during operation. It applies to ReRAM structures where the memory element is paired with such a selection component to improve switching reliability and prevent sneak-path currents.
[H10B 63/22] of the metal-insulator-metal type -- This subclass covers metal-insulator-metal (MIM) resistive-change memory devices, commonly called ReRAM, where a thin insulating layer is sandwiched between two metal electrodes and data are stored by altering the device's resistance. The classification includes structures that switch resistance through filament formation, interface modulation or other mechanisms, enabling non-volatile memory operation.
[H10B 63/24] of the Ovonic threshold switching type -- H10B 63/24 designates resistance-change memory devices that operate by the Ovonic threshold-switching mechanism, a type of ReRAM where a sudden increase in conductivity occurs once a voltage threshold is exceeded, enabling data storage through distinct high- and low-resistance states. This subclass falls under electronic memory devices within semiconductor devices and the broader electricity field.
[H10B 63/30] comprising selection components having three or more electrodes, e.g. transistors -- The subclass H10B 63/30 covers resistance-change memory devices, such as resistive RAM, that incorporate a selection component - for example a transistor - with three or more electrodes to enable precise addressing of individual memory cells.
[H10B 63/32] of the bipolar type -- The H10B 63/32 subclass covers bipolar-type resistive-RAM (ReRAM) devices, a form of resistance-change electronic memory in which the memory state is programmed by applying forward and reverse bias voltages across a semiconductor material to switch its resistance. This falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → electronic memory devices → resistance-change memory.
[H10B 63/34] of the vertical channel field-effect transistor type -- H10B 63/34 pertains to resistive memory devices that employ a vertical-channel field-effect transistor structure, where the channel is oriented perpendicular to the substrate and the transistor controls the resistance state of the memory cell. The vertical geometry is used to improve cell density and switching speed in ReRAM applications.
[H10B 63/80] Arrangements comprising multiple bistable or multi-stable switching components of the same type on a plane parallel to the substrate, e.g. cross-point arrays -- This CPC subclass H10B 63/80 addresses layouts where many identical resistive-memory cells are arranged in a plane parallel to the substrate, such as cross-point arrays, enabling each cell to toggle between two or more stable resistance states. It concerns the planar organization of bistable or multi-stable switching components of the same type.
[H10B 63/82] the switching components having a common active material layer -- The entry covers semiconductor memory devices that use resistive switching, where the switching elements share a common active material layer such as a metal-oxide or other thin film, enabling resistive RAM operation. It relates to the structure and fabrication of that shared active layer.
[H10B 63/84] arranged in a direction perpendicular to the substrate, e.g. 3D cell arrays -- This subclass covers resistive memory structures where the individual memory cells are stacked or oriented vertically relative to the wafer, forming three-dimensional arrays such as vertical ReRAM cells. It addresses configurations that exploit a perpendicular orientation to increase density and enable 3D integration.
[H10B 63/845] the switching components being connected to a common vertical conductor -- H10B 63/845 covers resistive-change memory cells in which the individual switching elements are all tied to a shared vertical conductor, typically forming a cross-point array where the common line supplies the write and read currents to each device. The classification applies to ReRAM structures that use this vertical interconnect scheme to reduce cell size and simplify routing.
[H10B 69/00] Erasable-and-programmable ROM [EPROM] devices not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-volatile memory devices having multiple memory cells, wherein in each cell, the logic state is erasable and reprogrammable, other than those covered by H10B41/00 - H10B63/00 . Examples include:
[H10B 80/00] Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one memory device covered by this subclass -- Group H10B80/00 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10W90/00 . Groups H10W90/00 and H10B80/00 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10B 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- H10B 99/00 designates semiconductor electronic memory devices whose features are not covered by any of the other more specific groups within the subclass, encompassing miscellaneous or novel memory-related inventions that fall under the broader category of electricity-semiconductor devices.
[H10B 99/10] Memory cells having a cross-point geometry -- Memory cells arranged in a cross-point geometry use intersecting conductive lines to form a matrix of selectable storage locations, each point acting as a memory element. The structure enables high-density integration by addressing cells at the line intersections, often employed in resistive, phase-change, or other non-volatile memory technologies. This classification covers such cross-point memory cell designs.
[H10B 99/14] comprising memory cells that only have passive resistors or passive capacitors -- The CPC subclass H10B 99/14 covers electronic memory devices that consist solely of passive components such as resistors or capacitors, without any active elements like transistors. It applies to memory cells that store information using charge or resistance states in purely passive structures.
[H10B 99/16] comprising memory cells having diodes -- H10B 99/16 refers to electronic memory devices in which the individual memory cells incorporate diodes, a configuration used in semiconductor memory structures to provide isolation or read-out functions for each stored bit.
[H10B 99/20] comprising memory cells having thyristors -- H10B 99/20 includes electronic memory devices in which each storage element is a thyristor, a four-layer semiconductor switch used to retain a binary state. The classification captures memory arrays that rely on thyristor-based cells for non-volatile or programmable storage, without further subdivision into more specific functional groups.
[H10B 99/22] including field-effect components -- H10B 99/22 covers semiconductor devices for electronic memory that are not otherwise classified, specifically field-effect components such as MOSFETs, JFETs, and related structures used as switching or storage elements. The entry is placed in the CPC subclass for semiconductor devices and electronic memory devices and captures designs that fall outside the more detailed groups of the subclass.
[H10D] INORGANIC ELECTRIC SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric semiconductor devices having inorganic semiconductor bodies.  This includes the following kinds of devices:  This place also covers:  In this subclass, the periodic system used is the I to VIII Group system indicated in the Periodic Table under Note (3) of section C .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electronic memory devices -> H10B - Semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared radiation, light, electromagnetic radiation of shorter wavelength or corpuscular radiation -> H10F - Light-emitting semiconductor devices having potential barriers -> H10H - Thermoelectric, thermomagnetic, piezoelectric, electrostrictive, magnetostrictive, magnetic-effect, superconducting, Ovshinsky-effect, bulk negative resistance effect devices -> H10N - Constructional details other than semiconductor bodies or electrodes thereof -> H10W99/00  **Glossary:** - chip: a piece of a wafer or a substrate that has been processed to contain devices therein or thereon.The expression "diced chip" refers to the result of dicing a wafer or a substrate into a plurality of chips, whereas "undiced chip" refers to a chip before dicing or with no dicing. - device: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.); (depending on the context) can also refer to an integrated device (e.g. CMOS-IC, DRAM device, etc.). A device may be in the form of a bare or packaged chip. - dopant: the atoms or compounds added to a material during doping - doping: the intentional addition of a small quantity of atoms or compounds into a material to achieve a desired characteristic, e.g. to produce an n-type or p-type material - individual: refers to: an electric circuit element not being an integrated device; or a component of an integrated device. Examples of individual devices include: diodes, transistors, photovoltaic cells, Josephson-junction devices, light-emitting diodes [LED], organic LEDs or a single LED component within an integrated device. - integrated device: a device consisting of a plurality of semiconductor or other solid-state electric circuit elements formed in or on a common substrate - integrated circuit: an integrated device where all the electric circuit elements (e.g. diodes, transistors, LEDs, etc.) are formed in or on a common substrate, including interconnections between the elements - component: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.) that is one of a plurality of elements formed in or on a common substrate, e.g. in an integrated device - wafer: it can be one of the following: (a) a slice of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material. For example: a slice of silicon; a slice of a semiconducting compound, e.g. gallium nitride [GaN]; a slice of lithium tantalate [LiTaO 3 ] for superconductor applications. (b) A multilayered laminate, having at least one layer of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material, the layer being meant to be processed into devices. For example: silicon-on-insulator [SOI]; silicon-on-glass [SOG]; silicon-on-sapphire [SOS]; a composite wafer comprising silicon carbide [SiC] on polycrystalline silicon [Si] support; a layer of semiconducting nanowires on glass. A wafer is typically processed by (e.g.) deposition, etching, doping or diffusion, and is then typically diced into chips. - body: the region of semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) within which, or at the surface of which, the physical effects that are characteristic of the device occur, and any bordering semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) that are contiguous with this region. Examples: in a field-effect transistor [FET], the physical effects occur in the channel region between the source and the drain. The semiconductor body includes the channel region, the source and drain regions, and any contiguous semiconductor material; in a light-emitting diode [LED], the physical effects occur at a junction of active semiconductor layers. The semiconductor body includes these active semiconductor layers and any contiguous semiconductor layers, such as buffer layers, possibly a growth substrate, etc., that are between the cathode and anode electrodes; in a thermoelectric device, the solid-state body includes all solid-state materials in the path of current between the electrodes. - electrode: a conductive region in or on the semiconductor body or solid-state body of a device (and other than the body itself) which exerts an electrical influence on the body, irrespective of whether or not an external electrical connection is made thereto. The term covers metallic regions which exert electrical influence on the body through an insulating region (e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling), or inductive coupling arrangements. In a capacitive coupling arrangement, the dielectric region is regarded as part of the electrode. The overall conductive wiring may comprise multiple portions. In such a case, only the wiring portions that exert an electrical influence on the body are considered portions of the electrode. Examples: conductive layer(s) in direct physical contact with the body; conductive region(s) exerting an inductive coupling onto the body; a multilayer structure which exerts influence on the body through an insulating region, e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling. - interconnection: a conductive arrangement for conducting electric current from an electrode of a circuit element to another part of the circuit. Examples include metal wirings. - container: a solid construction in which (one or more) devices are placed, or which is formed around the devices, for forming packaged devices. A container requires a partial or total enclosure and it may also comprise a filling. - encapsulation: an enclosure consisting of (one or more) layers, e.g. comprising organic polymers, which at least partially enclose the (one or more) devices, thereby protecting them. An encapsulation is often used to hermetically seal devices. - field-effect: refers to semiconductor technology wherein a voltage applied to a gate electrode creates an electric field that allows for control of current near the interface of the gate and the body, e.g. to create an inversion channel between the source and drain of a MOSFET - package: the collection of all elements, which are external to the chip, that protect the chip or connect it to another object. Package therefore covers encapsulations, containers, package substrates, interposers, heatsinks or the like. Package does not include objects at a higher system level, like circuit boards and beyond, e.g. a housing in which the circuit board is enclosed. - unipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that primarily involves one type only of charge carrier, i.e. it involves either holes or electrons but not both - bipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that involves multi-carrier-type operation, i.e. which simultaneously uses both electrons and holes as charge carriers - MIS: metal-insulator-semiconductor - MOS: metal-oxide-semiconductor - FET: field-effect transistor - MISFET: metal-insulator-semiconductor field-effect transistor - TFT: thin-film transistor - thyristor: device having a control electrode and having regenerative action within four or more alternating P-type and N-type regions - Group IV material: material comprising only Group IV elements, except for dopants or other impurities - Group III-V material: material comprising only Group III and Group V elements, except for dopants or other impurities - Group II-VI material: material comprising only Group II and Group VI elements, except for dopants or other impurities - Group I-VI material: material comprising only comprising Group I or Group VI elements, except for dopants or other impurities - Group I-VII material: material comprising only comprising Group I or Group VII elements, except for dopants or other impurities
[H10D 1/00] Individual devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Individual inorganic resistors or capacitors having potential barriers.  Individual resistors, capacitors or inductors having no potential barriers, and specially adapted for integration with other semiconductor components.
[H10D 10/00] Bipolar junction transistors [BJT] -- In this group, when the manufacture or treatment of a device is determined to be novel and non-obvious, the device itself is also classified.
[H10D 10/01] Manufacture or treatment -- Group H10D10/01 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D48/031 . Group H10D10/01 is also impacted by reclassification into groups H10D10/051 - H10D10/058 . Groups H10D48/031 , H10D10/01 and H10D10/051 - H10D10/058 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/021] of heterojunction BJTs [HBT] -- Group H10D10/021 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D48/031 . Groups H10D48/031 and H10D10/021 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/031] of Schottky BJTs -- Group H10D10/031 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D48/031 . Groups H10D48/031 and H10D10/031 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/041] of thin-film BJTs   of heterojunction BJTs  H10D10/021 -- Group H10D10/041 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D48/031 . Groups H10D48/031 and H10D10/041 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/051] of vertical BJTs  of heterojunction BJTs  H10D10/021 ; of Schottky BJTs  H10D10/031 ; of thin film BJTs  H10D10/041 -- Groups H10D10/051 , H10D10/056 and H10D10/058 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D10/01 and H10D48/031 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/052] of inverted vertical BJTs -- Group H10D10/052 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D10/01 and H10D48/031 . Group H10D10/052 is also impacted by reclassification into group H10D10/054 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/054] Forming extrinsic base regions on silicon substrate after insulating device isolation in vertical BJTs having single crystalline emitter, collector or base regions -- Group H10D10/054 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D10/01 , H10D10/052 and H10D48/031 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/056] of vertical BJTs having the main current going through the whole substrate, e.g. power BJTs -- This subclass covers vertical bipolar junction transistors where the main current flows through the entire semiconductor substrate, such as power transistors used for high-current applications. It is part of the inorganic electric semiconductor devices group in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 10/058] having multi-emitter structures, e.g. interdigitated, multi-cellular or distributed emitters -- H10D 10/058 belongs to the CPC subgroup for inorganic electric semiconductor devices and specifically addresses bipolar junction transistors that incorporate multi-emitter structures, such as interdigitated, multi-cellular or distributed emitters. These configurations provide several emission regions within a single device to increase drive current or enable multiple output connections.
[H10D 10/061] of lateral BJTs   of heterojunction BJTs  H10D10/021 ; of thin film BJTs  H10D10/041 -- Group H10D10/061 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D48/031 . Groups H10D48/031 and H10D10/061 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 1/01] Manufacture or treatment -- Group H10D1/01 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D8/051 . Groups H10D8/051 and H10D1/01 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 1/021] of resistors having no potential barriers -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor resistors that operate without any internal potential barrier, such as bulk-type resistors made from doped silicon, germanium or other compounds where conduction occurs by free carriers rather than a barrier junction. It includes devices where the resistance is defined by the material's intrinsic properties rather than by a p-n junction or other barrier structure.
[H10D 10/211] Point-contact BJTs -- Point-contact bipolar junction transistors are a type of BJT where the emitter is a tiny metallic point that contacts the base region of an inorganic semiconductor, forming a three-layer p-n-p or n-p-n structure. This geometry creates a localized junction that facilitates carrier injection and amplification, and it is used mainly in early high-frequency and microwave applications.
[H10D 10/221] Schottky barrier BJTs -- The H10D 10/221 subclass covers bipolar junction transistors that incorporate a Schottky barrier at one or more of their junctions to reduce storage time and improve switching speed. These Schottky barrier BJTs are used in high-frequency and fast-switching circuits, combining the amplification of BJTs with the low-voltage, low-loss characteristics of a Schottky diode.
[H10D 10/231] Tunnel BJTs -- H10D 10/231, Tunnel BJTs, concerns bipolar junction transistors that operate by quantum tunneling of charge carriers, enabling very fast switching and low-voltage operation in semiconductor circuits. The entry belongs to the inorganic electric semiconductor devices subclass within the electricity field of the CPC system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[H10D 10/241] Avalanche BJTs -- H10D 10/241 covers bipolar junction transistors designed to operate in avalanche mode, where the collector-base junction is intentionally reverse-biased beyond breakdown to achieve high current gain or switching speed. These devices exploit the controlled avalanche multiplication of carriers and are used in applications such as pulse generators, protection circuits, and high-frequency amplifiers.
[H10D 1/025] of resistors having potential barriers -- Group H10D1/025 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D8/051 and H10D48/021 . Groups H10D8/051 , H10D48/021 and H10D1/025 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/311] Thin-film BJTs -- Thin-film bipolar junction transistors are BJT devices in which the semiconductor layers that form the emitter, base and collector are deposited as thin films on a substrate rather than being formed from bulk crystal. This structure allows for fine geometry control, integration with other thin-film components and often improved high-frequency performance, making them suitable for compact, planar electronic circuits.
[H10D 10/40] Vertical BJTs -- The classification code H10D 10/40 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, specifically addresses vertical bipolar junction transistors (BJTs), detailing their structure and fabrication processes within the broader category of inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 1/041] of capacitors having no potential barriers -- H10D 1/041 classifies capacitors that store charge without internal potential-barrier junctions, such as double-layer or electrochemical capacitors where the electric double layer at the electrode-electrolyte interface provides the capacitance. The entry falls within inorganic electric semiconductor devices and covers individual devices that rely on barrier-free charge storage mechanisms.
[H10D 1/042] using deposition processes to form electrode extensions -- The code covers inorganic semiconductor devices in which the electrode contacts are lengthened or shaped by applying a deposited material. It includes processes such as chemical-vapor deposition, sputtering or plating that create electrode extensions on individual semiconductor components.
[H10D 10/421] having both emitter-base and base-collector junctions ending at the same surface of the body -- This subclass covers bipolar junction transistors in which the emitter-base and base-collector p-n junctions terminate at a common surface of the semiconductor body, typically a planar or surface-adjacent configuration used to simplify fabrication or improve performance. It falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices within the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 1/043] using patterning processes to form electrode extensions, e.g. etching -- The subclass H10D 1/043 covers manufacturing steps for inorganic electric semiconductor devices in which electrode extensions are created by patterning techniques such as etching or selective deposition. It pertains to processes that form extended electrode structures to improve contacts or interconnect geometry.
[H10D 10/441] having an emitter-base junction ending at a main surface of the body and a base-collector junction ending at a lateral surface of the body -- This subclass covers bipolar junction transistors where the emitter-base junction ends at the main surface of the semiconductor body and the base-collector junction ends at a lateral surface. This geometry supports planar or side-drive configurations and is found in certain power and integrated devices.
[H10D 1/045] of capacitors having potential barriers, e.g. varactors -- Groups H10D1/045 - H10D1/048 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D8/051 and H10D48/021 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 10/461] Inverted vertical BJTs -- The CPC subclass H10D 10/461 covers inverted vertical bipolar junction transistors, in which the collector is formed on top of the substrate and the base region lies between the emitter and collector in a vertical orientation, giving these inorganic semiconductor devices distinctive fabrication and electrical characteristics.
[H10D 1/047] of conductor-insulator-semiconductor capacitors, e.g. trench capacitors -- The H10D 1/047 subclass covers conductor-insulator-semiconductor capacitor structures, notably trench-type capacitors where a conductive electrode, a dielectric layer, and a semiconductor substrate are arranged in a vertical trench geometry for high-density, high-performance electronic applications.
[H10D 1/048] having PN junctions, e.g. hybrid capacitors with MOS control -- The H10D 1/048 class covers semiconductor devices that incorporate PN junctions, typically in hybrid capacitor structures where a MOS element provides electronic control of charge-discharge behavior. It includes designs such as hybrid capacitors that combine a PN-junction component with MOS-controlled switching or regulation.
[H10D 10/60] Lateral BJTs -- The H10D 10/60 subclass defines lateral bipolar junction transistors, a BJT style where the emitter, base and collector regions are arranged side-by-side on the same plane of the semiconductor wafer. These devices conduct current laterally across the substrate and are used for high-frequency switching and amplification in integrated circuits.
[H10D 10/80] Heterojunction BJTs -- Heterojunction bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) are semiconductor devices in which the emitter, base, or collector regions are formed from dissimilar semiconductor materials, creating a heterojunction that enhances carrier injection and reduces base resistance. This structure yields higher frequency response, lower noise and improved gain compared with conventional homojunction BJTs, making them useful in RF amplifiers, high-speed logic and power-efficient circuits.
[H10D 10/821] Vertical heterojunction BJTs -- The H10D 10/821 code classifies bipolar-junction transistors in which the emitter, base and collector are arranged vertically and the junctions are formed between dissimilar semiconductor materials, creating a heterojunction that can enhance carrier injection or breakdown characteristics. This falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices within the broader electricity and semiconductor device hierarchy.
[H10D 10/841] having a two-dimensional base, e.g. modulation-doped base, inversion layer base or delta-doped base -- H10D 10/841 covers bipolar junction transistors whose base region is formed as a two-dimensional layer, such as a modulation-doped, inversion-layer or delta-doped base. The classification applies to devices that confine charge carriers to a thin plane to enhance gain, speed or other performance characteristics.
[H10D 10/861] having an emitter region comprising one or more non-monocrystalline elements of Group IV, e.g. amorphous silicon -- H10D 10/861 classifies bipolar junction transistors whose emitter region contains one or more non-monocrystalline Group IV elements, such as amorphous silicon, rather than a single crystalline material. This feature affects the device's electrical characteristics and manufacturing processes.
[H10D 10/881] Resonant tunnelling transistors -- Resonant tunnelling transistors are a type of bipolar junction transistor that exploit quantum-mechanical tunnelling through a double-barrier structure to achieve very fast switching and negative differential resistance. They are used in high-frequency and microwave circuits, often in digital and analog applications requiring low power and high speed.
[H10D 10/891] comprising lattice-mismatched active layers, e.g. SiGe strained-layer transistors -- This classification refers to bipolar junction transistors that incorporate active semiconductor layers whose crystal lattice differs from the substrate, such as strained-layer silicon-germanium structures. The lattice mismatch is used to modify carrier mobility and device characteristics.
[H10D 1/20] Inductors -- H10D 1/20 covers inductors, passive components that store energy in a magnetic field when current passes through a coil. The subgroup includes single- and multi-turn coils, toroidal and planar inductors, and related magnetic circuit structures used for filtering, energy storage and impedance matching in electronic circuits.
[H10D 12/00] Bipolar devices controlled by the field effect, e.g. insulated-gate bipolar transistors [IGBT] -- In this group, when the manufacture or treatment of a device is determined to be novel and non-obvious, the device itself is also classified.

Group H10D12/01 is also impacted by reclassification into group H10D12/031 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
