[H10D 12/038] having a recessed gate, e.g. trench-gate IGBTs -- Group H10D12/038 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D12/031 and H10D48/031 . Groups H10D12/031 , H10D48/031 and H10D12/038 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/211] Gated diodes -- Group H10D12/211 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D18/40 and H10D18/60 - H10D18/655 . Groups H10D12/211 , H10D18/40 and H10D18/60 - H10D18/655 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/212] having PN junction gates, e.g. field controlled diodes -- This subclass covers semiconductor devices that use a PN-junction gate to control current flow, such as field-controlled diodes. It is a subcategory of bipolar devices controlled by the field effect within inorganic electric semiconductor devices. The CPC code H10D 12/212 is applied to classify these IGBT-type structures.
[H10D 12/411] Insulated-gate bipolar transistors [IGBT] -- Group H10D12/411 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D12/415 , H10D12/416 , H10D12/417 , H10D12/418 and H10D84/161 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/415] having edge termination structures -- Group H10D12/415 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/411 . Groups H10D12/411 and H10D12/415 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/416] Bidirectional devices, e.g. trench-gate IGBTs having additional gates at the anode side -- Group H10D12/416 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/411 . Groups H10D12/411 and H10D12/416 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/417] having a drift region having a doping concentration that is higher at the collector side relative to other parts of the drift region -- Group H10D12/417 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/411 . Groups H10D12/411 and H10D12/417 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/418] having a drift region having a doping concentration that is higher at the emitter side relative to other parts of the drift region -- Group H10D12/418 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/411 . Groups H10D12/411 and H10D12/418 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/421] on insulating layers or insulating substrates, e.g. thin-film IGBTs -- Group H10D12/421 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/491 . Groups H10D12/491 and H10D12/421 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/441] Vertical IGBTs -- H10D 12/441 covers vertical insulated-gate bipolar transistors, a type of bipolar device where the current flow is controlled by a field-effect gate. These IGBTs have a vertically oriented structure that combines the low-on-state loss of a MOSFET with the high current capability of a bipolar transistor, making them suited for medium-and high-power applications such as motor drives and inverters.
[H10D 12/461] having non-planar surfaces, e.g. having trenches, recesses or pillars in the surfaces of the emitter, base or collector regions -- Groups H10D12/461 and H10D12/481 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/491 . Groups H10D12/491 , H10D12/461 and H10D12/481 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 12/481] having gate structures on slanted surfaces, on vertical surfaces, or in grooves, e.g. trench gate IGBTs -- This subclass covers IGBT structures where the gate electrode is formed on non-planar geometries such as slanted surfaces, vertical faces or etched grooves, including trench-gate designs. The configuration improves field-effect control of the bipolar device and belongs to inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 12/491] having both emitter contacts and collector contacts in the same substrate side -- Group H10D12/491 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D12/421 and H10D12/461 - H10D12/481 . Groups H10D12/491 , H10D12/421 and H10D12/461 - H10D12/481 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 1/40] Resistors -- Group H10D1/40 is impacted by reclassification into group H10D48/38 . Groups H10D1/40 and H10D48/38 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 1/43] Resistors having PN junctions -- The subclass H10D 1/43 covers resistors that incorporate a pn junction as the primary resistance element, using the rectifying properties of the junction to achieve a stable resistance value. It includes semiconductor resistors where the pn interface is deliberately designed for resistive behavior, such as thin-film or bulk devices employed in integrated circuits and other electronic assemblies.
[H10D 1/47] Resistors having no potential barriers -- H10D 1/47 covers resistors that operate without potential barriers, such as ohmic or linear resistors where current flow is not limited by junctions or depletion regions. These devices belong to inorganic electric semiconductor devices within the semiconductor devices group under electricity.
[H10D 1/472] having an active material comprising carbon, e.g. diamond or diamond-like carbon [DLC] -- The subclass H10D 1/472 concerns individual inorganic electric semiconductor devices whose active layer is made of carbon-based materials, such as diamond or diamond-like carbon (DLC). These carbon materials are employed for their exceptional thermal conductivity, high breakdown voltage, and radiation-hard properties in power or high-frequency semiconductor applications.
[H10D 1/474] comprising refractory metals, transition metals, noble metals, metal compounds or metal alloys, e.g. silicides -- A subgroup of semiconductor device classifications that pertains to individual devices built from refractory, transition or noble metals, their compounds or alloys-such as silicides-used as inorganic electric semiconductor components.
[H10D 1/476] comprising conducting organic materials, e.g. conducting polymers -- The CPC subclass H10D 1/476 covers individual semiconductor devices that incorporate conducting organic materials, such as conducting polymers, within inorganic electric semiconductor structures. It specifies the use of organic conductors to form or improve the functionality of the device.
[H10D 1/60] Capacitors -- H10D 1/60 classifies capacitors that are individual inorganic semiconductor devices used in electrical circuits, falling under the broader groups of electricity, semiconductor devices and inorganic electric semiconductor devices. This entry groups all types of capacitors irrespective of their specific construction or application.
[H10D 1/62] Capacitors having potential barriers -- Capacitors that use a potential-barrier structure to store charge, typically formed by semiconductor junctions or depletion regions, are classified under H10D 1/62. These devices exploit the electric field across the barrier to provide variable capacitance, often employed in tunable filters, voltage-controlled oscillators and other high-frequency applications.
[H10D 1/64] Variable-capacitance diodes, e.g. varactors -- H10D 1/64 refers to variable-capacitance diodes - also known as varactors. They are inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 1/66] Conductor-insulator-semiconductor capacitors, e.g. MOS capacitors -- H10D 1/66 covers capacitors formed by a conductive electrode, an insulating layer, and a semiconductor substrate, the classic metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) capacitor structure. It includes designs where the insulator is an oxide or other dielectric and addresses device geometries, materials, and fabrication methods relevant to such conductor-insulator-semiconductor devices.
[H10D 1/665] Trench conductor-insulator-semiconductor capacitors, e.g. trench MOS capacitors -- H10D 1/665 covers trench-type capacitors where a conductor, an insulating layer, and a semiconductor are arranged in a vertical trench geometry, commonly used for trench MOS capacitors in integrated circuits. The classification applies to devices that exploit the trench structure to achieve high capacitance density and improved electrical performance.
[H10D 1/68] Capacitors having no potential barriers -- A subclass of inorganic electric semiconductor devices that includes capacitors which store charge without employing a dielectric or other material that creates a potential barrier, relying instead on direct conduction between the electrodes. These simple-plate or conductive-film capacitors are used where barrier-free charge accumulation is required, often in low-leakage or high-frequency applications.
[H10D 1/682] having dielectrics comprising perovskite structures -- The H10D 1/682 subclass encompasses semiconductor devices that incorporate dielectric materials with perovskite crystal structures, such as capacitor or insulating layers built from these inorganic compounds. It covers designs where the perovskite dielectric provides specific electrical properties within the device.
[H10D 1/684] the dielectrics comprising multiple layers, e.g. comprising buffer layers, seed layers or gradient layers -- The H10D 1/684 subclass covers inorganic semiconductor devices whose dielectric layers are formed as a stack of two or more distinct layers, such as a buffer layer combined with a seed layer or a gradient-type multilayer structure. It includes designs where the different dielectric layers are engineered to improve adhesion, stress management, electrical isolation, or performance of the overall device.
[H10D 1/688] comprising barrier layers to prevent diffusion of hydrogen or oxygen -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor devices that incorporate barrier layers specifically designed to prevent the diffusion of hydrogen or oxygen, thereby protecting device performance and reliability.
[H10D 1/692] Electrodes -- The H10D 1/692 subclass defines electrodes employed in inorganic electric semiconductor devices, encompassing the conductive contacts that form electrical connections to diodes, transistors, thyristors and similar components. It includes various electrode structures, materials and manufacturing methods designed to inject or collect charge carriers in solid-state devices.
[H10D 1/694] comprising noble metals or noble metal oxides -- H10D 1/694 classifies semiconductor devices that incorporate noble metals-such as gold, silver, platinum-or their oxides within the structure or contacts. It falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices, covering individual devices that use these materials to improve conductivity, stability or catalytic properties.
[H10D 1/696] comprising multiple layers, e.g. comprising a barrier layer and a metal layer  barrier layers to prevent diffusion of hydrogen or oxygen in perovskite based capacitors  H10D1/688 -- Covers inorganic semiconductor devices that employ a multilayer construction, such as a barrier layer combined with a metal layer, to inhibit hydrogen or oxygen diffusion in perovskite-based capacitors, thereby enhancing stability and performance.
[H10D 1/711] having non-planar surfaces, e.g. formed by texturisation -- H10D 1/711 covers individual inorganic semiconductor devices whose active elements have deliberately non-planar surfaces, such as textured, pyramidal or otherwise patterned faces, to modify optical or electrical properties; examples include textured solar-cell wafers or textured diode structures designed for enhanced light trapping or carrier collection.
[H10D 1/712] being rough surfaces, e.g. using hemispherical grains -- H10D 1/712 covers semiconductor devices whose active surfaces are intentionally roughened, for example by forming hemispherical grains, to enhance properties such as light absorption or electrical contact. The rough texture is a design feature of inorganic electric semiconductor devices, falling under the broader classes of electricity, semiconductor devices, and inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 1/714] having horizontal extensions -- H10D 1/714 pertains to inorganic semiconductor devices such as power diodes or thyristors that incorporate horizontal extensions on the chip or substrate. These lateral extensions serve to improve heat spreading, current distribution or mechanical support of the device.
[H10D 1/716] having vertical extensions -- H10D 1/716 covers individual inorganic semiconductor devices that incorporate vertical extensions such as fins, pillars, or raised epitaxial regions extending from the substrate. These structures increase surface area, improve current handling or device isolation, and are used in power, high-frequency or optoelectronic applications.
[H10D 18/00] Thyristors -- In this group, when the manufacture or treatment of a device is determined to be novel and non-obvious, the device itself is also classified.
[H10D 18/01] Manufacture or treatment -- Groups H10D18/01 - H10D18/031 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D12/031 and H10D48/031 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 18/021] of bidirectional devices, e.g. triacs -- This subclass concerns bidirectional semiconductor switching devices that can conduct current in either direction, most notably triacs used for AC power control and dimming applications. It is situated within the thyristor group of inorganic electric semiconductor devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 18/031] of lateral or planar thyristors -- H10D 18/031 covers thyristor devices in which the active layers are arranged laterally or in a planar geometry, meaning the current path runs parallel to the semiconductor surface rather than vertically through stacked layers. This classification includes designs such as lateral SCRs and planar power thyristors used in high-voltage switching applications.
[H10D 18/211] having built-in localised breakdown or breakover regions, e.g. self-protected against destructive spontaneous firing -- H10D 18/211 classifies inorganic semiconductor thyristors that incorporate built-in localized breakdown or breakover zones, providing self-protection against unintended destructive firing.
[H10D 18/221] having amplifying gate structures, e.g. cascade configurations -- The H10D 18/221 subclass refers to thyristors that incorporate amplifying gate structures, such as cascade configurations, where the gate provides gain or signal amplification. It applies to inorganic semiconductor power devices whose gate design enhances control properties beyond simple triggering.
[H10D 18/241] Asymmetrical thyristors -- The H10D 18/241 subclass groups thyristor devices whose structure or doping is deliberately asymmetrical, giving them different electrical characteristics depending on the direction of current flow. These asymmetrical thyristors are used in power-electronics applications where distinct turn-on thresholds or voltage handling on each polarity are required.
[H10D 18/251] Lateral thyristors -- Lateral thyristors are inorganic semiconductor power switches in which the anode, cathode and control electrode are arranged laterally on the same substrate surface, allowing current to flow across the plane of the device rather than through its thickness. This planar geometry facilitates high-density integration and fast switching in power-electronics applications.
[H10D 18/40] with turn-on by field effect -- Group H10D18/40 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/211 . Groups H10D12/211 and H10D18/40 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 18/60] Gate-turn-off devices -- Groups H10D18/60 - H10D18/655 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D12/211 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 18/65] with turn-off by field effect -- Thyristors that are capable of being switched off by applying a control electric field rather than by forced commutation. The field effect is used to extinguish the conductive state, as in MOS-controlled or gate-triggered thyristors employed in power conversion and motor-drive circuits.
[H10D 18/655] produced by insulated gate structures -- The subclass H10D 18/655 includes thyristor devices that are manufactured with insulated gate structures, where the control electrode is separated from the semiconductor by a dielectric layer. This design yields insulated-gate thyristors and similar voltage-controlled power switches that combine features of conventional thyristors with gate insulation for improved control and efficiency.
[H10D 18/80] Bidirectional devices, e.g. triacs -- The CPC entry H10D 18/80 covers bidirectional semiconductor devices that conduct current in both directions, such as triacs. These components belong to the thyristor family within inorganic electric semiconductor devices and are used where alternating current control is required.

[H10D 30/0193] by modifying properties of the stacked channels -- The H10D 30/0193 subclass covers field-effect transistors that achieve improved performance by altering the characteristics of multiple stacked channel layers, such as by adjusting doping, strain, dielectric composition or geometry of each channel. It includes methods that modify the electrical or physical properties of the stacked channels to influence carrier transport, threshold voltage or leakage current.
[H10D 30/0194] the stacked channels having different properties -- This subcategory covers FETs where multiple semiconductor channels are stacked vertically, each channel engineered with distinct electrical characteristics such as mobility, threshold voltage, or doping to achieve specific performance benefits.
[H10D 30/0195] forming inner spacers between adjacent channels, e.g. changing their shapes or sizes -- Groups H10D30/0195 - H10D30/0197 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D30/01 , H10D30/014 , H10D30/024 , H10D30/031 , H10D30/0321 , H10D30/0323 and H10D30/0327 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0196] by modifying properties of the inner spacers -- This subclass includes field-effect transistor designs where the electrical characteristics are adjusted by changing the material, geometry, or doping of the inner spacers that separate the gate from the source/drain regions, thereby influencing channel control, leakage, and switching speed.
[H10D 30/0197] the inner spacers having different properties -- The entry covers field-effect transistors in which the inner spacers-materials placed between the gate and the source/drain regions-are formed from different substances or have distinct physical or electrical characteristics, such as varied dielectric constant, stress profile, or thermal conductivity, to modify device behavior.
[H10D 30/0198] forming source or drain electrodes wherein semiconductor bodies are replaced by dielectric layers and the source or drain electrodes extend through the dielectric layers -- Group H10D30/0198 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D30/01 , H10D30/014 , H10D30/024 , H10D30/031 , H10D30/0321 , H10D30/0323 and H10D30/0327 . [H10D 30/021] of FETs having insulated gates [IGFET] -- Groups H10D30/021 , H10D30/0212 , H10D30/0213 , H10D30/0215 , H10D30/0217 , H10D30/0221 , H10D30/023 , H10D30/0243 , H10D30/025 , H10D30/026 , H10D30/027 , H10D30/0275 , H10D30/0277 , H10D30/0278 and H10D30/0413 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/01 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0212] using self-aligned silicidation -- The H10D 30/0212 classification concerns field-effect transistors that employ a self-aligned silicidation process, where a metal silicide is formed in precise alignment with the transistor's source, drain, or gate regions to reduce resistance and improve performance. It falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 30/0213] providing different silicide thicknesses on gate electrodes and on source regions or drain regions -- The H10D 30/0213 subclass relates to field-effect transistor fabrication techniques that apply silicide layers of differing thicknesses to the gate electrode versus the source and drain regions. By varying the silicide thickness, the process can optimize electrical characteristics such as resistance, capacitance, or device reliability.
[H10D 30/0215] using self-aligned selective metal deposition simultaneously on gate electrodes and the source regions or drain regions -- H10D 30/0215 relates to field-effect transistors in which a self-aligned selective metal deposition process is applied simultaneously to the gate electrode and to the source or drain regions. The method enables combined formation of metal contacts while preserving alignment accuracy.
[H10D 30/0217] forming self-aligned punch-through stoppers or threshold implants under gate regions -- H10D 30/0217 covers methods for creating self-aligned punch-through stoppers or threshold implants beneath the gate region of a field-effect transistor, typically in inorganic semiconductor devices. The processes ensure precise doping or structural features that control channel formation and device performance.
[H10D 30/0218] having pocket halo regions selectively formed at the sides of the gates -- Group H10D30/0218 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D30/01 and H10D30/022 . Groups H10D30/01 , H10D30/022 and H10D30/0218 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/022] having lightly-doped source or drain extensions selectively formed at the sides of the gates -- Group H10D30/022 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/01 . Group H10D30/022 is also impacted by reclassification into group H10D30/0218 . Groups H10D30/01 , H10D30/022 and H10D30/0218 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0221] having asymmetry in the channel direction, e.g. lateral high-voltage MISFETs having drain offset region or extended-drain MOSFETs [EDMOS] -- This subclass groups field-effect transistors whose channel is deliberately asymmetric, such as lateral high-voltage MISFETs with a drain-offset region or extended-drain MOSFETs, to improve breakdown voltage and reduce hot-carrier stress. It falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 30/0223] having source and drain regions or source and drain extensions self-aligned to sides of the gate -- Groups H10D30/0223 - H10D30/0229 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D30/01 and H10D30/0273 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0225] using an initial gate mask complementary to the prospective gate location, e.g. using dummy source and drain electrodes -- H10D 30/0225 relates to methods for forming the gate of a field-effect transistor by first applying an initial mask that matches the intended gate location, often using dummy source and drain electrodes to define the mask pattern before the final gate is created.
[H10D 30/0227] having both lightly-doped source and drain extensions and source and drain regions self-aligned to the sides of the gate, e.g. lightly-doped drain [LDD] MOSFET or double-diffused drain [DDD] MOSFET -- The code H10D 30/0227 classifies field-effect transistors that incorporate lightly-doped source and drain extensions together with source and drain regions that are self-aligned to the sides of the gate, exemplified by lightly-doped-drain (LDD) MOSFETs and double-diffused-drain (DDD) MOSFETs.
[H10D 30/0229] forming drain regions and lightly-doped drain [LDD] simultaneously, e.g. using implantation through a T-shaped mask -- The H10D 30/0229 subclass covers processes for creating the drain region of a field-effect transistor together with a lightly-doped drain (LDD) in a single step. Typical methods involve ion implantation through a T-shaped mask that defines both the main drain and the surrounding LDD area at once, reducing processing time and improving junction uniformity. This technique is used in manufacturing inorganic semiconductor devices such as MOSFETs.
[H10D 30/023] having multiple independently-addressable gate electrodes influencing the same channel  manufacture or treatment of dual gate TFTs  H10D30/031 -- This CPC subclass covers field-effect transistors that have two or more independently addressable gate electrodes influencing the same conduction channel, such as dual-gate thin-film transistors, and includes methods for their manufacture or post-fabrication treatment.

All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0243] using dummy structures having essentially the same shapes as the semiconductor bodies, e.g. to provide stability -- The classification covers methods that employ dummy structures that replicate the shape of the active semiconductor bodies, such as in field-effect transistors, to improve mechanical stability or process uniformity during fabrication.
[H10D 30/0245] by further thinning the channel after patterning the channel, e.g. using sacrificial oxidation on fins -- Group H10D30/0245 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/01 . Group H10D30/0245 is also impacted by reclassification into groups H10D30/0191 - H10D30/0194 . [H10D 30/025] of vertical IGFETs  of VDMOS  H10D30/0291 ; of vertical TFTs  H10D30/0318 -- H10D 30/025 concerns vertical insulated-gate field-effect transistors, specifically the design and fabrication of devices such as vertical double-diffused MOS (VDMOS) transistors and vertical thin-film transistors. It addresses the geometry, doping and layer arrangement that give the transistor a current-flow direction perpendicular to the substrate.
[H10D 30/026] having laterally-coplanar source and drain regions, a gate at the sides of the bulk channel, and both horizontal and vertical current flow  of LDMOS  H10D30/0289 -- H10D 30/026 covers field-effect transistors in which the source and drain regions lie laterally coplanar, the gate surrounds the bulk channel on its sides, and current can travel both horizontally across the channel and vertically into the substrate, as found in lateral diffused MOS (LDMOS) devices.
[H10D 30/027] of lateral single-gate IGFETs -- The H10D 30/027 class covers lateral single-gate insulated-gate field-effect transistors (IGFETs), a type of inorganic semiconductor device where the channel runs parallel to the substrate surface and is controlled by a single gate electrode. It includes structures, fabrication methods and variations specific to this layout.
[H10D 30/0273] forming final gates or dummy gates after forming source and drain electrodes, e.g. contact first technology -- Group H10D30/0273 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/01 . Group H10D30/0273 is also impacted by reclassification into groups H10D30/0223 - H10D30/0229 and H10D64/017 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0275] forming single crystalline semiconductor source or drain regions resulting in recessed gates, e.g. forming raised source or drain regions -- The H10D 30/0275 entry covers techniques for creating single-crystalline source or drain regions in field-effect transistors that produce recessed gate structures, such as processes that form raised source or drain areas to improve device performance.
[H10D 30/0277] forming conductor-insulator-semiconductor or Schottky barrier source or drain regions -- The entry covers processes for creating the source or drain regions of field-effect transistors that consist of a conductor-insulator-semiconductor stack or a Schottky barrier, typically by depositing or patterning metals, insulating layers, and semiconductor material and defining the junctions that control carrier injection.
[H10D 30/0278] forming single crystalline channels on wafers after forming insulating device isolations -- This subclass covers methods that create single-crystal semiconductor channels on a wafer after insulating device isolations have been formed, typically used in the fabrication of field-effect transistors. The process ensures high-mobility channel regions within defined isolation structures.
[H10D 30/028] of double-diffused metal oxide semiconductor [DMOS] FETs -- Groups H10D30/028 - H10D30/0297 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10D12/031 and H10D30/01 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/0281] of lateral DMOS [LDMOS] FETs -- The entry H10D 30/0281 refers to lateral DMOS (LDMOS) field-effect transistors, a power MOSFET architecture in which the drain and source are placed side-by-side on the same semiconductor substrate. LDMOS devices are optimized for high-voltage, high-frequency operation and are commonly used in RF power amplifiers, automotive and communication circuits.
[H10D 30/0285] using formation of insulating sidewall spacers -- This CPC subclass covers field-effect transistors in which insulating sidewall spacers are formed alongside the channel region. The spacers serve to define the active area, provide isolation, and aid in controlling the electric field during device operation. Techniques for creating these spacers, such as deposition and anisotropic etching, are included.
[H10D 30/0287] using recessing of the source electrodes -- The entry covers a manufacturing approach for inorganic field-effect transistors in which the source electrodes are recessed below the surrounding semiconductor surface. By creating a shallow cavity for the source contacts, the technique reduces access resistance, improves current flow and enhances gate control of the channel, leading to higher device performance and lower power loss.
[H10D 30/0289] using recessing of the gate electrodes, e.g. to form trench gate electrodes -- The code H10D 30/0289 pertains to inorganic electric semiconductor field-effect transistors whose gate electrodes are formed by recessing, for example by creating trench-type gates. It addresses manufacturing methods and device structures that employ a recessed or trench gate to improve performance.
[H10D 30/0291] of vertical DMOS [VDMOS] FETs -- H10D 30/0291 covers vertical DMOS (VDMOS) field-effect transistors, a class of power MOSFETs in which the current flows vertically from source to drain through the substrate. These devices are optimized for high voltage and high current operation, offering low on-resistance and fast switching, and are commonly used in power converters and motor drives.
[H10D 30/0293] using formation of insulating sidewall spacers -- This subclass covers field-effect transistor manufacturing methods that employ insulating sidewall spacers formed adjacent to the gate structure. The spacers define the active region, improve isolation, and enable precise control of channel dimensions during source/drain formation.
[H10D 30/0295] using recessing of the source electrodes -- The code H10D 30/0295 covers field-effect transistors in which the source electrodes are formed in a recessed region of the semiconductor. It includes methods that create depressions or cavities beneath the source contacts to improve electrical contact, reduce parasitic resistance, or enhance device reliability.
[H10D 30/0297] using recessing of the gate electrodes, e.g. to form trench gate electrodes -- This subclass concerns field-effect transistors whose gate electrodes are recessed, such as by forming trench-type gates, to enhance channel control and device performance. It includes methods and structures that deliberately create a hollow or depressed region for the gate within the semiconductor.

[H10D 30/0512] of FETs having PN homojunction gates -- The subclass H10D 30/0512 refers to field-effect transistors whose gate is formed by a pn homojunction within the same semiconductor material. This design uses the built-in junction to control the channel current, offering precise voltage-dependent switching without a separate metal gate. It falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 30/0515] of vertical FETs having PN homojunction gates -- This subclass covers vertical field-effect transistors whose gate is created by a pn homojunction, using the depletion region of the junction to control the channel. Such devices are employed in power and switching applications where the gate electrode is integrated as a doped region within the semiconductor stack.
[H10D 30/0516] of FETs having PN heterojunction gates -- H10D 30/0516 covers field-effect transistors in which the gate is formed by a p-n heterojunction, combining materials of differing band-gaps to create a junction that controls channel conductivity. Such PN-heterojunction gates improve carrier confinement and enable high-frequency or high-power operation in devices such as heterojunction FETs and high-electron-mobility transistors.

[H10D 30/0614] using processes wherein the final gate is made after the completion of the source and drain regions, e.g. gate-last processes using dummy gates -- H10D 30/0614 covers semiconductor manufacturing methods in which the gate electrode is formed after the source and drain regions have been completed, typically employing a gate-last approach that uses a temporary dummy gate during earlier processing steps. This category includes techniques for removing the dummy structure and depositing the final gate material to complete the field-effect transistor.
[H10D 30/0616] using processes wherein the final gate is made before the completion of the source and drain regions, e.g. gate-first processes -- H10D 30/0616 covers gate-first fabrication methods for field-effect transistors, in which the final gate structure is formed before the source and drain regions are completed. This process sequence is used to improve gate control and reduce parasitic capacitances in inorganic semiconductor devices.

[H10D 30/202] FETs having static field-induced regions, e.g. static-induction transistors [SIT] or permeable base transistors [PBT] -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors in which a static electric field creates a permanent conductive region, such as static-induction transistors and permeable-base transistors. The devices operate by maintaining a field-induced channel rather than using a conventional gate-controlled switching mechanism.
[H10D 30/204] Velocity modulation transistors [VMT] -- Velocity modulation transistors are a class of field-effect transistors in which the carrier velocity in the channel is controlled by an auxiliary gate or field, allowing rapid modulation of current flow for high-frequency operation. They typically employ a two-dimensional electron gas or similar channel and are used in microwave and terahertz applications.

[H10D 30/402] Single electron transistors; Coulomb blockade transistors -- H10D 30/402 covers single-electron transistors and Coulomb-blockade transistors, which are field-effect devices that control current by moving individual electrons through a nanoscale island, exploiting the Coulomb blockade effect for ultra-low-power operation.

[H10D 30/472] having lower bandgap active layer formed on top of wider bandgap layer, e.g. inverted HEMT -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors in which a lower-bandgap active layer is formed on top of a wider-bandgap layer, creating a reversed heterostructure such as an inverted high-electron-mobility transistor. It relates to inorganic semiconductor devices that use band-gap engineering to enhance carrier confinement and device performance.
[H10D 30/473] having confinement of carriers by multiple heterojunctions, e.g. quantum well HEMT -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors whose carriers are confined by a series of heterojunctions, such as quantum-well high-electron-mobility transistors. The device uses alternating semiconductor layers to form multiple quantum wells that trap electrons in the channel, enhancing speed and frequency response. It is applied to inorganic semiconductor FETs that achieve carrier confinement through engineered heterostructures.

It is used to classify technologies that leverage these inorganic materials to enhance the performance, speed, or frequency response of FETs within the broader electricity and semiconductor device categories.

[H10D 30/474] having multiple parallel 2D charge carrier gas channels -- This subclass covers FET structures where the conductive channel consists of several parallel two-dimensional electron or hole gases, enabling increased current drive or novel transport properties. Typically realized by stacking or laterally arranging multiple quantum-well layers within the transistor channel.
[H10D 30/475] having wider bandgap layer formed on top of lower bandgap active layer, e.g. undoped barrier HEMTs such as i-AlGaN/GaN HEMTs -- H10D 30/475 concerns field-effect transistors that incorporate a wider-bandgap semiconductor layer formed on top of a lower-bandgap active layer, creating an undoped barrier such as in AlGaN/GaN high-electron-mobility transistors.
[H10D 30/4755] having wide bandgap charge-carrier supplying layers, e.g. modulation doped HEMTs such as n-AlGaAs/GaAs HEMTs -- H10D 30/4755 classifies field-effect transistors that incorporate wide-bandgap charge-carrier supplying layers, for example modulation-doped high-electron-mobility transistors such as n-AlGaAs/GaAs HEMTs. These devices use a heterostructure to provide a high-mobility channel while the wide bandgap layer supplies carriers.
[H10D 30/476] having gate trenches interrupting the 2D charge carrier gas channels, e.g. hybrid MOS-HEMTs -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors in which trenches etched in the gate interrupt the two-dimensional charge-carrier gas channel, such as hybrid MOS-HEMT structures. The trench geometry isolates or modulates sections of the channel to improve control and reduce leakage.

[H10D 30/506] having different thicknesses, sizes or shapes -- This CPC subclass H10D 30/506 concerns field-effect transistors whose semiconductor layers or conductive regions are made with different thicknesses, sizes or shapes to obtain particular electrical properties. [H10D 30/507] characterised by inner spacers between adjacent channels -- H10D 30/507 covers field-effect transistors that include inner spacers positioned between adjacent channels, a design used to isolate or control charge flow in inorganic electric semiconductor devices. [H10D 30/508] characterised by the relative sizes, shapes or dispositions of the inner spacers -- The subclass pertains to field-effect transistors in which the design is defined by the comparative dimensions, geometry or arrangement of the inner spacers that separate device regions, influencing channel formation, isolation or leakage. It addresses variations in spacer size, shape or placement within inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 30/509] characterised by the material of the inner spacers -- H10D 30/509 covers field-effect transistors in which the inner spacer-a dielectric or insulating structure that separates regions of the device-is defined by its material composition. The classification applies to inorganic semiconductor FETs where the spacer material, such as silicon nitride, silicon dioxide or other ceramics, influences performance or manufacturing, distinguishing them from devices characterized by geometry or other features.
[H10D 30/60] Insulated-gate field-effect transistors [IGFET] -- Insulated-gate field-effect transistors (IGFETs) are semiconductor devices in which a thin insulating layer separates the gate electrode from the channel, allowing voltage-controlled modulation of current without direct gate-to-channel current flow. They are used in digital and analog circuits for high-speed switching and precise voltage regulation.

[H10D 30/63] Vertical IGFETs -- The H10D 30/63 class pertains to vertical insulated-gate field-effect transistors, a type of inorganic semiconductor device where the current channel is oriented perpendicular to the substrate surface. These devices employ a vertical architecture to achieve high current density and low on-resistance, making them suited for power switching and high-frequency applications.
[H10D 30/635] having no inversion channels, e.g. vertical accumulation channel FETs [ACCUFET] or normally-on vertical IGFETs -- This CPC subclass H10D 30/635 covers field-effect transistors that operate without an inversion channel, such as vertical accumulation channel FETs and normally-on vertical insulated-gate FETs. These devices rely on accumulation of carriers in a vertical channel rather than forming an inversion layer. They are used in power and high-frequency applications.
[H10D 30/637] Lateral IGFETs having no inversion channels, e.g. buried channel lateral IGFETs, normally-on lateral IGFETs or depletion-mode lateral IGFETs -- This subclass covers lateral insulated-gate field-effect transistors that operate without an inversion channel, such as buried-channel, normally-on, or depletion-mode lateral IGFETs. The devices are built on an inorganic semiconductor substrate and the channel is formed by doping rather than surface inversion, enabling a normally conductive state.
[H10D 30/64] Double-diffused metal-oxide semiconductor [DMOS] FETs -- Group H10D30/64 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D30/645 and H10D84/101 . Groups H10D30/64 , H10D30/645 and H10D84/101 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/645] Bidirectional devices -- Group H10D30/645 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/64 . Groups H10D30/64 and H10D30/645 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/65] Lateral DMOS [LDMOS] FETs -- Lateral DMOS (LDMOS) field-effect transistors are a type of double-diffused MOSFET where the current flows laterally across the semiconductor surface, allowing high voltage and high-frequency operation, especially in RF power amplifiers and broadband transmitters. The design uses an inorganic semiconductor substrate and incorporates a lateral layout that improves thermal handling and linearity compared with vertical MOSFET structures. This entry falls under the CPC class for inorganic electric semiconductor devices, specifically field-effect transistors.
[H10D 30/655] having edge termination structures -- H10D 30/655 classifies field-effect transistors that incorporate edge termination structures to control the electric field at the device periphery. These structures enhance breakdown voltage and reliability by preventing premature edge breakdown in inorganic semiconductor FETs.
[H10D 30/657] having substrates comprising insulating layers, e.g. SOI-LDMOS transistors -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors whose substrate includes an insulating layer separating the active region from the bulk, such as silicon-on-insulator (SOI) lateral diffused MOS (LDMOS) devices. The insulating layer improves isolation, reduces parasitic capacitance and allows higher voltage operation.
[H10D 30/658] having trench gate electrodes -- The code covers field-effect transistors that use a trench-shaped gate electrode etched into the semiconductor substrate, allowing the gate to surround the channel for improved control and reduced on-resistance. This structure is typical in power MOSFETs and similar devices.
[H10D 30/659] having voltage-sensing or current-sensing structures, e.g. emulator sections or overcurrent sensing cells -- H10D 30/659 covers field-effect transistors that integrate voltage-sensing or current-sensing elements, such as emulator sections or over-current sensing cells, enabling the device to monitor and respond to electrical conditions.
[H10D 30/66] Vertical DMOS [VDMOS] FETs -- Group H10D30/66 is impacted by reclassification into group H10D30/662 . Groups H10D30/66 and H10D30/662 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/662] having a drift region having a doping concentration that is higher between adjacent body regions relative to other parts of the drift region -- Group H10D30/662 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/66 . Groups H10D30/66 and H10D30/662 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/663] having both source contacts and drain contacts on the same surface, i.e. up-drain VDMOS -- This subclass covers vertical double-diffused MOS transistors where the source and drain electrodes are placed on the same surface of the semiconductor, enabling an up-drain configuration. The arrangement simplifies layout and improves certain performance aspects in power FETs.
[H10D 30/664] Inverted VDMOS transistors, i.e. source-down VDMOS transistors -- Inverted VDMOS transistors are a variant of vertical double-diffused MOSFETs where the source region is placed at the top of the device instead of the bottom, allowing the drain current to flow upward through the channel. This source-down architecture reduces on-resistance and improves switching speed, making the devices suitable for power-conversion and high-frequency applications.
[H10D 30/665] having edge termination structures -- The CPC subgroup H10D 30/665 comprises field-effect transistors that incorporate edge-termination structures to control the electric field at the device periphery. These structures mitigate field crowding, raise breakdown voltage and enhance reliability of power-FETs.
[H10D 30/667] having substrates comprising insulating layers, e.g. SOI-VDMOS transistors -- The H10D 30/667 classification covers field-effect transistors whose substrates incorporate insulating layers, such as silicon-on-insulator structures used in VDMOS devices. It includes designs where the insulating layer isolates the active region from the bulk substrate to improve performance and reduce parasitic effects.
[H10D 30/668] having trench gate electrodes, e.g. UMOS transistors -- H10D 30/668 covers field-effect transistors whose gate electrodes are formed in trenches etched into the semiconductor substrate, a structure typical of UMOS (ultra-thin-oxide) devices. The trench-gate design isolates the gate from the channel, allowing higher voltage tolerance, lower on-resistance and improved scaling for power and RF applications.
[H10D 30/669] having voltage-sensing or current-sensing structures, e.g. emulator sections or overcurrent sensing cells -- The H10D 30/669 classification covers field-effect transistors that incorporate voltage-sensing or current-sensing structures, such as emulator sections or over-current sensing cells, integrated into the device to monitor electrical conditions. This sub-group falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices within the broader electricity and semiconductor device categories.
[H10D 30/67] Thin-film transistors [TFT] -- Thin-film transistors (TFTs) are field-effect transistors in which the semiconductor layer is deposited as a thin film on an insulating substrate, often using materials such as amorphous silicon, IGZO or organic compounds. They are commonly employed in display technologies, sensors and flexible electronics, where low-cost, large-area fabrication is required.
[H10D 30/6704] having supplementary regions or layers in the thin films or in the insulated bulk substrates for controlling properties of the device -- Group H10D30/6704 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/6728 . Groups H10D30/6728 and H10D30/6704 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/6706] for preventing leakage current   TFTs characterised by the properties of the source or drain  H10D30/6713 -- H10D 30/6706 in the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses techniques for preventing leakage current in thin-film transistors (TFTs), specifically focusing on characteristics related to the properties of the source or drain regions.
[H10D 30/6708] for preventing the kink effect or the snapback effect, e.g. discharging the minority carriers of the channel region for preventing bipolar effect -- The H10D 30/6708 subclass covers field-effect transistor structures or circuits that incorporate methods to suppress the kink or snapback phenomena, usually by removing minority carriers from the channel to avoid unwanted bipolar conduction. These techniques aim to improve device stability and linearity in inorganic semiconductor FETs.
[H10D 30/6711] by using electrodes contacting the supplementary regions or layers -- H10D 30/6711 covers field-effect transistor designs in which electrodes are connected to supplementary semiconductor regions or layers, enabling control or biasing of those additional structures beyond the primary channel.
[H10D 30/6713] characterised by the properties of the source or drain regions, e.g. compositions or sectional shapes -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors whose defining feature is the nature of the source or drain regions, such as material composition, doping level, geometry or cross-sectional shape. It groups inventions that modify these region properties to affect device performance, including the use of different semiconductors or structural designs in the source or drain.
[H10D 30/6715] characterised by the doping profiles, e.g. having lightly-doped source or drain extensions -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10D 30/6715 pertains to field-effect transistors with specific characteristics in their doping profiles, such as lightly-doped source or drain extensions, which are crucial for enhancing device performance and reliability.
[H10D 30/6717] the source and the drain regions being asymmetrical -- This subcategory covers field-effect transistors whose source and drain regions are intentionally designed to be unequal, such as differing doping levels, sizes or contacts, influencing current flow and performance. It applies to inorganic semiconductor devices within the electricity class.
[H10D 30/6719] having significant overlap between the lightly-doped drains and the gate electrodes, e.g. gate-overlapped LDD [GOLDD] TFTs -- The H10D 30/6719 subclass covers field-effect transistors where the lightly-doped drain regions overlap the gate electrode, a layout known as gate-overlapped LDD (GOLDD) in thin-film transistors. This overlap improves control of the drain current and reduces hot-carrier effects.
[H10D 30/6721] having lightly-doped extensions consisting of multiple lightly doped zones or having non-homogeneous dopant distributions, e.g. graded LDD -- H10D 30/6721 concerns field-effect transistors that incorporate lightly-doped extensions formed from several lightly doped zones or that display a non-uniform dopant profile, such as a graded LDD. The entry covers structures where the source/drain extensions are tapered or segmented to improve field distribution and reduce hot-carrier effects.
[H10D 30/6723] having light shields -- This CPC subgroup covers inorganic field-effect transistors that incorporate light-shield structures to block or reduce incident photons, thereby preventing photo-induced charge effects and improving device stability.
[H10D 30/6725] having supplementary regions or layers for improving the flatness of the device -- The H10D 30/6725 class covers semiconductor field-effect transistors that incorporate additional regions or layers to improve the planar uniformity of the device, enhancing flatness and performance. It applies to inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 30/6727] having source or drain regions connected to bulk conducting substrates -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors in which the source or drain regions are electrically linked to a bulk conductive substrate rather than isolated diffusions. The design ties one or both terminals directly to the substrate to provide a low-resistance path and simplify device architecture. It is used in certain inorganic semiconductor devices within the broader electricity and semiconductor device categories.
[H10D 30/6728] Vertical TFTs -- Group H10D30/6728 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D30/6704 and H10D30/674 . Groups H10D30/6728 , H10D30/6704 and H10D30/674 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/6729] characterised by the electrodes -- H10D 30/6729 covers field-effect transistors whose defining feature is the design or material of the electrodes, such as source, drain, gate or contact structures, including their arrangement, doping, and integration with the semiconductor channel. It applies to inorganic semiconductor devices where electrode characteristics are essential to the transistor's operation.
[H10D 30/673] characterised by the shapes, relative sizes or dispositions of the gate electrodes -- H10D 30/673 covers field-effect transistors whose performance or function is defined primarily by the geometry of the gate electrodes, including their shapes, relative dimensions and spatial arrangement. The subclass is used for patents that claim specific gate structures such as multi-finger, interdigitated or specially sized gates to achieve particular electrical characteristics.
[H10D 30/6731] Top-gate only TFTs -- Top-gate only thin-film transistors are field-effect devices where the gate electrode is placed exclusively on the top side of the semiconductor layer, eliminating a bottom-gate structure. This architecture simplifies fabrication and can improve stability and performance in display panels and sensor arrays.
[H10D 30/6732] Bottom-gate only TFTs -- H10D 30/6732 covers thin-film transistors in which the gate electrode is located beneath the semiconductor layer, i.e., bottom-gate only TFTs. The class is used for devices that employ this bottom-gate architecture, common in display backplanes and sensor arrays.
[H10D 30/6733] Multi-gate TFTs -- Group H10D30/6733 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D30/501 - H10D30/509 and H10D30/674 . Groups H10D30/6733 , H10D30/501 - H10D30/509 and H10D30/674 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

[H10D 30/6736] characterised by the shape of gate insulators -- H10D 30/6736 designates field-effect transistors in which the gate-insulator is defined by its geometric shape rather than solely by material composition. The classification applies to inorganic semiconductor devices where the contour of the insulating layer influences device performance.
[H10D 30/6737] characterised by the electrode materials -- This CPC subclass covers field-effect transistors that are distinguished primarily by the composition of their electrodes, such as source, drain or gate materials, including metals, alloys, conductive oxides or other inorganic conductors. It includes designs where the electrode material itself provides the defining functional characteristic of the device.
[H10D 30/6738] Schottky barrier electrodes -- Schottky barrier electrodes are metal contacts that form a rectifying junction with a semiconductor, used in FETs to provide low-resistance source or drain connections and to modulate channel conduction without forming an insulating oxide layer. Their barrier height depends on the metal work function and the semiconductor material, influencing leakage current and switching speed.
[H10D 30/6739] Conductor-insulator-semiconductor electrodes -- H10D 30/6739 covers conductor-insulator-semiconductor (CIS) electrode structures used in field-effect transistors, where a conductive layer, an insulating dielectric, and a semiconductor are stacked to form the gate stack that modulates channel current. The entry addresses the design, materials and functional aspects of these CIS electrodes within inorganic electric semiconductor devices.

[H10D 30/6743] Silicon -- Silicon is an inorganic semiconductor material used in field-effect transistors and other semiconductor devices. It forms the basis of many integrated circuits due to its favorable electronic properties and ability to be doped to create n-type and p-type regions. The CPC entry H10D 30/6743 classifies inventions relating specifically to silicon in this context.
[H10D 30/6744] Monocrystalline silicon -- Monocrystalline silicon is a single-crystal form of silicon used as the semiconductor substrate in field-effect transistors. Its uniform crystal lattice provides high carrier mobility and low defect density, enabling reliable operation of FETs in integrated circuits. This material falls under the CPC class H10D 30/6744 for inorganic electric semiconductor devices.
[H10D 30/6745] Polycrystalline or microcrystalline silicon -- H10D 30/6745 covers the use of polycrystalline or microcrystalline silicon as the semiconductor material in inorganic electric semiconductor devices, specifically field-effect transistors. It addresses fabrication methods, structural characteristics, and performance aspects of FETs that employ silicon films composed of multiple crystal grains rather than a single crystal.
[H10D 30/6746] Amorphous silicon -- Amorphous silicon is a non-crystalline form of silicon used as the active material in thin-film field-effect transistors and related semiconductor devices. Its disordered structure gives a high resistivity and enables low-temperature deposition on large substrates, making it suitable for display back-planes and photovoltaic applications. This entry classifies inventions that employ amorphous silicon within inorganic electric semiconductor devices, specifically field-effect transistors.
[H10D 30/6748] having a multilayer structure or superlattice structure -- Group H10D30/6748 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10D30/501 - H10D30/509 . Groups H10D30/6748 and H10D30/501 - H10D30/509 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/675] Group III-V materials, Group II-VI materials, Group IV-VI materials, selenium or tellurium -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors that use compound semiconductors made from group III-V, II-VI or IV-VI elements, including devices in which selenium or tellurium serves as the channel or active material.
[H10D 30/6755] Oxide semiconductors, e.g. zinc oxide, copper aluminium oxide or cadmium stannate -- H10D 30/6755 covers oxide semiconductor materials such as zinc oxide, copper-aluminium oxide or cadmium stannate used in field-effect transistors. The code lies under inorganic electric semiconductor devices within the broader electricity and semiconductor device classes.
[H10D 30/6756] Amorphous oxide semiconductors -- This class covers amorphous oxide semiconductor materials used in field-effect transistors. These inorganic thin-film oxides, such as indium-gallium-zinc oxide, provide high mobility and transparency for display and driver circuits. The subclass focuses on device structures and processes that exploit the unique electrical properties of these amorphous oxides.

[H10D 30/6758] characterised by the insulating substrates -- The H10D 30/6758 subgroup covers inorganic field-effect transistors that are built on insulating substrates, such as silicon-on-insulator, sapphire or glass, where the insulating layer isolates the active channel from the bulk material. This architecture is used to reduce parasitic capacitance and improve device speed and radiation hardness.
[H10D 30/6759] Silicon-on-sapphire [SOS] substrates -- H10D 30/6759 covers silicon-on-sapphire (SOS) substrates used for field-effect transistors and other semiconductor devices. SOS is a thin silicon layer grown on a sapphire crystal, providing high-frequency performance, radiation hardness and electrical isolation. This entry falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices in the electricity classification.
[H10D 30/68] Floating-gate IGFETs -- Group H10D30/68 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/701 . Groups H10D30/701 and H10D30/68 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/681] having only two programming levels  Floating gate IGFETs programmable by two single electrons  H10D30/688 -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10D 30/681 pertains to field-effect transistors with inorganic electric semiconductor devices, specifically focusing on floating gate insulated-gate field-effect transistors (IGFETs) that are programmable by two single electrons, characterized by having only two programming levels.
[H10D 30/682] programmed by injection of carriers through a conductive insulator, e.g. Poole-Frankel conduction -- H10D 30/682 classifies field-effect transistors in which charge carriers are introduced into the channel by injection through a conductive insulating layer, a mechanism often described as Poole-Frankel conduction. This sub-category falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices within the broader electricity and semiconductor devices sections.
[H10D 30/683] programmed by tunnelling of carriers, e.g. Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors whose channel or gate is switched by quantum-mechanical tunnelling of charge carriers, such as Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling, enabling programmed or non-volatile operation. It includes devices where the tunnelling current is used to set the transistor state.
[H10D 30/684] programmed by hot carrier injection -- This subclass deals with field-effect transistors that are programmed by hot-carrier injection, a method where energetic carriers are driven into the gate dielectric to trap charge and alter the device's threshold voltage for data storage, as used in flash-type non-volatile memory.
[H10D 30/685] from the channel -- The CPC subclass H10D 30/685 covers field-effect transistors in which a control signal or bias is taken from, applied to, or originates within the semiconductor channel itself, rather than from a separate gate electrode. It pertains to inorganic FET structures where the channel region is used as the source of the controlling influence.
[H10D 30/686] using hot carriers produced by avalanche breakdown of PN junctions, e.g. floating gate avalanche injection MOS [FAMOS] -- H10D 30/686 covers semiconductor field-effect transistors that exploit hot carriers generated by avalanche breakdown of a pn junction to inject charge into a floating gate, as in floating-gate avalanche injection MOS (FAMOS) devices. The classification applies to circuits and methods that use this carrier-induced programming technique for memory or control functions.
[H10D 30/687] having more than two programming levels -- This subclass groups field-effect transistors that support more than two distinct programming or threshold levels, enabling multiple discrete charge or conductance states within a single device. Such multi-level programming is used for higher-density memory or fine-grained analog control.
[H10D 30/688] programmed by two single electrons -- H10D 30/688 classifies field-effect transistors in which the channel is controlled by the manipulation of exactly two individual electrons, i.e., devices programmed by two single electrons. It belongs to the inorganic electric semiconductor devices branch of the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 30/689] Vertical floating-gate IGFETs -- Vertical floating-gate IGFETs are a type of insulated-gate field-effect transistor in which the charge-storage gate is formed as a floating structure oriented perpendicular to the substrate, allowing current to flow vertically and enabling non-volatile memory functions. The device typically uses a semiconductor channel sandwiched between source and drain regions with the floating gate isolated by dielectric layers, providing a programmable threshold voltage.
[H10D 30/6891] characterised by the shapes, relative sizes or dispositions of the floating gate electrode -- H10D 30/6891 covers field-effect transistors in which the floating-gate electrode's geometry - its shape, size relationships or positioning - defines the device's operation. The classification applies to semiconductor structures such as flash memory where the floating gate stores charge. It is a subcategory of inorganic semiconductor devices within the CPC hierarchy.
[H10D 30/6892] having at least one additional gate other than the floating gate and the control gate, e.g. program gate, erase gate or select gate -- The code H10D 30/6892 designates field-effect transistors that incorporate at least one extra gate in addition to the conventional floating and control gates, for functions such as programming, erasing or selecting. These additional gates enable more complex operation and control of charge storage in semiconductor devices.
[H10D 30/6893] wherein the floating gate has multiple non-connected parts, e.g. multi-particle floating gate -- The CPC entry H10D 30/6893 pertains to field-effect transistors whose floating gate is formed from several separate, non-connected elements, such as a collection of particles. This structure is used to modify charge-storage behavior, adjust threshold characteristics, or enhance radiation tolerance in inorganic semiconductor devices.
[H10D 30/6894] having one gate at least partly in a trench -- This subclass covers field-effect transistors in which a single gate is at least partially embedded in a trench etched into the semiconductor or surrounding dielectric, providing enhanced electrostatic control and enabling more compact device geometries.
[H10D 30/69] IGFETs having charge trapping gate insulators, e.g. MNOS transistors -- The classification code H10D 30/69 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system pertains to insulated-gate field-effect transistors (IGFETs) that feature charge trapping gate insulators, such as those found in MNOS (Metal-Nitride-Oxide-Semiconductor) transistors.
[H10D 30/691] having more than two programming levels -- H10D 30/691 refers to field-effect transistors that are programmable with more than two distinct levels, such as multi-threshold or multi-state devices. These transistors can store several logic or analog values per cell, enabling higher-density memory or fine-grained control in circuit applications.
[H10D 30/693] Vertical IGFETs having charge trapping gate insulators -- This subclass covers vertical insulated-gate field-effect transistors whose gate dielectric contains charge-trapping regions, allowing programmable threshold voltage or non-volatile memory functionality. The devices are built with a vertical architecture, often used in power or three-dimensional applications, where trapped charge modulates the channel conductivity.
[H10D 30/694] characterised by the shapes, relative sizes or dispositions of the gate electrodes -- H10D 30/694 covers field-effect transistors in which the design of the gate electrodes - such as their shape, size and spatial arrangement - is used as a distinguishing feature. The classification applies to inorganic semiconductor devices where these geometric variations influence device performance.
[H10D 30/696] having at least one additional gate, e.g. program gate, erase gate or select gate -- This CPC subclass H10D 30/696 covers field-effect transistors that incorporate at least one additional gate, such as a program, erase or select gate, providing extra control of the channel or memory functions.
[H10D 30/697] having trapping at multiple separated sites, e.g. multi-particles trapping sites -- H10D 30/697 covers field-effect transistors whose operation involves charge carriers being trapped at several distinct locations within the semiconductor, such as multiple-particle trapping sites. The classification addresses designs that deliberately create or manage these separated traps to modify device characteristics like threshold voltage, stability or switching speed.
[H10D 30/699] having the gate at least partly formed in a trench -- H10D 30/699 covers field-effect transistors in which the gate electrode is at least partially formed within a trench etched into the semiconductor substrate, a design used to improve device control and scaling in inorganic electric semiconductor technologies.
[H10D 30/701] IGFETs having ferroelectric gate insulators, e.g. ferroelectric FETs -- Group H10D30/701 is impacted by reclassification into group H10D30/68 . Groups H10D30/701 and H10D30/68 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/711] having floating bodies -- The H10D 30/711 subclass covers field-effect transistors whose semiconductor body is electrically isolated, or "floating," rather than tied to a fixed potential. This design improves control of threshold voltage and reduces parasitic effects, often used in advanced MOSFET and related inorganic semiconductor devices. It falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, inorganic electric semiconductor devices, and FETs.
[H10D 30/721] having a gate-to-body connection, i.e. bulk dynamic threshold voltage IGFET   TFTs having gate-to-body connection  H10D30/6708 -- H10D 30/721 designates field-effect transistors that feature a direct electrical connection between the gate and the semiconductor body, resulting in a bulk dynamic threshold voltage. It is used for IGFETs and thin-film transistors where the gate-to-body tie influences device operation, and it falls under the broader sub-group H10D 30/6708.
[H10D 30/751] having composition variations in the channel regions -- Group H10D30/751 is impacted by reclassification into group H10D30/798 . Groups H10D30/751 and H10D30/798 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/791] Arrangements for exerting mechanical stress on the crystal lattice of the channel regions -- The code H10D 30/791 concerns structures that deliberately apply mechanical stress to the crystal lattice of the channel region in field-effect transistors in order to modify its electrical characteristics. Such arrangements may include strained-silicon layers, stress liners, or external mechanical actuators designed to enhance carrier mobility or adjust threshold voltage.
[H10D 30/792] comprising applied insulating layers, e.g. stress liners -- The subclass H10D 30/792 relates to field-effect transistors that include one or more applied insulating layers, such as stress-lin​ers, on the semiconductor structure. These insulating films are deposited to modify mechanical stress, enhance carrier mobility, and improve device performance, and they are typically added after the basic transistor geometry has been formed.
[H10D 30/794] comprising conductive materials, e.g. silicided source, drain or gate electrodes -- H10D 30/794 covers field-effect transistors in which conductive materials, for example silicided source, drain, or gate electrodes, are used to lower contact resistance and improve performance. The subclass is part of the inorganic electric-semiconductor device group and focuses on integrating such conductive layers into the transistor structure.
[H10D 30/795] being in lateral device isolation regions, e.g. STI -- This subclass covers features that are located within the lateral isolation areas of a field-effect transistor, such as structures formed in shallow-trench isolation (STI). It includes designs where active or passive elements are placed in the STI to provide lateral device isolation.
[H10D 30/796] having memorised stress for introducing strain in the channel regions, e.g. recrystallised polysilicon gates -- This subclass concerns field-effect transistors that incorporate a memorised stress, which is released to introduce strain in the channel regions and thereby enhance carrier mobility. The technique often uses recrystallised polysilicon gates that retain stress and transfer it to the channel during operation.
[H10D 30/797] being in source or drain regions, e.g. SiGe source or drain -- The entry H10D 30/797 pertains to field-effect transistors where the source or drain regions are formed from materials such as silicon-germanium alloy. It falls under inorganic electric semiconductor devices in the EPC hierarchy and addresses patents that use SiGe in the source or drain of the FET.
[H10D 30/798] being provided in or under the channel regions -- Group H10D30/798 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10D30/751 . Groups H10D30/751 and H10D30/798 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10D 30/80] FETs having rectifying junction gate electrodes -- These are field-effect transistors where the gate is formed by a rectifying junction, such as a p-n diode, that controls channel conductivity. The junction gate provides electrical isolation and voltage-dependent conduction, making it suitable for power and high-frequency applications.
[H10D 30/801] FETs having heterojunction gate electrodes -- H10D 30/801 covers field-effect transistors in which the gate electrode forms a heterojunction with the semiconductor channel, meaning the gate and channel are made from different semiconductor materials. Such designs exploit band-gap differences to improve control of the channel current and reduce leakage.
[H10D 30/803] Programmable transistors, e.g. having charge-trapping quantum well -- This subgroup covers field-effect transistors that can be programmed by trapping charge in a quantum-well layer, allowing the device's electrical characteristics such as threshold voltage to be set or altered. It includes flash-type FETs, charge-trap FETs and other programmable transistor structures that use an inorganic semiconductor quantum well for charge storage.
[H10D 30/83] FETs having PN junction gate electrodes -- This CPC subclass covers field-effect transistors where the gate electrode is formed as a p-n junction, allowing channel conductivity to be controlled by biasing that junction. It includes MOS-like structures with a doped gate region that creates a depletion zone, commonly used in power and analog applications.
[H10D 30/831] Vertical FETs having PN junction gate electrodes  Vertical SIT  H10D30/202 -- A vertical field-effect transistor whose gate electrode is created by a p-n junction, typically realized as a vertical silicon-controlled rectifier. The design places the channel and source-drain current flow perpendicular to the substrate, differentiating it from lateral FETs and from devices with metal or insulated gates. It falls within the inorganic electric semiconductor devices hierarchy.
[H10D 30/832] Thin-film junction FETs [JFET] -- Thin-film junction field-effect transistors (JFETs) are semiconductor devices in which a thin, planar layer of inorganic material forms the channel and the gate-source junction, allowing current modulation by an electric field. This subclass covers variants that use thin-film fabrication techniques for the channel, gate, and contacts, emphasizing planar geometries and integration methods distinct from bulk or bulk-crystal FET designs.
[H10D 30/87] FETs having Schottky gate electrodes, e.g. metal-semiconductor FETs [MESFET] -- The CPC subclass H10D 30/87 pertains to field-effect transistors that employ a Schottky gate electrode, such as metal-semiconductor FETs (MESFETs). It lies within the hierarchy of inorganic electric semiconductor devices under the broader category of electricity.
[H10D 30/871] Vertical FETs having Schottky gate electrodes   Vertical SIT or PBT  H10D30/202 -- The H10D 30/871 class covers vertical field-effect transistors in which the gate electrode is a Schottky metal-semiconductor junction, such as vertical static-induction transistors (SIT) or vertical power-bipolar transistors (PBT). These devices are inorganic semiconductor power components that use a vertical channel geometry and a Schottky gate to control current flow.
[H10D 30/873] having multiple gate electrodes -- This subgroup covers field-effect transistors that incorporate two or more gate electrodes to modulate the channel, providing enhanced control, threshold adjustment or multifunctional operation, often employed in power, radio-frequency or advanced logic applications.
[H10D 30/875] having thin-film semiconductor bodies -- The H10D 30/875 subgroup deals with field-effect transistors that incorporate thin-film inorganic semiconductor bodies as the active channel layer. It includes structures, manufacturing methods and functional features of FETs where the semiconductor layer is deposited as a thin film, such as poly-silicon, metal-oxide or other inorganic films. This classification is used for patents describing the design and fabrication of thin-film FET devices.
[H10D 30/877] having recessed gate electrodes -- H10D 30/877 refers to inorganic electric semiconductor field-effect transistors in which the gate electrode is recessed into the semiconductor substrate, a design that enhances channel control and reduces leakage currents. This subclass applies to devices such as MOSFETs where the gate sits below the surface of the chip.
[H10D 44/00] Charge transfer devices -- In this group, when the manufacture or treatment of a device is determined to be novel and non-obvious, the device itself is also classified.
[H10D 44/01] Manufacture or treatment -- The CPC subclass H10D 44/01 deals with processes for producing or treating inorganic charge-transfer semiconductor devices. It covers manufacturing steps, post-fabrication modifications and surface or bulk treatments applied to devices such as photodiodes, solar cells or other charge-transfer elements made from inorganic materials. This subclass falls under the hierarchy Electricity - Semiconductor devices - Inorganic electric semiconductor devices - Charge transfer devices.
[H10D 44/041] having insulated gates -- This subclass covers semiconductor charge-transfer devices whose gate electrode is electrically insulated from the channel, such as insulated-gate field-effect transistors. The insulated gate allows voltage control of current flow while preventing gate current, enabling efficient switching and amplification in electronic circuits.
[H10D 44/061] having Schottky gates -- The H10D 44/061 subclass covers inorganic electric semiconductor charge-transfer devices that incorporate Schottky gates. It includes structures where a metal-semiconductor junction forms the gate electrode, providing fast switching and low leakage. This classification is used for patents on Schottky-gate field-effect transistors and similar devices.
[H10D 44/40] Charge-coupled devices [CCD] -- Charge-coupled devices (CCDs) are semiconductor image sensors in which photo-generated charge is stored in an array of MOS capacitors and shifted sequentially across the chip to a readout register. The device converts light into an electrical signal with high image fidelity and is used in cameras, scientific instruments and other optical measurement systems.
[H10D 44/45] having field effect produced by insulated gate electrodes -- H10D 44/45 covers inorganic charge-transfer semiconductor devices in which an electric field is produced by a gate electrode that is insulated from the channel by a dielectric layer. Typical examples include MOSFETs and other insulated-gate transistors that control current flow via the field-effect.
[H10D 44/452] Input structures -- H10D 44/452 covers the design and arrangement of input structures for inorganic charge-transfer semiconductor devices such as CCDs and related sensors. It includes the configuration of input electrodes, gating elements and dielectric layers that receive and control charge packets before they are transferred through the device.
