[H10F 19/37] comprising means for obtaining partial light transmission through the integrated devices, or the assemblies of multiple devices, e.g. partially transparent thin-film photovoltaic modules for windows -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for obtaining partial light transmission through the integrated device or the assembly. [H10F 19/40] comprising photovoltaic cells in a mechanically stacked configuration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Photovoltaic cells stacked, one on top of the other, each having their own electrodes. [H10F 19/50] Integrated devices comprising at least one photovoltaic cell and other types of semiconductor or solid-state components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Photovoltaic cells integrated or directly associated with one or more electric components in or on the same substrate. Examples include a photovoltaic cell being used as an energy source to drive other semiconductor devices such as a transistor.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. [H10F 19/75] the bypass diodes being integrated or directly associated with the photovoltaic cells, e.g. formed in or on the same substrate -- H10F 19/75 concerns photovoltaic devices in which a bypass diode is built into or directly attached to the same substrate as the solar cell, providing integrated protection against reverse-bias currents that arise from shading or fault conditions.
[H10F 19/80] Encapsulations or containers for integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, having photovoltaic cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Packages, or parts thereof, for protecting the photovoltaic cells in the integrated device or assembly. For example, encapsulations, laminated coatings or materials formed between adjacent photovoltaic cells. [H10F 19/804] Materials of encapsulations -- H10F 19/804 concerns the encapsulating materials used in integrated inorganic semiconductor devices that incorporate at least one photovoltaic cell, especially those sensitive to infrared, visible light, or other short-wavelength radiation. It covers polymers, glasses, ceramics and similar protective layers that seal and protect the cell within the assembly.
[H10F 19/807] Double-glass encapsulation, e.g. photovoltaic cells arranged between front and rear glass sheets -- The CPC subclass H10F 19/807 covers the use of a double-glass encapsulation for photovoltaic cells, where the cells are sandwiched between a front and a rear glass sheet. This structure is applied to inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, light or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, providing enhanced mechanical protection and environmental sealing.
[H10F 19/85] Protective back sheets -- Protective back sheets are transparent or opaque layers applied to the rear of photovoltaic cells in integrated inorganic semiconductor assemblies that detect infrared, light, or short-wavelength radiation. They shield the cells from moisture, mechanical stress and contamination, enhancing durability and performance.
[H10F 19/90] Structures for connecting between photovoltaic cells, e.g. interconnections or insulating spacers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for providing the desired electrical connections between photovoltaic cells. For example, interconnections that provide a serial connection of photovoltaic cells, and insulating spacers that prevent short circuits. [H10F 19/902] for series or parallel connection of photovoltaic cells -- H10F 19/902 covers the electrical interconnection of photovoltaic cells within a module or assembly, specifically arrangements that connect the cells in series, in parallel, or a combination of both to achieve desired voltage or current characteristics. It applies to integrated inorganic semiconductor devices such as solar cells that are sensitive to infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation.
[H10F 19/904] characterised by the shapes of the structures -- H10F 19/904 is a CPC subgroup for inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation. It applies to integrated or multi-device assemblies containing at least one photovoltaic cell and is defined by the specific shapes of the semiconductor structures.
[H10F 19/906] characterised by the materials of the structures -- The H10F 19/906 subclass covers integrated inorganic semiconductor devices or assemblies-such as photovoltaic cells-that are defined primarily by the materials used in their structures. It applies to devices responsive to infrared, visible light, shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, or corpuscular radiation, where material composition is the distinguishing feature.
[H10F 19/908] for back-contact photovoltaic cells -- The subclass H10F 19/908 covers integrated or multicomponent devices that include at least one photovoltaic cell with rear-side (back) electrical contacts. It pertains to inorganic semiconductor devices that convert light, infrared or other short-wavelength radiation into electricity, and specifically to back-contact solar cells where the contacts are formed on the cell's back surface rather than the front.
[H10F 30/00] Radiation-controlled devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Individual inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to electromagnetic radiation or corpuscular radiation in which radiation controls the flow of current through the devices, e.g. to detect such radiation.  The group H10F30/00 itself covers devices sensitive to corpuscular radiation and having no potential barriers. The groups indented under H10F30/00 cover photoconductive devices or photodetectors having potential barriers.  **Glossary:** - photoconductive: the electrical conductivity of the material changes when light is absorbed by said material
[H10F 30/10] the devices being sensitive to infrared radiation, visible or ultraviolet radiation, and having no potential barriers, e.g. photoresistors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Photoconductive devices, having no junction potential barriers, which are sensitive to infrared, visible and UV light.
[H10F 30/15] comprising amorphous semiconductors -- The CPC subclass H10F 30/15 pertains to radiation-controlled devices that incorporate amorphous semiconductor materials. It falls under inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation, specifying the use of amorphous semiconductors in such detectors.
[H10F 30/20] the devices having potential barriers, e.g. phototransistors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Individual inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to electromagnetic radiation or corpuscular radiation in which radiation controls the flow of current through the devices, e.g. to detect such radiation, and comprising at least one potential barrier.  The groups indented under H10F30/20 cover photodiodes (one potential barrier), bipolar phototransistors (two potential barriers), photothyristors (three potential barriers) and field-effect phototransistors (junction or MIS potential barriers).
[H10F 30/21] the devices being sensitive to infrared, visible or ultraviolet radiation -- H10F 30/21 classifies inorganic semiconductor components that generate an electrical response when exposed to infrared, visible or ultraviolet radiation. It includes photodetectors, photodiodes, phototransistors and similar radiation-controlled devices used in imaging, sensing and communication.
[H10F 30/22] the devices having only one potential barrier, e.g. photodiodes -- A radiation-controlled semiconductor device that contains a single potential barrier, such as a photodiode, which converts incoming light or other short-wavelength radiation directly into an electrical signal. This class covers devices where only one junction or barrier is used to achieve the photoconductive or photovoltaic effect.
[H10F 30/2205] the potential barrier being a point contact -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor devices whose operation relies on a point-contact potential barrier, such as photodetectors or radiation-controlled switches that respond to infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. The localized junction created by the point contact modulates charge flow when exposed to radiation, enabling detection or switching functions.
[H10F 30/221] the potential barrier being a PN homojunction -- This CPC subclass H10F 30/221 covers inorganic semiconductor radiation-controlled devices where the governing potential barrier is a p-n homojunction formed from the same material. It includes devices such as infrared-sensing photodiodes and other detectors that rely on the PN homojunction barrier for operation.
[H10F 30/2212] the devices comprising active layers made of only Group II-VI materials, e.g. HgCdTe infrared photodiodes -- The code H10F 30/2212 covers radiation-controlled semiconductor devices whose active layer is composed exclusively of Group II-VI compounds, such as mercury-cadmium-telluride infrared photodiodes. These devices respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. They fall under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, and inorganic semiconductors sensitive to various radiation types.
[H10F 30/2215] the devices comprising active layers made of only Group III-V materials -- These are inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation, whose active layers are formed solely from Group III-V compounds such as GaAs, InP or AlGaAs. Using only III-V materials gives the devices a tunable band-gap and high carrier mobility, making them suitable for photodetectors, laser diodes and other radiation-controlled components.
[H10F 30/2218] the devices comprising active layers made of only Group IV-VI materials -- H10F 30/2218 covers radiation-controlled semiconductor devices whose active layers are formed solely from Group IV-VI compounds. These inorganic devices are sensitive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. The entry sits under electricity → semiconductor devices → inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to radiation.
[H10F 30/222] the potential barrier being a PN heterojunction -- This subclass covers radiation-controlled semiconductor devices in which the device's potential barrier is formed by a pn heterojunction, a junction between dissimilar semiconductor materials that creates a built-in electric field used for detection or modulation of infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation. It relates to inorganic semiconductor devices that convert or respond to such radiation.
[H10F 30/223] the potential barrier being a PIN barrier -- The subclass H10F 30/223 covers semiconductor devices in which the controlling potential barrier is formed by a PIN (p-type/intrinsic/n-type) structure, a common design for radiation-controlled components such as infrared, light or short-wavelength photodetectors. It is part of the broader group of inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to various forms of radiation.
[H10F 30/2235] the devices comprising Group IV amorphous materials -- H10F 30/2235 classifies radiation-controlled devices that incorporate group IV amorphous semiconductor materials, such as amorphous silicon or germanium alloys, as the active element. These devices operate by detecting infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, and include sensors and detectors whose response is driven by the material's photoconductive or photovoltaic properties.
[H10F 30/225] the potential barrier working in avalanche mode, e.g. avalanche photodiodes -- The entry H10F 30/225 relates to inorganic semiconductor devices whose internal potential barrier is operated in avalanche mode, meaning the barrier undergoes impact ionisation under high reverse bias. It primarily covers avalanche photodiodes and similar radiation-controlled detectors that amplify incoming photons by avalanche multiplication, and falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices and radiation-sensitive inorganic semiconductors.
[H10F 30/2255] in which the active layers form heterostructures, e.g. SAM structures -- H10F 30/2255 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that are controlled by radiation and whose active layers are built as heterostructures, for example semiconductor-insulator-semiconductor (SAM) structures. The classification pertains to devices sensitive to infrared, visible light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation, placed under the electricity → semiconductor devices hierarchy.
[H10F 30/227] the potential barrier being a Schottky barrier -- The CPC entry H10F 30/227 pertains to inorganic semiconductor devices that are controlled by radiation-such as infrared, visible light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation-where the active potential barrier is a Schottky barrier. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity, semiconductor devices, and radiation-controlled devices.
[H10F 30/2275] being a metal-semiconductor-metal [MSM] Schottky barrier -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation and employ a metal-semiconductor-metal (MSM) structure forming a Schottky barrier. The MSM configuration is used to modulate current flow in response to incident radiation, providing a radiation-controlled device within the broader H10F hierarchy.
[H10F 30/24] the devices having only two potential barriers, e.g. bipolar phototransistors -- The subclass H10F 30/24 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that are controlled by radiation and contain only two potential barriers, such as bipolar phototransistors, where light-induced carriers modify the current flow across a simple p-n-p or n-p-n structure. These devices respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and are used where a minimal junction configuration is desired.
[H10F 30/245] Bipolar phototransistors -- The class H10F 30/245 covers bipolar phototransistors, which integrate a bipolar transistor structure with a photosensitive region so that incident light directly modulates the transistor's current. These devices convert photons from infrared, visible or ultraviolet radiation into electrical signals and are used in light-detection and imaging applications.
[H10F 30/26] the devices having three or more potential barriers, e.g. photothyristors -- H10F 30/26 designates semiconductor devices that contain three or more successive potential barriers, typically formed by pn or metal-semiconductor junctions, and that operate as radiation-controlled switches such as photothyristors. These components are used where light or other short-wavelength radiation triggers or modulates current flow.
[H10F 30/263] Photothyristors -- Photothyristors are semiconductor switches that combine the characteristics of a thyristor with light-sensitivity, allowing them to be triggered or modulated by optical radiation such as infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength light. They belong to the group of radiation-controlled inorganic semiconductor devices used in power control, optical sensing and photonic switching applications.Photothyristors are semiconductor switches that combine the characteristics of a thyristor with light-sensitivity, allowing them to be triggered or modulated by optical radiation such as infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength light. They belong to the group of radiation-controlled inorganic semiconductor devices used in power control, optical sensing and photonic switching applications.
[H10F 30/2635] Static induction photothyristors -- Static induction photothyristors are semiconductor switching devices that combine the high-current capability of static induction thyristors with light sensitivity, allowing them to be triggered by optical radiation. They belong to inorganic semiconductor devices responsive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation and are used in power control and optical isolation.
[H10F 30/28] the devices being characterised by field-effect operation, e.g. junction field-effect phototransistors -- H10F 30/28 covers semiconductor radiation-controlled devices that operate by the field-effect, such as junction field-effect phototransistors, where incident light or other radiation modulates a channel's conductivity. These components are used as photodetectors in infrared and optical applications.
[H10F 30/282] Insulated-gate field-effect transistors [IGFET], e.g. MISFET [metal-insulator-semiconductor field-effect transistor] phototransistors -- H10F 30/282 covers insulated-gate field-effect transistors, such as metal-insulator-semiconductor (MIS) FETs, that are designed to function as phototransistors. These devices integrate a gate insulated by a dielectric layer, allowing control of current flow by incident infrared, visible or other short-wavelength radiation. It falls under radiation-controlled semiconductor devices sensitive to light or electromagnetic radiation.
[H10F 30/2823] the devices being conductor-insulator-semiconductor devices, e.g. diodes or charge-coupled devices [CCD]  Insulated-gate field-effect transistors  H10F30/282 -- This subclass covers conductor-insulator-semiconductor structures such as diodes, charge-coupled devices and insulated-gate field-effect transistors used as radiation-controlled components. These devices exploit the interface between a conductive layer, an insulating layer, and a semiconductor to modulate electrical signals in response to infrared, light, or other short-wavelength radiation.
[H10F 30/283] the devices having Schottky gates -- This subgroup comprises inorganic semiconductor devices whose control element is a Schottky gate, a metal-semiconductor junction that forms a rectifying barrier. Such gates are used in radiation-controlled switches, photodetectors and other devices responsive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation.
[H10F 30/2837] CCDs having Schottky gates -- This subclass covers charge-coupled devices that incorporate Schottky gate structures for controlling charge transfer. Such devices are used as infrared or short-wavelength photodetectors, falling under inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to various radiation.
[H10F 30/2843] Schottky gate FETs, e.g. photo MESFETs -- A Schottky-gate field-effect transistor (FET) uses a metal-semiconductor Schottky barrier as the gate electrode, providing fast switching and low leakage. When implemented as a photo-MESFET, the device's channel conductivity is modulated by infrared or visible light, enabling radiation-controlled operation. This class covers such inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to optical or short-wavelength radiation.
[H10F 30/285] the devices having PN homojunction gates -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor devices that are controlled by radiation such as infrared, visible light or short-wavelength electromagnetic waves, where the gate is formed by a pn homojunction. The pn homojunction gate modulates charge flow directly in response to the incident photons, enabling radiation-sensitive operation of devices like photodiodes or phototransistors.
[H10F 30/2857] CCDs having PN homojunction gates -- This CPC subclass covers charge-coupled devices that incorporate pn homojunction gates to control charge transfer. The devices belong to inorganic semiconductor structures responsive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation and are used as radiation-controlled elements in imaging and sensing applications.
[H10F 30/2863] Field-effect phototransistors having PN homojunction gates -- Field-effect phototransistors that incorporate a pn homojunction as the gate structure, where illumination of the junction modulates the channel current, are classified under inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, light, or short-wavelength radiation. These radiation-controlled devices combine transistor switching with photodetection.
[H10F 30/287] the devices having PN heterojunction gates -- H10F 30/287 classifies semiconductor devices that employ a pn heterojunction gate to control current flow, typically used in radiation-sensitive components such as infrared detectors and other short-wavelength or corpuscular radiation sensors. The gate structure combines differing semiconductor materials to form a heterojunction, enabling enhanced modulation of electrical characteristics.
[H10F 30/2873] CCDs having PN heterojunction gates -- This subclass covers charge-coupled devices that use pn-heterojunction gates to control charge transfer within the pixel array. The pn-heterojunction gate structure improves sensitivity and speed for infrared, visible or short-wavelength radiation detectors, and is employed in inorganic semiconductor image sensors.
[H10F 30/2877] Field-effect phototransistors having PN heterojunction gates -- These are field-effect phototransistors that incorporate a PN heterojunction as the gate electrode, allowing incident light or infrared radiation to modulate the channel current. The PN junction both senses photons and controls the transistor's conduction, enabling radiation-controlled operation in inorganic semiconductor devices.
[H10F 30/288] the devices being sensitive to multiple wavelengths, e.g. multi-spectrum radiation detection devices -- H10F 30/288 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to several different wavelength ranges, such as detectors that can sense infrared, visible and ultraviolet radiation simultaneously. These radiation-controlled devices are used in multi-spectral detection and monitoring applications.
[H10F 30/289] the devices being transparent or semi-transparent devices -- H10F 30/289 covers radiation-controlled inorganic semiconductor devices that are designed to be transparent or semi-transparent, allowing incident light or other radiation to pass through the device while still providing detection or conversion functions. Such devices are used in applications like infrared cameras, photovoltaic windows, or sensor arrays where light transmission and semiconductor activity are combined.
[H10F 30/29] the devices being sensitive to radiation having very short wavelengths, e.g. X-rays, gamma-rays or corpuscular radiation -- H10F 30/29 covers semiconductor devices that respond to radiation with extremely short wavelengths, such as X-rays, gamma rays or other corpuscular particles. The classification applies to detectors, sensors and similar components whose operation is triggered by these high-energy radiations, distinguishing them from devices sensitive to longer-wavelength light.
[H10F 30/292] Bulk-effect radiation detectors, e.g. Ge-Li compensated PIN gamma-ray detectors -- H10F 30/292 covers bulk-effect radiation detectors based on inorganic semiconductor crystals such as germanium-lithium compensated PIN structures that detect gamma rays by measuring charge generated throughout the crystal volume. These devices are used where high-energy photon detection and spectroscopic precision are required, typical in nuclear and astrophysical instrumentation.
[H10F 30/2925] Li-compensated PIN gamma-ray detectors -- Li-compensated PIN gamma-ray detectors are inorganic semiconductor devices that use a lithium-doped PIN structure to sense high-energy gamma photons. The lithium compensation reduces dark current and improves charge collection, allowing precise detection of ionizing radiation for applications such as nuclear instrumentation, medical imaging and radiation monitoring.
[H10F 30/295] Surface barrier or shallow PN junction radiation detectors, e.g. surface barrier alpha-particle detectors -- H10F 30/295 covers inorganic semiconductor detectors that use a surface-barrier or shallow p-n junction to sense ionising particles such as alpha particles. These devices convert the energy of incident radiation into an electrical signal and are employed in radiation-controlled applications.
[H10F 30/2955] Shallow PN junction radiation detectors -- The H10F 30/2955 entry covers radiation-controlled semiconductor devices that use a shallow p-n junction as the detecting element. These detectors respond to infrared, visible, ultraviolet or other short-wavelength radiation by generating charge carriers across the thin junction, enabling fast, low-noise sensing of light or other electromagnetic energy.
[H10F 30/298] the devices being characterised by field-effect operation, e.g. MIS type detectors -- H10F 30/298 covers radiation-controlled semiconductor devices that operate by a field-effect mechanism, notably metal-insulator-semiconductor (MIS) type detectors. These devices belong to the class of inorganic semiconductors sensitive to infrared, visible light, shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, or corpuscular radiation, and they convert the absorbed radiation into an electrical signal via the field effect.
[H10F 30/301] the devices being sensitive to very short wavelength, e.g. being sensitive to X-rays, gamma-rays or corpuscular radiation -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10F 30/301 pertains to inorganic semiconductor devices that are sensitive to very short wavelengths, including X-rays, gamma-rays, and corpuscular radiation. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy focusing on radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices.
[H10F 39/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one element covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[H10F 39/011] Manufacture or treatment of image sensors covered by group  H10F39/12 -- The code H10F 39/011 covers manufacturing and post-production treatments applied to image sensors that contain inorganic semiconductor elements responsive to infrared, visible or short-wave electromagnetic radiation. It pertains to processes such as wafer-level fabrication, doping, surface passivation, packaging and annealing steps that create or improve the performance of these sensor devices.
[H10F 39/014] of CMOS image sensors -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10F 39/014 pertains to integrated devices or assemblies that include at least one inorganic semiconductor device sensitive to infrared, light, or shorter wavelength electromagnetic radiation, specifically focusing on CMOS image sensors.
[H10F 39/016] of thin-film-based image sensors -- H10F 39/016 covers thin-film image sensors that are part of inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, visible light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. The classification applies to integrated or multi-device assemblies that incorporate at least one thin-film sensor element. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity, semiconductor devices, inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to radiation.
[H10F 39/018] of hybrid image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Manufacture or treatment of hybrid-type imagers, i.e. image sensors having two substrates that are connected together, wherein the sensing elements are in one substrate and the driving circuitry is in the other substrate.
[H10F 39/021] of image sensors having active layers comprising only Group III-V materials, e.g. GaAs, AlGaAs or InP -- H10F 39/021 covers image sensors whose active layers are made exclusively from group-III-V compound semiconductors such as gallium arsenide, aluminum gallium arsenide or indium phosphide. These devices fall under inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, light or short-wave electromagnetic radiation and are integrated or assembled with other components.
[H10F 39/022] of image sensors having active layers comprising only Group II-VI materials, e.g. CdS, ZnS or CdTe -- H10F 39/022 is a CPC subgroup for image sensors whose photosensitive layers consist exclusively of Group II-VI compound semiconductors, such as CdS, ZnS or CdTe. These devices are classified within inorganic semiconductor components that respond to infrared, visible or short-wave electromagnetic radiation. The code is used for integrated or multi-device assemblies that incorporate at least one such element.
[H10F 39/024] of coatings or optical elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Manufacture or treatment of coatings (e.g. antireflective coatings, filters or shielding) or other optical elements (e.g. microlenses).
[H10F 39/026] Wafer-level processing -- The subclass H10F 39/026 covers wafer-level processing steps performed on inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. It includes deposition, patterning, etching, cleaning and other treatments applied to the wafer before it is diced into individual components.
[H10F 39/028] performed after manufacture of the image sensors, e.g. annealing, gettering of impurities, short-circuit elimination or recrystallisation -- This subclass covers post-manufacturing treatments applied to inorganic semiconductor image sensors, such as annealing, impurity gettering, short-circuit elimination and recrystallisation, undertaken to improve device performance or yield.
[H10F 39/10] Integrated devices -- H10F 39/10 refers to integrated devices that incorporate inorganic semiconductor elements sensitive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, such as combined photodiode-amplifier or detector-oscillator assemblies. It covers configurations wherein at least one component detects or reacts to light or other short-wave radiation within a single package or module.
[H10F 39/103] the at least one element covered by  H10F30/00  having potential barriers, e.g. integrated devices comprising photodiodes or phototransistors -- H10F 39/103 covers integrated semiconductor devices that incorporate at least one element from the H10F 30/00 group-photodiodes, phototransistors or similar structures-with built-in potential barriers, for example assemblies that combine such photosensitive elements into a single package. The classification applies to inorganic semiconductor components designed to detect infrared, visible light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation.
[H10F 39/107] having multiple elements covered by  H10F30/00  in a repetitive configuration, e.g. radiation detectors comprising photodiode arrays -- H10F 39/107 covers integrated semiconductor devices that include multiple elements defined in H10F 30/00 arranged in a repetitive configuration, such as radiation detectors built as photodiode arrays.
[H10F 39/12] Image sensors -- H10F 39/12 covers integrated semiconductor image sensors that detect infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and convert it into electrical signals. The class includes CCD, CMOS and other photodetector arrays fabricated as monolithic or hybrid assemblies, used in cameras, remote-sensing and industrial imaging.
[H10F 39/15] Charge-coupled device [CCD] image sensors -- Charge-coupled device (CCD) image sensors are integrated semiconductor assemblies that convert incident light into electronic charge and transfer the charge across the chip to a readout register. They are inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to photons and other short-wavelength radiation, widely used in digital cameras, astronomy and scientific instrumentation.
[H10F 39/151] Geometry or disposition of pixel elements, address lines or gate electrodes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates photoelectric conversion elements (110 - 112) connected to wiring lines (106 - 108), with the disposition thereof being chosen such that the distance between the photoelectric conversion elements and the wiring lines are shifted based on pupil correction (see where [106 and 110] overlap more than [107 and 111] overlap, which overlap more than [106 and 112]).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates an image sensor having a buried photodiode (23), buried gate electrode (22) and buried floating diffusion (16) in such a geometry that that buried gate can be used to efficiently transfer charges to the buried floating diffusion through path R1 at a depth equal to that of the photodiode.
[H10F 39/1515] Optical shielding -- This subclass refers to measures and structures that block or filter unwanted light in integrated inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible, or shorter-wavelength radiation. It includes designs of shields, filters, or opaque layers incorporated in the device or its packaging to reduce stray radiation and improve performance.
[H10F 39/152] One-dimensional array CCD image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CCD-type image sensors having a linear arrangement of the pixels, e.g. fax heads or photocopiers.
[H10F 39/153] Two-dimensional or three-dimensional array CCD image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CCD-type image sensors having pixels arranged in a 2D matrix or in a 3D matrix.
[H10F 39/1532] Frame-interline transfer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CCD image sensors having a combination of interline transfer together with frame transfer.  Each photodiode has a parallel CCD region which shifts charge vertically to a storage 2D matrix (one storage pixel per one photosensitive pixel). The charges stored in the storage matrix are then read out.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[H10F 39/1534] Interline transfer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CCD image sensors wherein each photodiode has a parallel CCD storage region covered by an opaque mask. After image data has been collected and transferred to the adjacent CCD storage region charge is CCD-shifted vertically to the readout IC.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor having columns of photo-sensitive areas adjacent to columns of storage regions (denoted "CCD registers").
[H10F 39/1536] Frame transfer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CCD image sensor wherein a 2D storage area is adjacent to the photosensitive 2D array, having a storage pixel per photosensitive pixel. The charges collected are transferred in parallel to the storage area for readout.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor having a 2D storage area adjacent to a 2D photo-sensitive array.
[H10F 39/1538] Time-delay and integration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  CCD image sensors of the time delay and integration (TDI) type, wherein the pixel clock rate must be matched to the image velocity.
[H10F 39/154] Charge-injection device [CID] image sensors  H10F39/156 ,  H10F39/157  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Charge-injection device [CID] image sensors wherein the object to be imaged is in contact with the sensor and typically use LEDs for the illumination of the object to be imaged.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - CCD or CID colour image sensors -> H10F39/156 - CCD or CID infrared image sensors -> H10F39/157
[H10F 39/156] CCD or CID colour image sensors -- H10F 39/156 covers colour image sensors based on charge-coupled or charge-injection devices that are built as integrated semiconductor structures. These sensors use inorganic semiconductor elements that respond to infrared, visible light, or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, and may be combined with other components in a single chip or module.
[H10F 39/157] CCD or CID infrared image sensors -- This subclass covers charge-coupled (CCD) or charge-injection (CID) image sensors that are specifically designed to detect infrared radiation. It includes integrated semiconductor devices that convert infrared photons into electrical signals for use in thermal imaging, night-vision, and other infrared sensing applications.
[H10F 39/1575] of the hybrid type -- H10F 39/1575 covers integrated assemblies that contain at least one hybrid inorganic semiconductor element capable of detecting infrared, visible light or other short-wavelength or corpuscular radiation. The hybrid type combines different semiconductor materials or structures within a single device to provide radiation sensitivity in a compact package.
[H10F 39/158] having arrangements for blooming suppression -- H10F 39/158 covers integrated inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation and include built-in features to suppress blooming, i.e., mechanisms that prevent excessive charge spreading or over-exposure from intense radiation events.
[H10F 39/159] comprising a photoconductive layer deposited on the CCD structure -- This subclass covers charge-coupled device (CCD) structures that have a photoconductive layer deposited on them, forming an integrated detector that converts incident light into charge for readout. The photoconductive coating extends the device's sensitivity to infrared, visible or other short-wavelength radiation while being part of an inorganic semiconductor assembly.
[H10F 39/18] Complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor [CMOS] image sensors -- CMOS image sensors are integrated semiconductor devices that convert light into electrical signals using complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor technology. They combine photodiodes and readout circuitry on a single chip, providing low power consumption, high speed and on-chip processing for cameras and other imaging systems.
[H10F 39/182] Colour image sensors -- Colour image sensors are integrated semiconductor devices that combine an array of light-sensitive elements, each equipped with colour-filter structures, to capture visual information in separate red, green and blue channels. They convert incident radiation into electrical signals and are employed in digital cameras, smartphones and other imaging systems for colour photography and video.
[H10F 39/1825] Multicolour image sensors having stacked structure, e.g. NPN, NPNPN or multiple quantum well [MQW] structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor having stacked diodes sense blue, green and red light.
[H10F 39/184] Infrared image sensors -- This subclass covers infrared image sensors built from inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, typically implemented as integrated circuits or assemblies containing at least one such element. It includes focal-plane arrays, photodiode or phototransistor arrays, and related structures used for thermal imaging, night-vision, and other IR detection applications.
[H10F 39/1843] of the hybrid type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hybrid image sensors for sensing infrared radiation, having two substrates that are connected together, wherein the sensing elements are in one substrate and the driving circuitry is in the other substrate.
[H10F 39/1847] Multispectral infrared image sensors having a stacked structure, e.g. NPN, NPNPN or multiple quantum well [MQW] structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Image sensors having generally a stacked structure for sensing different parts of the infrared spectrum, e.g. for sensing long-wave IR [LWIR], middle wavelength IR [MWIR] and short wavelength IR [SWIR].
[H10F 39/186] having arrangements for blooming suppression -- The code H10F 39/186 belongs to the CPC section H (Electricity) subclass H10F covering inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation. It refers to integrated or multi-device assemblies that incorporate features to suppress blooming, i.e. the unwanted spread of charge or light in the detector.
[H10F 39/1865] Overflow drain structures -- H10F 39/1865 covers overflow-drain structures incorporated in integrated inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation. The classification refers to designs that provide a path for excess charge carriers or fluid to be removed, improving device stability and response in photodetectors, image sensors and related optoelectronic assemblies.
[H10F 39/189] X-ray, gamma-ray or corpuscular radiation imagers -- H10F 39/189 relates to semiconductor devices that detect or form images using high-energy radiation such as X-rays, gamma-rays or particle beams. It covers integrated or assembled inorganic semiconductor detectors, arrays and associated circuitry designed to convert these corpuscular radiations into electrical signals for imaging applications.
[H10F 39/1892] Direct radiation image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  X-ray, gamma-ray or corpuscular radiation image sensor wherein the semiconductor layers directly convert the incoming radiation into charges, without need of a scintillator.
[H10F 39/1895] of the hybrid type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hybrid image sensors for sensing X-ray, gamma-ray or corpuscular radiation, having two substrates that are connected together, wherein the sensing elements are in one substrate and the driving circuitry is in the other substrate.
[H10F 39/1898] Indirect radiation image sensors, e.g. using luminescent members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  X-ray, gamma-ray or corpuscular radiation image sensors having a scintillator (e.g. an ionic solid or phosphor) which converts incoming radiation into visible light, which is then detected.
[H10F 39/191] Photoconductor image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Image sensors wherein incoming radiation changes the conductivity of a photoconductive layer and the change in conductivity is measured to determine the incoming radiation.
[H10F 39/192] Colour image sensors -- Colour image sensors are integrated semiconductor assemblies that convert light into electrical signals, typically comprising arrays of photodiodes each covered by colour filters to separate red, green and blue components. They are used in cameras and imaging systems to capture colour information directly in the sensor.
[H10F 39/193] Infrared image sensors -- Infrared image sensors are integrated semiconductor devices or assemblies that contain at least one element designed to detect infrared radiation, converting the energy into electrical signals for imaging applications. They are typically fabricated from inorganic semiconductor materials sensitive to short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and are used in thermal cameras, night vision, and scientific instruments.
[H10F 39/1935] of the hybrid type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hybrid photoconductive image sensors having two substrates that are connected together, wherein the photoconductive sensing elements are in one substrate and the driving circuitry is in the other substrate.
[H10F 39/194] having arrangements for blooming suppression -- This classification covers integrated semiconductor devices such as CCD or CMOS image sensors that incorporate anti-blooming arrangements to prevent charge overflow from over-exposed pixels. The configurations may include gating structures, drains or other circuitry that limit the spread of photo-generated charge, preserving image quality under high illumination.
[H10F 39/1945] Overflow drain structures -- Overflow drain structures are design features in inorganic semiconductor photodetectors sensitive to infrared, visible or short-wavelength radiation that provide a low-impedance path for excess charge carriers generated under high illumination, preventing device saturation and improving linearity. They are usually implemented as heavily doped drains or mesa-type regions adjacent to the active area.
[H10F 39/195] X-ray, gamma-ray or corpuscular radiation imagers -- Integrated devices or assemblies that incorporate inorganic semiconductor elements capable of detecting X-ray, gamma-ray or other particle radiation and converting it into an electrical signal for imaging purposes, often used in medical, security and scientific imaging systems.Integrated devices or assemblies that incorporate inorganic semiconductor elements capable of detecting X-ray, gamma-ray or other particle radiation and converting it into an electrical signal for imaging purposes, often used in medical, security and scientific imaging systems.
[H10F 39/196] Junction field effect transistor [JFET] image sensors; Static induction transistor [SIT] image sensors -- This subclass covers image sensors that employ a junction field-effect transistor or a static-induction transistor as the photosensitive element, where the semiconductor device converts incident infrared, visible or short-wave radiation into an electrical signal. It includes integrated or hybrid assemblies containing at least one such JFET or SIT element.
[H10F 39/197] Bipolar transistor image sensors -- H10F 39/197 classifies bipolar transistor image sensors, which are integrated semiconductor devices that use bipolar transistor structures to convert light or other short-wavelength radiation into electrical signals for imaging applications. They belong to the group of inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, visible, or ultraviolet radiation.
[H10F 39/198] Contact-type image sensors [CIS] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Image sensors having integrated light sources, wherein the light emitted from the integrated light source is reflected on the object to be detected and enters the image sensors. Examples include scanning heads, photocopier heads and fingerprint detectors.
[H10F 39/199] Back-illuminated image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Image sensors wherein radiation (e.g. light) impinges from the surface of the semiconductor wafer opposite to the surface having the imaging structure.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates light (250) illuminated on the surface of the semiconductor wafer that is opposite to the surface having photodiodes (200) therein.
[H10F 39/80] Constructional details of image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structural or functional details of semiconductor imaging structures such as encapsulations, geometry of disposition of passive and active elements, lenses, isolation, etc., whenever they are specific for semiconductor imaging devices, i.e. they solve problems specific to semiconductor imaging devices.
[H10F 39/802] Geometry or disposition of elements in pixels, e.g. address-lines or gate electrodes -- Groups H10F39/802 - H10F39/8027 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10F39/80 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10F 39/8023] Disposition of the elements in pixels, e.g. smaller elements in the centre of the imager compared to larger elements at the periphery -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor having optical waveguides (106) having two components (106a and 106b) having differing index of refraction. In the pixels near the periphery of the image sensor, regions (106b) are large, and in the pixels near the middle of the image sensor, regions (106b) are small.
[H10F 39/8027] Geometry of the photosensitive area -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates the implanted photosensitive region (432) of a photodiode having a shape (see dotted lines) determined by mask (305).
[H10F 39/803] Pixels having integrated switching, control, storage or amplification elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pixels of active pixel sensors [APS], wherein each pixel comprises a photodetecting element and amplifications means.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a. [H10F 39/8033] Photosensitive area -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of the photosensitive area of an active pixel sensor, such as doping profiles, etc.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a photodiode (121) comprising photosensitive regions (133 and 134C). The region (134) extends below a buried channel (135) in a way that helps prevent charge in (121) from mixing into other regions.
[H10F 39/8037] the integrated elements comprising a transistor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Active pixel sensors characterised by a transistor (e.g. transfer transistor, reset transistor or source follower) in the pixel.
[H10F 39/80373] characterised by the gate of the transistor -- H10F 39/80373 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that are sensitive to infrared, visible, ultraviolet or other radiation and are presented as integrated structures where the transistor's gate is a defining feature. The classification is applied to devices such as photodetectors or image sensors that incorporate a gate-controlled element within the semiconductor assembly.
[H10F 39/80377] characterised by the channel of the transistor, e.g. channel having a doping gradient -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10F 39/80377 pertains to integrated semiconductor devices sensitive to various types of radiation, specifically focusing on transistors with channels that have specific characteristics such as a doping gradient. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under the broader categories of Electricity and Semiconductor Devices.
[H10F 39/804] Containers or encapsulations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a flexible container (9) comprising copper (13a), adhesive (13b), and polymer (14a), that is attached to metal pads (24) of an image sensor and is wrapped therearound. It further shows an encapsulation material (30), such as epoxy, that seals the container (9) to the top of the image sensor.
[H10F 39/805] Coatings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coatings within the image sensor, e.g. interlayer dielectrics (ILD) or antireflective coatings (ARC).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor having coatings such as a transparent film (30) having a carefully chosen apex angle such that a lens may be formed thereon by deposition of other thin films.
[H10F 39/8053] Colour filters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Colour filters for image sensors, e.g. arrangement of multiple filters in a Bayer pattern.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a colour filter (23) having blue colour filter components (2B), red colour filter components (2R) and green colour filter components (2G), in a pattern known as a Bayer array, wherein each green colour filter component is surrounded by red and blue filter components.

[H10F 39/806] Optical elements or arrangements associated with the image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical elements such as lenses, reflectors or light-guiding structures within image sensors.

[H10F 39/8067] Reflectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elements that reflect light in the image sensor, for example reflecting light towards the light detecting elements. [H10F 39/807] Pixel isolation structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrical or thermal isolation structures between pixels.
[H10F 39/809] of hybrid image sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a hybrid image sensor, having photodiodes in sensor wafer (40), connected to control circuitry in CMOS wafer (41). The details of this hybrid image sensor, such as the wafer-to-wafer interconnection (56) and the metal shield (100), are covered by this subgroup.
[H10F 39/811] Interconnections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Interconnections for image sensors, e.g. wirings that connect an imaging substrate with its driving substrate; or that connect an image sensor with the external driving circuitry; or that connect between various elements within the image sensor.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor (100) having interconnections including wiring portions (12), conductive pillars (9) and pad portions (17).
[H10F 39/812] Arrangements for transferring the charges in the image sensor perpendicular to the imaging plane, e.g. buried regions used to transfer generated charges to circuitry under the photosensitive region -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements in the image sensor for transferring charges perpendicular to the imaging plane, e.g. circuitry beneath the photosensitive area that move the charges away from the sensing elements.
[H10F 39/813] Electronic components shared by multiple pixels, e.g. one amplifier shared by two pixels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electronic components shared by multiple pixels, e.g. transistors or doped regions shared by adjacent pixels.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image sensor (31) having photodiodes PD1 through PD4 that share a floating diffusion region FD, which is connected to the gate of amplifier transistor Tr3.
[H10F 39/90] Assemblies of multiple devices -- Groups H10F39/90 and H10F39/95 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10W90/00 . Groups H10W90/00 , H10F39/90 and H10F39/95 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10F 39/95] comprising at least one integrated device covered by group
[H10F 55/00] Other devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[H10F 55/10] wherein the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices control the electric light source, e.g. image converters, image amplifiers or image storage devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices wherein the signal from the photodetector is used for controlling the emission of light from the light source (e.g. image converters).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an image-converting device having a photoconductive layer (6) associated with an electroluminescent layer (12) between back electrode (7) and transparent electrode (2). Radiation into the image-converting device decreases the resistivity of the photoconductive layer (6) so that the electroluminescent layer (12) emits light by the application of an electric field.
[H10F 55/15] wherein the radiation-sensitive devices and the electric light source are all semiconductor devices -- H10F 55/15 covers arrangements in which both the radiation-sensitive element and the electric light source are implemented as semiconductor devices. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible, ultraviolet or other short-wavelength radiation, and pertains to integrated or coupled semiconductor components used together in a single system.
[H10F 55/155] formed in, or on, a common substrate -- H10F 55/155 pertains to inorganic semiconductor devices that are formed in, or on, a common substrate, meaning that two or more active components such as detectors, emitters or other elements are fabricated together on the same chip. It is used for devices sensitive to infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation or corpuscular radiation, and falls under the broader class of semiconductor devices within the electricity field.
[H10F 55/16] wherein the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices have no potential barriers -- Inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation without incorporating p-n junctions or other potential-barrier structures. These sensors rely on bulk photoconductivity or similar mechanisms, where the radiation directly alters the conductivity of a uniform semiconductor material.
[H10F 55/165] wherein the electric light source comprises semiconductor devices having potential barriers, e.g. light emitting diodes -- H10F 55/165 concerns electric lighting units whose light source is formed from semiconductor devices that incorporate potential barriers, such as p-n junctions, exemplified by light-emitting diodes. The subclass groups lamps and fixtures that generate illumination by electroluminescence from these barrier-based semiconductor structures.
[H10F 55/17] wherein the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices have potential barriers -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor detectors such as photodiodes, phototransistors and similar devices that convert infrared, visible or short-wavelength radiation into electrical signals and whose operation depends on a potential barrier - for example a p-n junction, Schottky barrier, heterojunction or quantum-well barrier - that separates charge-carrier regions.
[H10F 55/18] wherein the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices and the electric light source share a common body having dual-functionality of light emission and light detection -- This subclass covers integrated optoelectronic components in which a single semiconductor body simultaneously functions as a light-emitting source and as a detector of infrared, visible or short-wave radiation. It applies to devices that combine a light-emitting diode or similar emitter with a photodiode or similar sensor in the same structure, enabling both emission and detection.
[H10F 55/20] wherein the electric light source controls the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices, e.g. optocouplers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices wherein the light from the light source is sent to the photodetector, which provides an electrical signal.
[H10F 55/205] wherein the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices have no potential barriers, e.g. photoresistors -- This subgroup of the CPC deals with inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and are constructed without internal potential barriers, for example photoresistors. These are classified under H10F 55/205 within the broader categories of electricity and semiconductor devices.
[H10F 55/207] wherein the electric light source comprises semiconductor devices having potential barriers, e.g. light emitting diodes -- The classification code H10F 55/207 pertains to semiconductor devices that are sensitive to various types of radiation and specifically addresses configurations where the electric light source includes semiconductor devices with potential barriers, such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs).
[H10F 55/208] Optical potentiometers -- Optical potentiometers are variable resistive sensors that adjust an electrical signal by modulating light transmission through a photosensitive element, allowing precise control of voltage or current based on the position of a moving aperture or reflector. They are used in applications where non-contact position sensing, durability, and resistance to wear are required, such as in lighting dimmers, motor speed controllers, and industrial instrumentation.
[H10F 55/25] wherein the radiation-sensitive devices and the electric light source are all semiconductor devices -- H10F 55/25 classifies arrangements in which both the light-emitting element and the radiation-sensing component are made from semiconductor materials, such as infrared-sensitive diodes powered by semiconductor light sources. This falls under inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation.
[H10F 55/255] formed in, or on, a common substrate -- H10F 55/255 designates inorganic semiconductor devices that are fabricated in or on a common substrate, so that two or more active regions share the same bulk material, typical of integrated arrays or multi-pixel infrared sensors.
[H10F 55/26] wherein the radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices have potential barriers -- H10F 55/26 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation and that incorporate potential barriers within the radiation-sensitive semiconductor structure. The barriers are used to control charge carrier flow, enhancing detector selectivity, response speed or noise characteristics.
[H10F 71/00] Manufacture or treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus specially adapted for the formation of semiconductor devices that are sensitive to infrared radiation, visible light, ultraviolet radiation, x-ray radiation, gamma ray radiation or corpuscular radiation, and which are specially adapted for either the conversion of the radiation energy into electrical energy or for the control of electrical energy by such radiation, having active materials that are inorganic.  This includes the formation, patterning or treatment of the inorganic materials used in active layers or in electrodes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Patterning processes to connect thin photovoltaic cells in integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, having photovoltaic cells -> H10F19/33 - Manufacture or treatment of encapsulations or containers for integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, having photovoltaic cells -> H10F19/80 - Manufacture or treatment of integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one element in which radiation controls the flow of current -> H10F39/00

[H10F 77/143] comprising quantum structures -- H10F 77/143 covers inorganic semiconductor detectors that incorporate quantum-scale structures such as quantum wells, quantum dots, superlattices or other nanostructured layers, using quantum confinement to modify the electronic or optical response for infrared, visible or short-wave radiation detection. It addresses the constructional features of such devices, including the choice of substrate material and its ability to bend while maintaining the semiconductor's functional properties.
[H10F 77/1699] the films including Group I-III-VI materials, e.g. CIS or CIGS on metal foils or polymer foils -- H10F 77/1699 relates to inorganic semiconductor devices that incorporate thin-film layers of Group I-III-VI compounds such as copper-indium-selenide (CIS) or copper-indium-gallium-selenide (CIGS) deposited on flexible metal or polymer foils. These films are used to create photovoltaic or photodetector structures that respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation.
[H10F 77/20] Electrodes -- H10F 77/20 covers the electrodes incorporated in inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. It includes the design, material selection and placement of contacts that collect or inject charge carriers in such photodetectors and sensors.
[H10F 77/206] for devices having potential barriers -- It covers constructional details of inorganic semiconductor infrared or short-wavelength detectors that employ a potential barrier, such as a p-n junction, heterojunction, Schottky or quantum-well barrier, to separate charge carriers and control dark current. The barrier is integral to the device architecture for improved responsivity.
[H10F 77/211] for photovoltaic cells -- The subclass H10F 77/211 concerns constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that are sensitive to infrared, visible or other short-wavelength radiation, specifically photovoltaic cells that convert light into electrical power.
[H10F 77/215] Geometries of grid contacts -- H10F 77/215 concerns the design and layout of grid contacts in inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or ultraviolet light or other short-wavelength or corpuscular radiation. It covers geometric aspects such as shape, spacing, pattern and arrangement of the grid structures that form the device's electrical contacts.
[H10F 77/219] Arrangements for electrodes of back-contact photovoltaic cells -- The entry covers the design and layout of electrode structures in back-contact photovoltaic cells, where both the front-side and rear-side contacts are located on the cell's rear surface. It includes interconnections, busbars, and contact patterns that enable carrier collection in inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation.
[H10F 77/223] for metallisation wrap-through [MWT] photovoltaic cells -- H10F 77/223 covers inorganic semiconductor photovoltaic cells that use a metallisation wrap-through (MWT) technique, where conductive tracks are formed through the cell substrate to connect the front and rear contacts. This construction improves current collection and reduces resistive losses in infrared-sensitive solar devices.
[H10F 77/227] for emitter wrap-through [EWT] photovoltaic cells, e.g. interdigitated emitter-base back-contacts -- The subclass H10F 77/227 covers constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, specifically emitter-wrap-through (EWT) photovoltaic cells such as interdigitated emitter-base back-contact designs. It includes features of the cell structure where the emitter region penetrates through the wafer to improve carrier collection and overall cell efficiency.
[H10F 77/241] comprising ring electrodes -- The subclass H10F 77/241 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and are built with ring-shaped electrodes as part of their structure. This constructional feature pertains to devices such as photodiodes or phototransistors where the electrode encircles the active region to influence electrical fields and light collection.
[H10F 77/244] made of transparent conductive layers, e.g. transparent conductive oxide [TCO] layers -- H10F 77/244 pertains to inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and incorporate transparent conductive layers, such as transparent conductive oxide (TCO) films, to form electrodes or contacts while allowing light to pass through. This subclass defines the constructional use of such optically clear conductive coatings in photodetectors and related sensors.
[H10F 77/247] comprising indium tin oxide [ITO] -- H10F 77/247 classifies inorganic semiconductor devices that incorporate indium tin oxide, such as infrared-sensing or optoelectronic components, specifying the structural features of the ITO layers within the device.
[H10F 77/251] comprising zinc oxide [ZnO] -- H10F 77/251 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation and are constructed using zinc oxide. Such devices include infrared detectors and light-sensing components where ZnO serves as an active or structural material.
[H10F 77/254] comprising a metal, e.g. transparent gold -- H10F 77/254 relates to inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and that incorporate a metal, for example a transparent gold layer, as part of the device structure such as electrodes or reflective coatings.
[H10F 77/30] Coatings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  One or more layers that are formed essentially conformally on, and directly associated with, at least a portion of the semiconductor device. These layers may provide passivating or optical effects.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Provisions for preventing damage caused by corpuscular radiation, e.g. for space applications -> H10F77/80
[H10F 77/306] for devices having potential barriers -- H10F 77/306 covers the structural features of inorganic semiconductor infrared or short-wavelength light detectors that rely on potential barriers such as p-n junctions, Schottky contacts or heterojunctions. It includes the design of barrier layers, doping profiles and interfaces that create the electric field needed for photodetection. The subclass focuses on how these barriers are implemented within the device architecture.
[H10F 77/311] for photovoltaic cells -- The H10F 77/311 subclass pertains to constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that convert light into electricity, specifically photovoltaic cells. It covers structural features, material choices and design aspects of solar-cell devices that generate electric power from solar or other electromagnetic radiation.
[H10F 77/315] the coatings being antireflective or having enhancing optical properties -- H10F 77/315 covers the use of antireflective or optically enhancing coatings applied to inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible, ultraviolet or other short-wavelength radiation. These coatings are designed to reduce reflection losses or modify the light-matter interaction to improve device performance. The subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy ELECTRICITY → SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES → INORGANIC SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES SENSITIVE TO INFRARED RADIATION, LIGHT, ELECTROMAGNETIC RADIATION OF SHORTER WAVELENGTH OR CORPUSCULAR RADIATION.
[H10F 77/331] for filtering or shielding light, e.g. multicolour filters for photodetectors -- This subclass covers semiconductor devices that incorporate structures to filter or shield light, such as multicolour filters applied to photodetectors. It pertains to inorganic semiconductor components responsive to infrared, visible or shorter wavelength radiation, focusing on the constructional aspects that manage light transmission. Under CPC H10F 77/331
[H10F 77/334] for shielding light, e.g. light blocking layers or cold shields for infrared detectors -- H10F 77/334 relates to infrared detector assemblies that incorporate features to prevent undesired light from reaching the sensing element. It covers light-blocking layers, cold shields and similar structures designed to absorb, reflect or otherwise exclude visible or shorter-wavelength radiation, thereby improving detector performance.
[H10F 77/337] using interference filters, e.g. multilayer dielectric filters -- H10F 77/337 covers inorganic semiconductor devices-such as photodiodes or infrared detectors-whose operation incorporates interference filters, typically multilayer dielectric filters, to selectively transmit or reflect particular wavelengths of light or radiation. These filters are integrated into the device structure to enhance spectral sensitivity, reduce background noise, or isolate specific radiation bands for improved performance.
[H10F 77/40] Optical elements or arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical elements or arrangements that are associated with the radiation-sensitive device, e.g. used for focusing, reflecting or diffracting light.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Surface textures for light trapping effects -> H10F77/70
[H10F 77/407] indirectly associated with the devices -- The subclass H10F 77/407 covers elements that are not part of the active inorganic semiconductor itself but are linked to its operation, such as mounting structures, interconnections, thermal-management components, shielding or other auxiliary features that support devices sensitive to infrared, visible, ultraviolet or other short-wavelength radiation.
[H10F 77/413] directly associated or integrated with the devices, e.g. back reflectors  directly associated or integrated with photovoltaic cells  H10F77/42 -- H10F 77/413 covers components that are directly associated with or integrated into inorganic semiconductor devices such as photovoltaic cells, for example back reflectors or other structures that form part of the cell assembly and improve its performance. This subclass falls under the broader group dealing with semiconductor devices sensitive to infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation.
[H10F 77/42] directly associated or integrated with photovoltaic cells, e.g. light-reflecting means or light-concentrating means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical elements or arrangements directly associated or integrated with photovoltaic cells, e.g. solar concentrators, lenses, reflectors or Fresnel lenses.
