[H10F 77/48] Back surface reflectors [BSR] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Light-reflecting means that reflects the light that already went through the photovoltaic cell back into the cell.
[H10F 77/484] Refractive light-concentrating means, e.g. lenses -- The subclass H10F 77/484 covers refractive light-concentrating elements such as lenses that are incorporated into inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible, or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. These components serve to focus or direct incoming radiation onto the active region of the detector or sensor, improving its efficiency or spectral response.
[H10F 77/488] Reflecting light-concentrating means, e.g. parabolic mirrors or concentrators using total internal reflection -- This CPC subclass (H10F 77/488) relates to devices that use reflective light-concentrating elements such as parabolic mirrors or total-internal-reflection concentrators to focus radiation onto inorganic semiconductor detectors or emitters that are sensitive to infrared, visible or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. The purpose is to increase optical flux on the active region, improving detection efficiency or power conversion.
[H10F 77/492] Spectrum-splitting means, e.g. dichroic mirrors -- Spectrum-splitting means, such as dichroic mirrors, are employed in inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible, or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation. The technology redirects different portions of the incident spectrum to separate detector elements or functional regions, improving overall efficiency and enabling simultaneous multispectral detection.
[H10F 77/496] Luminescent members, e.g. fluorescent sheets  wavelength conversion means for photovoltaic cells  H10F77/45 -- H10F 77/496 covers luminescent components such as fluorescent sheets that function as wavelength-conversion layers for photovoltaic cells, placed in inorganic semiconductor devices that are sensitive to infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation.
[H10F 77/50] Encapsulations or containers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Encapsulations or containers, e.g. housings, transparent windows or resins, for individual radiation-sensitive devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Encapsulations or containers for integrated devices or assemblies of multiple devices having at least one photovoltaic cell -> H10F19/80
[H10F 77/60] Arrangements for cooling, heating, ventilating or compensating for temperature fluctuations -- The H10F 77/60 subclass covers thermal-management features incorporated in inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation. It includes designs for cooling, heating, ventilating or other means of compensating temperature fluctuations, such as heat sinks, thermoelectric coolers, temperature-controlled enclosures and related circuitry.
[H10F 77/63] Arrangements for cooling directly associated or integrated with photovoltaic cells, e.g. heat sinks directly associated with the photovoltaic cells or integrated Peltier elements for active cooling -- The H10F 77/63 subclass covers cooling structures that are directly linked to photovoltaic cells, such as heat sinks mounted on the cells or integrated Peltier elements providing active temperature control. These arrangements are designed to manage thermal load and improve cell efficiency.
[H10F 77/67] including means to utilise heat energy directly associated with the photovoltaic cells, e.g. integrated Seebeck elements -- H10F 77/67 covers inorganic semiconductor devices, such as photovoltaic cells, that incorporate structures for directly harvesting the heat generated by the cells, for example by integrating Seebeck-type thermoelectric elements that convert the waste thermal energy into electrical power. This subclass focuses on combined photovoltaic-thermoelectric designs that improve overall energy efficiency.
[H10F 77/68] using gaseous or liquid coolants, e.g. air flow ventilation or water circulation -- H10F 77/68 relates to inorganic semiconductor devices that sense infrared, light or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation and covers constructional features using gaseous or liquid coolants, such as air-flow ventilation or water circulation, to control device temperature.
[H10F 77/70] Surface textures, e.g. pyramid structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Surface textures specially adapted for light trapping effects.
[H10F 77/703] of the semiconductor bodies, e.g. textured active layers -- H10F 77/703 covers the constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation, specifically focusing on the semiconductor bodies themselves such as textured active layers that form part of the device's functional structure.
[H10F 77/707] of the substrates or of layers on substrates, e.g. textured ITO layer on a glass substrate -- This subclass covers manufacturing or treatment of substrates and the layers deposited on them in inorganic semiconductor infrared or short-wave devices, such as applying textured indium-tin-oxide coatings to glass. It pertains to constructional details of the substrate and its surface layers that affect device performance.
[H10F 77/80] Arrangements for preventing damage to photovoltaic cells caused by corpuscular radiation, e.g. for space applications -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Provisions to specially adapt photovoltaic devices for space applications or to improve the radiation resistance to avoid radiation damage.
[H10F 77/90] Energy storage means directly associated or integrated with photovoltaic cells, e.g. capacitors integrated with photovoltaic cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Energy storage means directly associated or integrated with photovoltaic cells, e.g. batteries to store electrical energy produced by photovoltaic cells.
[H10F 77/93] Interconnections -- H10F 77/93 relates to the interconnections used in inorganic semiconductor devices that are sensitive to infrared, light, short-wavelength electromagnetic or corpuscular radiation. The subclass covers the physical arrangement, bonding, and contact structures that connect the active semiconductor elements within such devices.
[H10F 77/933] for devices having potential barriers -- This subclass covers inorganic semiconductor detectors sensitive to infrared, visible or short-wavelength radiation that incorporate built-in potential barriers such as p-n junctions, heterojunctions or Schottky contacts to separate charge carriers. It addresses the constructional details of those barrier structures within the device.
[H10F 77/935] for photovoltaic devices or modules -- H10F 77/935 classifies constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that respond to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, specifically when employed as photovoltaic devices or modules. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → inorganic semiconductor devices sensitive to radiation, and addresses structural features that enable photovoltaic function.
[H10F 77/937] Busbar structures for modules -- Busbar structures for modules are conductive metal interconnections that collect and distribute current within inorganic semiconductor devices such as infrared-sensitive photodiodes or solar cells. The design details address the geometry, positioning, and material of the busbars to optimize electrical performance, minimize shading, and ensure reliable thermal and mechanical stability of the module.
[H10F 77/939] Output lead wires or elements -- H10F 77/939 covers the design and arrangement of the output leads or conductive elements that connect inorganic semiconductor devices, such as infrared or short-wavelength detectors, to external circuits. It addresses the geometry, materials and attachment methods of these leads to ensure proper signal extraction and device performance.
[H10F 77/95] Circuit arrangements -- H10F 77/95 relates to the structural design of circuit arrangements for inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, visible light or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation, covering the layout of interconnections, contacts and packaging that integrate the detector elements with supporting electronic components.
[H10F 77/953] for devices having potential barriers -- H10F 77/953 covers constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that are responsive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and that incorporate potential-barrier structures, such as p-n junctions, heterojunctions or quantum-well layers. The entry specifies how these barriers are formed, positioned and integrated within the device architecture to control charge carrier flow and enhance detection or emission performance.
[H10F 77/955] for photovoltaic devices -- The subclass H10F 77/955 covers constructional details of inorganic semiconductor devices that are sensitive to infrared, visible or shorter-wavelength radiation and are used as photovoltaic converters. It includes features such as electrode arrangements, junction designs, encapsulation methods and light-absorbing layer structures specific to solar-cell implementations. This classification is part of the broader group for semiconductor devices responsive to various radiation types.
[H10F 77/957] for position-sensitive photodetectors, e.g. lateral-effect photodiodes or quadrant photodiodes -- H10F 77/957 covers inorganic semiconductor devices that detect infrared, light, or short-wavelength electromagnetic radiation and are designed to provide positional information, such as lateral-effect or quadrant photodiodes. The subclass specifically addresses the construction and layout of these position-sensitive photodetectors.
[H10F 77/959] for devices working in avalanche mode -- H10F 77/959 pertains to inorganic semiconductor detectors that operate in avalanche mode, using impact ionisation to amplify the generated photocurrent. It includes infrared, visible or short-wavelength photodiodes and similar devices that rely on avalanche multiplication for increased signal gain.
[H10F 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- Group H10F99/00 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10F10/00 , H10F19/00 , H10F30/00 , H10F39/00 and H10F39/10 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H] INORGANIC LIGHT-EMITTING SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES HAVING POTENTIAL BARRIERS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inorganic semiconductor devices having potential barriers which emit light when current is passed through them.  This includes the following kinds of devices:  In this subclass, the light-emitting semiconductor devices may emit visible, infrared [IR] or ultraviolet [UV] light.  This place also covers processes and apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of such devices.  In this subclass, the periodic system used is the I to VIII group system indicated in the Periodic Table under Note (3) of section C .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Semiconductor lasers -> H01S5/00  **Glossary:** - bipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that involves multi-carrier-type operation, i.e. which simultaneously uses both electrons and holes as charge carriers - body: the region of semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) within which, or at the surface of which, the physical effects that are characteristic of the device occur, and any bordering semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) that are contiguous with this region. Examples: in a field-effect transistor [FET], the physical effects occur in the channel region between the source and the drain. The semiconductor body includes the channel region, the source and drain regions, and any contiguous semiconductor material; in a light-emitting diode [LED], the physical effects occur at a junction of active semiconductor layers. The semiconductor body includes these active semiconductor layers and any contiguous semiconductor layers, such as buffer layers, possibly a growth substrate, etc., that are between the cathode and anode electrodes; in a thermoelectric device, the solid-state body includes all solid-state materials in the path of current between the electrodes. - chip: a piece of a wafer or a substrate that has been processed to contain devices therein or thereon. The expression "diced chip" refers to the result of dicing a wafer or a substrate into a plurality of chips, whereas "undiced chip" refers to a chip before dicing or with no dicing. - component: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.) that is one of a plurality of elements formed in or on a common substrate, e.g. in an integrated device - container: a solid construction in which (one or more) devices are placed, or which is formed around the devices, for forming packaged devices. A container requires a partial or total enclosure and it may also comprise a filling. - device: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.); (depending on the context) can also refer to an integrated device (e.g. CMOS-IC, DRAM device, etc.). A device may be in the form of a bare or packaged chip. - dopant: the atoms or compounds added to a material during doping - doping: the intentional addition of a small quantity of atoms or compounds into a material to achieve a desired characteristic, e.g. to produce an n-type or p-type material - electrode: a conductive region in or on the semiconductor body or solid-state body of a device (and other than the body itself) which exerts an electrical influence on the body, irrespective of whether or not an external electrical connection is made thereto. The term covers metallic regions which exert electrical influence on the body through an insulating region (e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling), or inductive coupling arrangements. In a capacitive coupling arrangement, the dielectric region is regarded as part of the electrode. The overall conductive wiring may comprise multiple portions. In such a case, only the wiring portions that exert an electrical influence on the body are considered portions of the electrode. Examples: conductive layer(s) in direct physical contact with the body; conductive region(s) exerting an inductive coupling onto the body; a multilayer structure which exerts influence on the body through an insulating region, e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling. - encapsulation: an enclosure consisting of (one or more) layers, e.g. comprising organic polymers, which at least partially enclose the (one or more) devices, thereby protecting them. An encapsulation is often used to hermetically seal devices. - FET: field-effect transistor - field-effect: refers to semiconductor technology wherein a voltage applied to a gate electrode creates an electric field that allows for control of current near the interface of the gate and the body, e.g. to create an inversion channel between the source and drain of a MOSFET - individual: refers to: an electric circuit element not being an integrated device; or a component of an integrated device. Examples of individual devices include: diodes, transistors, photovoltaic cells, Josephson-junction devices, light-emitting diodes [LED], organic LEDs, or a single LED component within an integrated device. - integrated circuit: an integrated device where all the electric circuit elements (e.g. diodes, transistors, LEDs, etc.) are formed in or on a common substrate, including interconnections between the elements - integrated device: a device consisting of a plurality of semiconductor or other solid-state electric circuit elements formed in or on a common substrate - interconnection: a conductive arrangement for conducting electric current from an electrode of a circuit element to another part of the circuit. Examples include metal wirings. - MIS: metal-insulator-semiconductor - MISFET: metal-insulator-semiconductor field-effect transistor - MOS: metal-oxide-semiconductor - package: the collection of all elements, which are external to the chip, that protect the chip or connect it to another object. Package, therefore, covers encapsulations, containers, package substrates, interposers, heatsinks or the like. Package does not include objects at a higher system level, like circuit boards and beyond, e.g. a housing in which the circuit board is enclosed. - TFT: thin-film transistor - unipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that primarily involves one type only of charge carrier, i.e. it involves either holes or electrons but not both - wafer: It can be one of the following: (a) a slice of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material. For example: a slice of silicon; a slice of a semiconducting compound, e.g. gallium nitride [GaN]; a slice of lithium tantalate [LiTaO 3 ] for superconductor applications. (b) a multilayered laminate, having at least one layer of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material, the layer being meant to be processed into devices. For example: silicon-on-insulator [SOI]; silicon-on-glass [SOG]; silicon-on-sapphire [SOS]; a composite wafer comprising silicon carbide [SiC] on polycrystalline silicon [Si] support; a layer of semiconducting nanowires on glass. A wafer is typically processed by (e.g.) deposition, etching, doping or diffusion, and is then typically diced into chips.
[H10H 20/00] Individual inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices having potential barriers, e.g. light-emitting diodes [LED] -- The CPC entry H10H 20/00 defines individual inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain a potential barrier, such as a p-n junction, where electroluminescence occurs across the barrier. It includes LEDs and similar devices built from inorganic semiconductor materials.
[H10H 20/01] Manufacture or treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes and apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of individual light-emitting semiconductor devices covered by main group H10H20/00 .
[H10H 20/011] of bodies, e.g. forming semiconductor layers -- Groups H10H20/011 , H10H20/016 , H10H20/0165 and H10H20/017 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10H20/01 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/012] having light-emitting regions comprising only Group II-IV materials -- H10H 20/012 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LEDs, whose light-emitting region is formed solely from compounds of group II-IV elements. These devices rely on potential barriers within the semiconductor material to confine carriers and produce light.
[H10H 20/0125] with a substrate not being Group II-VI materials -- H10H 20/0125 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LEDs, that incorporate a substrate made from materials other than Group II-VI compounds. It specifies devices where the potential-barrier structure is formed on alternative semiconductor substrates, distinguishing them from those using typical II-VI substrates.
[H10H 20/013] having light-emitting regions comprising only Group III-V materials -- The CPC subclass H10H 20/013 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices whose light-emitting region is formed solely from Group III-V compound materials, such as gallium nitride-based LEDs. It falls under the hierarchy Electricity → Semiconductor devices → Inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices having potential barriers.
[H10H 20/0133] with a substrate not being Group III-V materials -- H10H 20/0133 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that include a potential barrier and are built on substrates other than Group III-V materials, such as silicon, sapphire, or other non-III-V hosts. The classification is used for LEDs and similar devices where the active layers are grown or bonded to these alternative substrates.
[H10H 20/01335] the light-emitting regions comprising nitride materials -- This sub-class concerns inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices with potential-barrier structures whose light-emitting region is formed from nitride materials such as gallium-nitride, aluminum-gallium-nitride or indium-gallium-nitride, typical of modern LEDs where the active layer is a nitride compound.
[H10H 20/0137] the light-emitting regions comprising nitride materials -- The entry covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that incorporate potential barriers, specifically those whose light-emitting regions are formed from nitride materials such as gallium nitride or related alloys. It pertains to LEDs and similar devices that use nitride-based active layers to generate light.
[H10H 20/014] having light-emitting regions comprising only Group IV materials -- Group H10H20/014 is impacted by reclassification into group H10H20/0145 . Groups H10H20/014 and H10H20/0145 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/0145] comprising polycrystalline, amorphous or porous Group IV materials -- Group H10H20/0145 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10H20/014 . Groups H10H20/014 and H10H20/0145 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/016] Thermal treatments -- H10H 20/016 designates thermal treatments applied to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain potential barriers, such as light-emitting diodes. The class is part of the hierarchy under electricity, semiconductor devices and inorganic LED structures, and includes processes like annealing, rapid thermal processing and other heat-based steps used to modify device performance.
[H10H 20/0165] for recrystallisation -- The CPC subclass H10H 20/0165 refers to methods of recrystallisation applied to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain potential barriers, such as light-emitting diodes. It falls within the hierarchy ELECTRICITY → SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES → INORGANIC LIGHT-EMITTING SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES HAVING POTENTIAL BARRIERS.
[H10H 20/017] Etching -- The code H10H 20/017 covers etching operations used in the manufacture of inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices with potential barriers, such as LEDs. It includes processes for removing material to form or modify structures, patterns or channels on the semiconductor layers.

Group H10H20/0364 is also impacted by reclassification into group H10H29/0364 . Groups H10H20/01 , H10H20/0364 and H10H29/0364 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

Group H10H20/0365 is also impacted by reclassification into group H10H29/0365 . Groups H10H20/01 , H10H20/0365 and H10H29/0365 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/042] Superluminescent diodes -- The CPC subclass H10H 20/042 covers superluminescent diodes, semiconductor light sources that combine features of LEDs and laser diodes by using potential-barrier structures to emit broadband, high-brightness light without resonant cavity feedback. These devices are employed in optical sensing, spectroscopy and short-range communication.
[H10H 20/052] Light-emitting semiconductor devices having Schottky type light-emitting regions; Light emitting semiconductor devices having Metal-Insulator-Semiconductor type light-emitting regions -- The H10H 20/052 subclass covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that employ a potential-barrier region for light generation, specifically structures in which the emitting zone is a Schottky-type junction or a metal-insulator-semiconductor (MIS) configuration, such as certain LED designs.
[H10H 20/062] Light-emitting semiconductor devices having field effect type light-emitting regions, e.g. light-emitting High-Electron Mobility Transistors -- H10H 20/062 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that incorporate a field-effect region, such as light-emitting high-electron-mobility transistors, where the light source is generated in the channel of a field-effect device. These devices merge the functions of a transistor and a LED, allowing electrical control of emission via a potential barrier in the channel.
[H10H 20/80] Constructional details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional details of individual light-emitting semiconductor devices covered by main group H10H20/00 . Classification is made in this group if the invention lies in the details of the constructional parts per se, e.g. electrodes, bodies, coatings, etc.

[H10H 20/8131] Stacked light-emitting regions -- Group H10H20/8131 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10H20/813 . Groups H10H20/813 and H10H20/8131 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/8132] Laterally arranged light-emitting regions, e.g. nano-rods -- Groups H10H20/8132 and H10H20/8133 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10H20/813 . Groups H10H20/813 , H10H20/8132 and H10H20/8133 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/8133] having core-shell structures -- H10H 20/8133 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that use core-shell structures, where a light-emitting core is surrounded by a shell layer of different composition. The shell confines carriers, reduces defects and improves efficiency and wavelength stability of LEDs.
[H10H 20/814] having reflecting means, e.g. semiconductor Bragg reflectors -- The subclass H10H 20/814 relates to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LEDs, that incorporate reflecting means like semiconductor Bragg reflectors to improve light extraction or control emission. It is situated under electricity → semiconductor devices → inorganic LED devices having potential barriers.
[H10H 20/8142] forming resonant cavity structures -- The H10H 20/8142 subclass deals with processes for forming resonant-cavity structures in inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain potential barriers, such as LEDs. It includes techniques for creating micro-cavities, distributed Bragg reflectors or other resonant elements to improve light extraction, spectral control or efficiency.
[H10H 20/815] having stress relaxation structures, e.g. buffer layers -- Inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that incorporate stress-relaxation structures, such as buffer layers, to mitigate mechanical strain between layers with mismatched lattice constants, thereby enhancing device reliability and performance. This applies to LEDs and similar potential-barrier emitters.
[H10H 20/816] having carrier transport control structures, e.g. highly-doped semiconductor layers or current-blocking structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Semiconductor bodies having regions, structures or layers that modify the carrier path, or impede or enhance carrier mobility, e.g. carrier transport, blocking or injection layers.
[H10H 20/8162] Current-blocking structures -- Current-blocking structures are features incorporated in inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices such as LEDs that create potential barriers to restrict unwanted charge-carrier flow, improving efficiency and reducing leakage currents. By confining the current to the active region, they help achieve more uniform light emission and enhance device reliability.
[H10H 20/817] characterised by the crystal structures or orientations, e.g. polycrystalline, amorphous or porous -- The H10H 20/817 subclass covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that incorporate potential barriers and are distinguished by their crystal structure or orientation, such as polycrystalline, amorphous or porous forms, typically applied to LEDs. This category falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices having potential barriers.
[H10H 20/818] within the light-emitting regions -- When classifying in this group, classification is also made in group H10H20/822 in order to identify the chemical composition of the light-emitting region.
[H10H 20/819] characterised by their shape, e.g. curved or truncated substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Semiconductor bodies characterised by the shape of their semiconductor bodies, e.g. curved or truncated semiconductor substrates, periodic interfaces or nanostructures.
[H10H 20/82] Roughened surfaces, e.g. at the interface between epitaxial layers -- H10H 20/82 concerns inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices with potential barriers, such as LEDs, where the interface between epitaxial layers is deliberately roughened. The roughened surface modifies optical and electrical properties, influencing light extraction and carrier recombination.
[H10H 20/821] of the light-emitting regions, e.g. non-planar junctions -- H10H 20/821 pertains to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LEDs, that employ non-planar light-emitting regions or shaped junctions to create potential barriers, thereby controlling carrier recombination and improving light-output efficiency.
[H10H 20/8215] characterised by crystalline imperfections, e.g. dislocations; characterised by the distribution of dopants, e.g. delta-doping -- The H10H 20/8215 subclass covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain potential barriers, such as LEDs, where the performance is influenced by crystalline imperfections like dislocations and by the spatial distribution of dopants, for example delta-doping.The H10H 20/8215 subclass covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain potential barriers, such as LEDs, where the performance is influenced by crystalline imperfections like dislocations and by the spatial distribution of dopants, for example delta-doping.
[H10H 20/822] Materials of the light-emitting regions -- When classifying in this group, constituents of a material are considered irrespective of any dopants or other impurities.
[H10H 20/823] comprising only Group II-VI materials, e.g. ZnO -- This subclass concerns inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that rely on potential-barrier structures and are fabricated solely from Group II-VI compounds such as zinc oxide. It includes LEDs and related devices where the active layers consist only of these II-VI materials.
[H10H 20/8232] characterised by the dopants -- The classification covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices such as LEDs that incorporate potential barriers and are defined by the specific dopants used to form those barriers, influencing carrier injection and recombination.
[H10H 20/824] comprising only Group III-V materials, e.g. GaP -- This CPC subclass H10H 20/824 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain only Group III-V compound materials, such as gallium phosphide, and use potential-barrier structures like light-emitting diodes.
[H10H 20/8242] characterised by the dopants -- This subclass pertains to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain potential barriers, where the defining characteristic is the type or amount of dopants incorporated into the semiconductor layers. It is applied to LEDs and similar devices whose electrical and optical properties are engineered through specific dopant selections.
[H10H 20/825] containing nitrogen, e.g. GaN -- This subclass covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that contain nitrogen in the active material, such as gallium nitride LEDs. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for semiconductor devices with potential barriers, code H10H 20/825.
[H10H 20/8252] characterised by the dopants -- This CPC subgroup pertains to individual inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LEDs, whose structure or operation is defined by the specific dopants introduced into the semiconductor material. It covers any device in which the type, concentration or distribution of dopant elements is the distinguishing characteristic.
[H10H 20/826] comprising only Group IV materials -- Group H10H20/826 is impacted by reclassification into group H10H20/8264 . Groups H10H20/826 and H10H20/8264 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10H 20/8262] characterised by the dopants -- This CPC subclass H10H 20/8262 covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that incorporate potential barriers, such as LEDs, where the defining feature is the type or distribution of dopants in the active or barrier layers. It addresses how dopant choice, concentration, or profile modifies the device's electrical and optical performance.

[H10H 20/8312] extending at least partially through the bodies -- This subclass pertains to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices whose potential-barrier layer extends at least partially through the semiconductor bodies, such as LEDs in which a heterojunction or quantum-well region runs across part of the chip to confine charge carriers. It describes designs where the barrier spans the device thickness rather than being limited to a surface layer.
[H10H 20/8314] extending at least partially onto an outer side surface of the bodies -- This CPC subclass covers inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LEDs with built-in potential barriers, wherein a layer or structural element extends at least partially onto the outer side surface of the semiconductor bodies.

It addresses electrode structures where a segmented or non-continuous layer is integrated to improve electrical or optical performance.

[H10H 29/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one light-emitting semiconductor element covered by group -- H10H 29/00 covers integrated or combined structures that incorporate at least one inorganic light-emitting semiconductor element with potential barriers, such as LEDs, together with other components to form a functional device or assembly. It pertains to semiconductor devices within the broader electricity category, focusing on multi-device modules rather than single isolated emitters.

[H10H 29/012] of active-matrix LED displays -- H10H 29/012 covers active-matrix LED displays, i.e., integrated devices that use an array of light-emitting semiconductor elements arranged in a matrix where each pixel is driven individually by thin-film transistors or similar switching elements. The classification applies to displays that employ inorganic LEDs with potential barriers as the light source.
[H10H 29/02] using pick-and-place processes -- H10H 29/02 covers the use of pick-and-place automation for assembling integrated devices that include inorganic light-emitting semiconductor elements such as LEDs, where the elements are positioned onto substrates or into packages by robotic placement. It pertains to manufacturing methods for semiconductor lighting assemblies.
[H10H 29/03] using mass transfer of LEDs, e.g. by using liquid suspensions -- The CPC subgroup H10H 29/03 pertains to methods that relocate inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices with potential barriers by means of mass transfer, such as moving LEDs in liquid suspensions, for integration into larger assemblies or devices. It focuses on the physical handling and placement of LED components rather than their electrical operation.

[H10H 29/20] Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one light-emitting semiconductor device covered by group -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Active-matrix LED displays -> H10H29/30
[H10H 29/24] comprising multiple light-emitting semiconductor devices -- H10H 29/24 classifies integrated assemblies that contain two or more inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices, such as LED arrays or modules, where the devices are combined in a single package or structure. The entry applies to configurations that incorporate multiple light-emitting chips to achieve desired optical or electrical performance.
[H10H 29/30] Active-matrix LED displays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  LED displays wherein the LEDs are arranged into subpixels of different colours (i.e. full-colour LED displays) or into pixels of the same colour (i.e. monochromatic LED displays) and the pixels or subpixels are individually driven by the active-matrix circuitry.  For classification of active-matrix LED displays here, the emphasis of the invention must concern the LEDs, the layers closely related to the LEDs or constructional details closely related to the LEDs, e.g. interconnections between the LEDs or their encapsulations.  **Glossary:** - pixel: unit of a display device, the unit often having an arrangement of subpixels, e.g. an RGB pixel comprising red (R), blue (B) and green (G) subpixels, and the unit typically repeating across the display - subpixel: portion of a pixel capable of emitting light of a particular colour, typically red (R), blue (B) or green (G), and separately addressable from the other subpixels of the pixel. The subpixel includes one or more light-emitting elements, each being connected to addressing circuitry, e.g. a driving transistor.
[H10H 29/32] characterised by the geometry or arrangement of elements within a subpixel, e.g. arrangement of the transistor within its RGB subpixel -- This subclass covers designs that specify the geometry or internal arrangement of components within a subpixel, for example the positioning of the driving transistor relative to the red, green and blue light-emitting elements in an inorganic LED display. It pertains to integrated semiconductor light-emitting devices where subpixel layout affects performance.
[H10H 29/34] characterised by the geometry or arrangement of subpixels within a pixel, e.g. relative disposition of the RGB subpixels -- The code refers to integrated inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices whose function depends on the geometric layout of subpixels inside a pixel, such as the relative placement of red, green and blue emitters. It classifies designs that specify how subpixels are arranged to achieve desired colour or imaging performance.
[H10H 29/345] the area of the subpixels being different -- H10H 29/345 pertains to integrated inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices in which the individual subpixels are designed with different surface areas, typically to modify light output, color balance or efficiency within a display or illumination module. This classification covers arrangements where the area of each subpixel varies intentionally as part of the device architecture.
[H10H 29/352] characterised by differences in geometry or arrangement of elements, subpixels or pixels in different regions of the display, e.g. at the central and peripheral regions -- The code H10H 29/352 applies to inorganic light-emitting semiconductor displays where the geometry or arrangement of subpixels or pixels varies between different regions of the screen, such as the central versus peripheral areas, influencing the device's construction or operation.
[H10H 29/362] comprising more than three subpixels, e.g. red-green-blue-white [RGBW] -- This CPC subgroup covers integrated assemblies that include at least one inorganic light-emitting semiconductor element and employ more than three subpixels, for example a red-green-blue-white (RGBW) pixel arrangement. It is used to classify devices where multiple colour subpixels are combined within a single package or panel.
[H10H 29/37] Pixel-defining structures, e.g. banks between the LEDs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The layer or layers of an active-matrix display that define the region in which the light-emitting devices are disposed and physically separates the light-emitting devices in one subpixel from the light-emitting devices in other subpixels, or the light-emitting devices in one pixel from the light-emitting devices in other pixels.
[H10H 29/39] Connection of the pixel electrodes to the driving transistors -- The code H10H 29/39 covers methods for electrically connecting pixel electrodes of inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices to their driving transistors within integrated displays. It pertains to the interconnect architecture linking each pixel's electrode to the active-matrix transistor in LED or OLED arrays.
[H10H 29/41] Insulating layers formed between the driving transistors and the LEDs -- This subclass concerns the use of insulating layers positioned between the driving transistors and light-emitting diodes in integrated semiconductor lighting modules. It addresses the design and material aspects of such inter-die insulation to provide electrical isolation, thermal management and reliability in inorganic LED devices.
[H10H 29/45] comprising two substrates, each having active devices thereon, e.g. displays comprising LED arrays and driving circuitry on different substrates -- The code H10H 29/45 covers integrated assemblies that use two separate substrates, each carrying active semiconductor devices. Typical examples are displays where an LED array is fabricated on one substrate while the driving circuitry is fabricated on a second substrate.
[H10H 29/49] Interconnections, e.g. wiring lines or terminals -- Group H10H29/49 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10H20/857 and H10H29/142 . Groups H10H20/857 , H10H29/142 and H10H29/49 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

[H10H 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- H10H 99/00 is a residual group in the CPC for inorganic light-emitting semiconductor devices that incorporate potential barriers. It is used when the device's features or functions are not covered by any of the more specific groups defined in the same subclass. This entry ensures that all such semiconductor light-emitting inventions have a classification place even if they fall outside the detailed categories.
[H10K] ORGANIC ELECTRIC SOLID-STATE DEVICES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric solid-state devices having organic materials as the active layers, or using a combination of organic materials and other materials as the active layers.  This includes the following kind of devices:  Processes and apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of such devices.  Organic materials used in active layers, layers having high carrier mobility or electrodes of devices covered by this subclass.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Organic resistors without potential barriers and not being specially adapted for integrated devices -> H01C - Organic capacitors, e.g. organic polymer capacitors, without potential barriers and not being specially adapted for integrated devices -> H01G - Organic electronic memory devices -> H10B - Organic thermoelectric devices; Organic thermomagnetic devices -> H10N10/00 , H10N15/00 , H10N19/00 - Organic piezoelectric or electrostrictive devices -> H10N30/00 , H10N39/00 - Organic magnetostrictive devices -> H10N35/00 , H10N39/00 - Organic galvanomagnetic or Hall-effect devices -> H10N50/00 , H10N52/00 , H10N59/00 - Organic superconducting devices -> H10N60/00 , H10N69/00 - Organic solid-state devices without potential barriers, and specially adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching -> H10N70/00 , H10N79/00  **Glossary:** - chip: a piece of a wafer or a substrate that has been processed to contain devices therein or thereon. The expression "diced chip" refers to the result of dicing a wafer or a substrate into a plurality of chips, whereas "undiced chip" refers to a chip before dicing or with no dicing. - device: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.); (depending on the context) can also refer to an integrated device (e.g. CMOS-IC, DRAM device, etc.). A device may be in the form of a bare or packaged chip. - dopant: the atoms or compounds added to a material during doping - doping: the intentional addition of a small quantity of atoms or compounds into a material to achieve a desired characteristic, e.g. to produce an n-type or p-type material - individual: refers to: an electric circuit element not being an integrated device; or a component of an integrated device. Examples of individual devices include: diodes, transistors, photovoltaic cells, Josephson-junction devices, light-emitting diodes [LED], organic LEDs or a single LED component within an integrated device. - integrated device: a device consisting of a plurality of semiconductor or other solid-state electric circuit elements formed in or on a common substrate - integrated circuit: an integrated device where all the electric circuit elements (e.g. diodes, transistors, LEDs, etc.) are formed in or on a common substrate, including interconnections between the elements - component: an electric circuit element (e.g. diode, transistor, LED, etc.) that is one of a plurality of elements formed in or on a common substrate, e.g. in an integrated device - wafer: it can be one of the following: (a) a slice of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material. For example: a slice of silicon; a slice of a semiconducting compound, e.g. gallium nitride [GaN]; a slice of lithium tantalate [LiTaO 3 ] for superconductor applications. (b) A multilayered laminate, having at least one layer of semiconductor or electric solid-state active material, the layer being meant to be processed into devices. For example: silicon-on-insulator [SOI]; silicon-on-glass [SOG]; silicon-on-sapphire [SOS]; a composite wafer comprising silicon carbide [SiC] on polycrystalline silicon [Si] support; a layer of semiconducting nanowires on glass. A wafer is typically processed by (e.g.) deposition, etching, doping or diffusion, and is then typically diced into chips. - body: the region of semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) within which, or at the surface of which, the physical effects that are characteristic of the device occur, and any bordering semiconductor (resp. solid-state) material(s) that are contiguous with this region. Examples: in a field-effect transistor [FET], the physical effects occur in the channel region between the source and the drain. The semiconductor body includes the channel region, the source and drain regions, and any contiguous semiconductor material; in a light-emitting diode [LED], the physical effects occur at a junction of active semiconductor layers. The semiconductor body includes these active semiconductor layers and any contiguous semiconductor layers, such as buffer layers, possibly a growth substrate, etc., that are between the cathode and anode electrodes; in a thermoelectric device, the solid-state body includes all solid-state materials in the path of current between the electrodes. - electrode: a conductive region in or on the semiconductor body or solid-state body of a device (and other than the body itself) which exerts an electrical influence on the body, irrespective of whether or not an external electrical connection is made thereto. The term covers metallic regions which exert electrical influence on the body through an insulating region (e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling), or inductive coupling arrangements. In a capacitive coupling arrangement, the dielectric region is regarded as part of the electrode. The overall conductive wiring may comprise multiple portions. In such a case, only the wiring portions that exert an electrical influence on the body are considered portions of the electrode. Examples: conductive layer(s) in direct physical contact with the body; conductive region(s) exerting an inductive coupling onto the body; a multilayer structure which exerts influence on the body through an insulating region, e.g. in intentional non-parasitic capacitive coupling. - interconnection: a conductive arrangement for conducting electric current from an electrode of a circuit element to another part of the circuit. Examples include metal wirings. - container: a solid construction in which (one or more) devices are placed, or which is formed around the devices, for forming packaged devices. A container requires a partial or total enclosure and it may also comprise a filling. - encapsulation: an enclosure consisting of (one or more) layers, e.g. comprising organic polymers, which at least partially enclose the (one or more) devices, thereby protecting them. An encapsulation is often used to hermetically seal devices. - field-effect: refers to semiconductor technology wherein a voltage applied to a gate electrode creates an electric field that allows for control of current near the interface of the gate and the body, e.g. to create an inversion channel between the source and drain of a MOSFET - package: the collection of all elements, which are external to the chip, that protect the chip or connect it to another object. Package therefore covers encapsulations, containers, package substrates, interposers, heatsinks or the like. Package does not include objects at a higher system level, like circuit boards and beyond, e.g. a housing in which the circuit board is enclosed. - unipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that primarily involves one type only of charge carrier, i.e. it involves either holes or electrons but not both - bipolar: refers to semiconductor technology that involves multi-carrier-type operation, i.e. which simultaneously uses both electrons and holes as charge carriers - MIS: metal-insulator-semiconductor - MOS: metal-oxide-semiconductor - FET: field-effect transistor - MISFET: metal-insulator-semiconductor field-effect transistor - TFT: thin-film transistor - active material: the material within which the physical effects that are characteristic of the device occur - auxiliary electrode: one part of a multi-layered electrode, often being metallic and intended to increase the conductivity of transparent oxide electrodes - coordination compound: a material having a chemical structure in which a central atom is chemically bonded to surrounding nonmetal atoms or groups of atoms. The central atom may be a metal atom or may be a metalloid (e.g. B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te or Po). - electroluminescent layer, emissive layer: the layer within which electrons and holes combine, resulting in light emission - organic device: a device that comprises one or more organic materials as the active material, e.g. using only organic active materials or e.g. using a combination of an organic material and another material - radiation-sensitive: refers to a device or a component that is sensitive to infrared radiation, light, electromagnetic radiation of shorter wavelength or corpuscular radiation - tandem OLED: an OLED that comprises multiple electroluminescent units between one set of electrodes and a charge generation layer between the electroluminescent units - tandem PV cell: a photovoltaic cell that comprises multiple stacked photovoltaic units, e.g. p-n junctions, between one set of electrodes. Often each unit is made from a semiconductor of different bandgap energy, so each is sensitive to a different part of the electromagnetic spectrum. - terminal: the electrode or interconnection within a device, which serves as a connecting point between electrodes or interconnections within the device and interconnections that may be in the device's package or may be external to the device. An example is a bond pad on the cathode of an OLED, which may connect between the cathode electrode and a bonding wire in the OLED's package. - tiled display: a display that comprises a juxtaposition of smaller interconnected panels in order to achieve a large-area display
[H10K 10/00] Organic devices specially adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Organic devices wherein an electrical input is rectified, amplified, oscillated or switched.  Active resistors or capacitors using organic materials as the active layers, or using a combination of organic materials with other material as the active layers.  Examples include:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Organic integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices -> H10K19/00
[H10K 10/10] Organic capacitors or resistors having potential barriers -- H10K 10/10 defines organic capacitors or resistors that contain internal potential barriers, which regulate charge transport in the device. These solid-state components are used in organic rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching applications, and are classified under organic electric solid-state devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10K 10/20] Organic diodes -- H10K 10/20 covers organic diodes-solid-state devices in which an organic semiconductor layer forms a rectifying junction that permits current flow in one direction. These components are used for low-power rectification, signal detection, and switching in flexible or printed electronics, and they differ from inorganic counterparts by employing carbon-based materials that can be deposited on lightweight or large-area substrates.
[H10K 10/23] Schottky diodes -- H10K 10/23 covers Schottky diodes, a type of semiconductor device that uses a metal-semiconductor junction to provide fast, low-voltage rectification and switching. These organic-solid-state components are employed in power supplies, radio-frequency circuits and detector circuits where low forward drop and high speed are required.
[H10K 10/26] Diodes comprising organic-organic junctions -- H10K 10/26 covers diodes that are built from two organic semiconductor layers forming an organic-organic junction, where charge transport occurs across the interface between the two materials. These devices are used for rectification, amplification, oscillation or switching in solid-state circuits and fall under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 10/29] Diodes comprising organic-inorganic heterojunctions -- Diodes built with an organic-inorganic heterojunction, where a thin organic layer contacts an inorganic semiconductor to form the rectifying junction. These devices belong to organic solid-state technology and are suited for rectification, amplification, oscillation or switching functions.
[H10K 10/40] Organic transistors -- H10K 10/40 covers organic transistors, semiconductor devices made from organic materials that serve as rectifiers, amplifiers, oscillators or switches. These components are part of the organic electric solid-state devices subclass within the broader electricity and semiconductor categories.
[H10K 10/43] Bipolar transistors, e.g. organic bipolar junction transistors [OBJT] -- The H10K 10/43 class covers organic bipolar transistors, including organic bipolar-junction devices used for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. It encompasses the design, material composition, fabrication methods and electrical characteristics of these organic semiconductor structures, as well as related circuit arrangements that exploit their bipolar operation.
[H10K 10/46] Field-effect transistors, e.g. organic thin-film transistors [OTFT] -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bipolar transistors, e.g. organic bipolar junction transistors [OBJTs] -> H10K10/43
[H10K 10/462] Insulated gate field-effect transistors [IGFETs] -- H10K 10/462 covers insulated gate field-effect transistors, known as IGFETs, which use an insulated gate to control current flow through a semiconductor channel. These devices are employed in electronic circuits for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions.
[H10K 10/464] Lateral top-gate IGFETs comprising only a single gate -- The subgroup defines lateral insulated-gate field-effect transistors in which a single gate electrode is positioned on the top of the semiconductor channel. It applies to organic solid-state devices used for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions.
[H10K 10/466] Lateral bottom-gate IGFETs comprising only a single gate -- This subclass defines lateral bottom-gate insulated-gate field-effect transistors that use only a single gate electrode positioned beneath the semiconductor channel. It applies to organic solid-state devices designed for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions, where the gate is formed on the bottom side of the structure.
[H10K 10/468] characterised by the gate dielectrics -- H10K 10/468 refers to organic solid-state semiconductor devices such as organic field-effect transistors in which the gate dielectric layer is a defining element. The classification covers devices where the dielectric material - organic, inorganic, polymeric or composite - is specified as part of the structure and influences the rectification, amplification, oscillation or switching function.
[H10K 10/471] the gate dielectric comprising only organic materials -- H10K 10/471 covers gate dielectrics made exclusively of organic materials used in organic solid-state semiconductor devices such as organic transistors. The subclass falls under organic electric solid-state devices that perform rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions.
[H10K 10/472] the gate dielectric comprising only inorganic materials -- The code H10K 10/472 refers to organic solid-state semiconductor devices in which the gate dielectric layer is formed entirely from inorganic materials, such as oxides or nitrides, instead of organic compounds. This restriction applies to devices used for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions.
[H10K 10/474] the gate dielectric comprising a multilayered structure -- This subclass concerns organic solid-state semiconductor devices in which the gate dielectric is built as a multilayered structure, often combining inorganic and organic layers to achieve specific dielectric properties. The layered arrangement is used to improve performance, stability and scaling of rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching organic devices such as transistors.
[H10K 10/476] comprising at least one organic layer and at least one inorganic layer -- H10K 10/476 covers organic-inorganic hybrid solid-state semiconductor devices that contain at least one organic layer together with at least one inorganic layer, used for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. The classification falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the broader semiconductor device hierarchy.
[H10K 10/478] the gate dielectric comprising a layer of composite material comprising interpenetrating or embedded materials, e.g. TiO 2  particles in a polymer matrix -- H10K 10/478 relates to organic solid-state devices in which the gate dielectric is a composite layer formed by inorganic particles such as TiO2 embedded in a polymer matrix, providing interpenetrating or dispersed structures that modify dielectric properties. This subclass covers the use of such composite dielectrics in devices designed for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions.
[H10K 10/481] characterised by the gate conductors -- This CPC subgroup covers organic solid-state devices, such as organic field-effect transistors, that are defined primarily by the presence and configuration of gate conductors. The gate electrode is a key structural element enabling rectification, amplification, oscillation or switching functions within the device.
[H10K 10/482] the IGFET comprising multiple separately-addressable gate electrodes -- The subclass H10K 10/482 covers integrated-gate field-effect transistors that incorporate two or more gate electrodes which can be addressed separately, allowing independent control of different regions of the channel for functions such as multi-threshold operation or on-chip logic. It applies to organic semiconductor devices used for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching.
[H10K 10/484] characterised by the channel regions -- This subclass covers organic solid-state semiconductor devices whose defining feature is the channel region, the portion that controls charge flow in devices such as field-effect transistors. It includes inventions where the geometry, doping or material of the channel is central to rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions.
[H10K 10/486] the channel region comprising two or more active layers, e.g. forming pn heterojunctions -- The H10K 10/486 subclass covers organic solid-state devices whose channel region includes two or more active layers, such as a pn heterojunction, to provide rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. It falls under Electricity → Semiconductor devices → Organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 10/488] the channel region comprising a layer of composite material having interpenetrating or embedded materials, e.g. a mixture of donor and acceptor moieties, that form a bulk heterojunction -- H10K 10/488 covers organic solid-state devices where the channel region consists of a composite layer of interpenetrating donor and acceptor materials that form a bulk heterojunction. The structure is employed to enhance charge transport in rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching applications.
[H10K 10/491] Vertical transistors, e.g. vertical carbon nanotube field effect transistors [CNT-FETs] -- H10K 10/491 pertains to vertical transistor configurations, such as vertical carbon-nanotube field-effect transistors, in which the active channel extends perpendicular to the substrate, enabling high current density and compact integration in organic or carbon-based solid-state devices.
[H10K 10/50] Bistable switching devices -- H10K 10/50 refers to bistable switching devices, organic solid-state semiconductor components that can remain in one of two stable states without continuous power and are used for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. This class falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 10/701] Organic molecular electronic devices -- H10K 10/701 covers organic molecular electronic devices that employ organic compounds as the active material for functions such as rectification, amplification, oscillation or switching. These include organic diodes, transistors, mixers and similar solid-state components where charge transport occurs through molecular or polymeric layers rather than inorganic semiconductors. The classification is part of the CPC hierarchy under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 10/80] Constructional details -- The H10K 10/80 subclass pertains to constructional details of organic solid-state devices designed for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. It covers the physical layout, layer-stack configurations, substrate arrangements and other structural features of organic diodes, transistors, thyristors and similar components.
[H10K 10/82] Electrodes -- H10K 10/82 covers the electrodes of organic solid-state semiconductor devices such as organic diodes, transistors and similar components. It includes the conductive elements that inject or collect charge carriers in organic rectifiers, amplifiers, oscillators and switches. The class addresses material composition, structure and arrangement of these electrodes.
[H10K 10/84] Ohmic electrodes, e.g. source or drain electrodes -- H10K 10/84 covers ohmic electrodes such as source or drain contacts used in organic solid-state devices like organic transistors. These electrodes provide low-resistance, non-rectifying connections to the organic semiconductor layer. The class falls under electricity, semiconductor devices, organic electric solid-state devices, specifically devices adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching.
[H10K 10/86] Schottky electrodes -- H10K 10/86 covers Schottky electrodes, metal contacts that create a Schottky barrier with an organic semiconductor in solid-state devices such as diodes, transistors or switches. The classification is used for patents describing the structure, material selection, fabrication and performance of these rectifying or amplifying contacts within organic electronic components.
[H10K 10/88] Passivation -- Passivation refers to the application of protective layers or treatments to organic solid-state semiconductor devices to improve stability, prevent degradation, and enhance performance in rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. It includes coating, surface treatment, or incorporation of barrier materials that shield the active organic layers from moisture, oxygen, and electrical stress.
[H10K 19/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one organic element specially adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching, covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrated devices comprising at least one organic component specially adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching covered by group H10K10/00 .  Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one organic device specially adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching covered by group H10K10/00 .
[H10K 19/10] comprising field-effect transistors -- The CPC entry H10K 19/10 covers integrated organic solid-state assemblies that contain at least one organic field-effect transistor. It applies to devices where the transistor is part of a circuit that may perform rectification, amplification, oscillation or switching, and is grouped under organic semiconductor devices.
[H10K 19/20] comprising components having an active region that includes an inorganic semiconductor -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10K 19/20 pertains to integrated devices or assemblies with at least one organic element, specifically those that include components with an active region containing an inorganic semiconductor. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on organic electric solid-state devices adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating, or switching.
[H10K 19/201] Integrated devices having a three-dimensional layout, e.g. 3D ICs -- H10K 19/201 pertains to integrated organic semiconductor devices with a three-dimensional structure, such as 3D integrated circuits (ICs), which are designed for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating, or switching functions.
[H10K 19/202] Integrated devices comprising a common active layer -- H10K 19/202 covers integrated organic semiconductor assemblies in which two or more devices share a single, common active layer that performs the electronic function. The entry pertains to organic solid-state components such as rectifiers, amplifiers, oscillators or switches that are fabricated together rather than as separate parts.
[H10K 19/80] Interconnections, e.g. terminals -- The H10K 19/80 entry concerns the interconnections such as terminals used in integrated organic solid-state devices that incorporate at least one organic semiconductor element for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching functions. It addresses the wiring, contacts and connection methods within such assemblies.
[H10K 19/901] Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one organic element specially adapted for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching -- The CPC subclass H10K 19/901 covers integrated assemblies that contain two or more electronic components, with at least one organic semiconductor element specifically designed for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching. It includes circuits or modules where organic diodes, transistors or similar devices are combined with other parts to perform those functions.
[H10K 2101/00] Indexing scheme associated with group -- H10K 2101/00 is a CPC subgroup for organic electric solid-state devices that defines the indexing scheme used to organise the individual patent groups within this field. It provides a systematic numbering system to locate inventions concerning organic semiconductor structures and related manufacturing methods. The entry falls under the hierarchy electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 2101/10] Triplet emission -- H10K 2101/10 designates organic solid-state devices that generate light by triplet-state emission, such as phosphorescent organic light-emitting diodes. The code covers structures and materials that harvest triplet excitons to improve electroluminescent efficiency within the broader class of semiconductor devices and electricity technologies.
[H10K 2101/20] Delayed fluorescence emission -- H10K 2101/20 covers organic solid-state electronic devices that emit light via delayed fluorescence, where excitation energy is stored and released after a measurable time interval, improving the efficiency of OLEDs and related applications.
[H10K 2101/25] using exciplex -- H10K 2101/25 covers the use of exciplexes-intermolecular excited-state complexes-in organic solid-state electric devices such as OLEDs, organic photovoltaics, and other semiconductor applications. It addresses how exciplex formation improves charge transport, emission properties, or energy-conversion efficiency within these devices.
[H10K 2101/27] Combination of fluorescent and phosphorescent emission -- The H10K 2101/27 subclass concerns organic solid-state light-emitting devices that incorporate both fluorescent and phosphorescent emitters, usually within an OLED architecture. It covers designs that combine the two emission mechanisms to boost efficiency, broaden colour gamut, or improve operational stability of the device.
[H10K 2101/30] Highest occupied molecular orbital [HOMO], lowest unoccupied molecular orbital [LUMO] or Fermi energy values -- The H10K 2101/30 subclass covers organic solid-state semiconductor devices in which the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital, or the Fermi energy are specified or measured. It pertains to the design, analysis or optimization of such devices based on these electronic-structure parameters.
[H10K 2101/40] Interrelation of parameters between multiple constituent active layers or sublayers, e.g. HOMO values in adjacent layers -- The code H10K 2101/40 covers the analysis of how electrical parameters of one active layer in an organic solid-state device affect or are linked to those of neighboring layers, such as the relative HOMO energy levels across interfaces. It addresses design approaches that consider interlayer coupling to optimise device performance.
[H10K 2101/50] Oxidation-reduction potentials, e.g. excited state redox potentials -- The H10K 2101/50 subclass deals with the specification and measurement of oxidation-reduction (redox) potentials, including those of excited states, for organic solid-state semiconductor devices such as OLEDs and organic photovoltaics, providing data essential for designing and optimizing their electrical performance.
[H10K 2101/60] Up-conversion, e.g. by triplet-triplet annihilation -- The code H10K 2101/60 designates organic solid-state up-conversion techniques that transform lower-energy light into higher-energy light, most commonly via triplet-triplet annihilation. It applies to semiconductor devices such as organic LEDs and photovoltaic cells that exploit this photon-energy conversion.
[H10K 2101/70] Down-conversion, e.g. by singlet fission -- H10K 2101/70 covers down-conversion techniques in organic solid-state electronic devices, notably processes such as singlet fission that split a high-energy exciton into two lower-energy excitons to improve photon utilization. This subclass is nested under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 2101/80] Composition varying spatially, e.g. having a spatial gradient -- H10K 2101/80 classifies organic solid-state semiconductor devices whose active material has a composition that varies across the device, such as a spatial gradient. It covers structures and manufacturing techniques that intentionally create compositional gradients to modify electrical or optical performance.
[H10K 2101/90] Multiple hosts in the emissive layer -- The H10K 2101/90 subclass pertains to organic solid-state electric devices-such as OLEDs-in which the emissive layer incorporates two or more host materials. Using multiple hosts optimises charge transport, emission uniformity and colour stability, enabling improved device performance and broader application ranges.
[H10K 2102/00] Indexing scheme associated with groups -- This CPC subclass defines the indexing scheme used to organise groups within the H10K 2102 area, which covers organic electric solid-state devices such as organic light-emitting diodes, organic photovoltaic cells and related semiconductor structures. It specifies how functional elements, layers or material compositions are systematically indexed for classification.
[H10K 2102/10] Transparent electrodes, e.g. using graphene -- Transparent electrodes, such as those employing graphene, provide a conductive layer that permits light transmission and are used in organic solid-state devices like displays, solar cells and sensors, improving performance while maintaining electrical connectivity.
[H10K 2102/101] comprising transparent conductive oxides [TCO] -- H10K 2102/101 designates organic solid-state semiconductor devices that incorporate transparent conductive oxides as part of the structure, typically as electrodes or charge-transport layers, providing both electrical conductivity and optical transparency. It is a sub-group of electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 2102/102] comprising tin oxides, e.g. fluorine-doped SnO 2 -- This CPC subclass H10K 2102/102 covers organic electric solid-state devices that incorporate tin-oxide materials, such as fluorine-doped SnO₂, as conductive or transparent layers, electrodes, or functional components. It applies to semiconductor devices like thin-film transistors, displays and sensors where tin oxides provide the required electrical properties.
[H10K 2102/103] comprising indium oxides, e.g. ITO -- H10K 2102/103 is a CPC subclass for organic solid-state electric devices that incorporate indium oxides, such as indium-tin-oxide, as conductive layers or electrodes. It resides under the hierarchy Electricity → Semiconductor Devices → Organic Electric Solid-State Devices and designates devices where indium-oxide compounds play a functional role.
[H10K 2102/20] Metallic electrodes, e.g. using a stack of layers -- H10K 2102/20 relates to organic electric solid-state devices that use metallic electrodes, especially when the electrode is built as a stack of multiple metal layers, and covers the design and integration of such layered metal contacts in semiconductor components.
[H10K 2102/301] Details of OLEDs -- H10K 2102/301 is a CPC subclass within electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices that addresses the detailed features of organic light-emitting diodes, including layer structures, materials, fabrication techniques and performance characteristics.
[H10K 2102/302] of OLED structures -- H10K 2102/302 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification dealing with organic electric solid-state devices, specifically the structural features of organic light-emitting diode (OLED) assemblies. It covers layer configurations, substrate types, electrode arrangements, encapsulation methods and other design elements that define how OLEDs are built and function.
[H10K 2102/3023] Direction of light emission -- The CPC subclass H10K 2102/3023 pertains to organic electric solid-state devices in which the direction of light emission is defined, controlled, or optimized, such as organic light-emitting diodes with specific emission angles or patterned output. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 2102/3026] Top emission -- The H10K 2102/3026 subclass covers organic solid-state devices-such as OLEDs-designed to emit light through the top electrode rather than through the substrate. Top-emission structures are used when a thin, transparent bottom electrode is impractical or when integration with display backplanes requires illumination from above.
[H10K 2102/3031] Two-side emission, e.g. transparent OLEDs [TOLED] -- The CPC entry H10K 2102/3031 falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices and covers organic light-emitting structures that emit light from both sides of the substrate, such as transparent OLEDs (TOLED).
[H10K 2102/3035] Edge emission -- H10K 2102/3035 designates technologies that employ edge emission in organic electric solid-state devices, where light is generated and extracted from the side edges of the organic layer instead of the top surface. The group covers device structures, materials and manufacturing methods that enable or enhance this edge-coupled optical output in organic LEDs and similar components.
[H10K 2102/311] Flexible OLED -- H10K 2102/311 covers flexible organic light-emitting diode (OLED) devices, focusing on bendable or rollable solid-state displays built from organic semiconductor layers. It includes structures, materials, and manufacturing methods that enable OLED panels to maintain performance while being deformed, and falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 2102/321] Inverted OLED, i.e. having cathode between substrate and anode -- Inverted organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) are solid-state devices where the cathode is positioned between the substrate and the anode, opposite the conventional stack order. This configuration alters charge injection and can improve device stability and manufacturing simplicity. The entry falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices in the CPC system.
[H10K 2102/331] Nanoparticles used in non-emissive layers, e.g. in packaging layer -- The CPC entry H10K 2102/331 classifies the use of nanoparticles incorporated into non-emissive layers of organic electric solid-state devices, such as the packaging or encapsulation layers that protect the active components. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices and addresses materials that enhance barrier, mechanical or optical properties without participating in light emission.
[H10K 2102/341] Short-circuit prevention -- H10K 2102/341 covers measures and structures designed to prevent short-circuit conditions in organic solid-state devices such as organic LEDs, organic photovoltaics and similar semiconductor components. It includes protective circuits, current-limiting layers, and material engineering techniques that mitigate unintended electrical connections and protect device performance.
[H10K 2102/351] Thickness -- H10K 2102/351 covers the thickness of active layers or other functional layers in organic solid-state electric devices such as organic light-emitting diodes and organic field-effect transistors. It includes methods for defining, measuring, or controlling layer thickness during fabrication or in the final device.
[H10K 2102/361] Temperature -- H10K 2102/361 covers temperature-related aspects of organic electric solid-state devices, such as the influence of temperature on device performance, methods for temperature measurement, control, compensation, and stabilization in organic semiconductor components. It is a sub-group of the organic electric solid-state devices class within semiconductor devices under the broader electricity field.
