[H10K 30/00] Organic radiation-sensitive devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices specially adapted for sensing infra-red radiation, light, electro-magnetic radiation of shorter wavelength or corpuscular radiation and adapted for the conversion of the energy of such radiation into electrical energy or for the control of electrical energy by such radiation using organic materials as the active layers, or using a combination of organic materials with other material as the active layers.  Examples include:  H10K30/50 - H10K30/57 specifically cover photovoltaic cells. H10K30/60 - H10K30/65 specifically cover photodiodes, photoresistors and phototransistors. H10K30/10 - H10K30/451 cover structural or junction aspects of photovoltaic cells, photodiodes, photoresistors and phototransistors.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Organic integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices -> H10K39/00 , H10K65/00 - Electrolytic light-sensitive devices -> H01G9/20
[H10K 30/10] comprising heterojunctions between organic semiconductors and inorganic semiconductors -- This CPC sub-class covers organic radiation-sensitive devices that incorporate heterojunctions where an organic semiconductor layer is joined to an inorganic semiconductor layer, enabling charge separation or transport across the interface. It includes devices such as organic-inorganic hybrid photovoltaic cells, light-emitting structures, and photodetectors that rely on such heterojunctions.
[H10K 30/15] Sensitised wide-bandgap semiconductor devices, e.g. dye-sensitised TiO -- H10K 30/15 covers semiconductor devices that use a wide-bandgap material whose optical response is extended by a sensitising layer, typically an organic dye, as exemplified by dye-sensitised TiO2 structures used in photovoltaic or photodetection applications.
[H10K 30/151] the wide bandgap semiconductor comprising titanium oxide, e.g. TiO 2 -- H10K 30/151 covers the use of wide-bandgap semiconductors such as titanium oxide (TiO₂) in organic radiation-sensitive devices. The subclass falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices, and concerns the material's properties and applications as a photoactive layer or charge-transport medium in organic optoelectronic components.
[H10K 30/152] the wide bandgap semiconductor comprising zinc oxide, e.g. ZnO -- The H10K 30/152 subclass covers semiconductor devices that employ a wide band-gap material based on zinc oxide, such as ZnO, for use in organic radiation-sensitive components. It includes the composition, fabrication and performance aspects of ZnO-based semiconductors that enable high-frequency, high-power or UV-responsive operation within solid-state organic devices.
[H10K 30/20] comprising organic-organic junctions, e.g. donor-acceptor junctions -- The H10K 30/20 subclass covers organic-organic junctions in radiation-sensitive solid-state devices, such as donor-acceptor interfaces where two distinct organic layers meet to facilitate charge separation or transport. These junctions are used in photodiodes, photovoltaic cells and similar optoelectronic components that rely on organic semiconductor materials.
[H10K 30/211] comprising multiple junctions, e.g. double heterojunctions -- H10K 30/211 refers to organic radiation-sensitive devices that incorporate multiple junctions, such as double heterojunction structures, to improve charge separation or transport. These configurations are used in organic photodiodes, solar cells and image sensors to enhance efficiency and performance.
[H10K 30/30] comprising bulk heterojunctions, e.g. interpenetrating networks of donor and acceptor material domains -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code H10K 30/30 pertains to organic radiation-sensitive devices that feature bulk heterojunctions, which are structures composed of interpenetrating networks of donor and acceptor materials. This classification is used for inventions involving advanced organic photovoltaic or photoresponsive components.
[H10K 30/35] comprising inorganic nanostructures, e.g. CdSe nanoparticles -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers organic solid-state radiation-sensitive devices that incorporate inorganic nanostructures such as cadmium selenide (CdSe) nanoparticles to modify or improve their optical or electrical response. It is nested under electricity, semiconductor devices, organic electric solid-state devices, organic radiation-sensitive devices, and is identified by code H10K 30/35.
[H10K 30/352] the inorganic nanostructures being nanotubes or nanowires, e.g. CdTe nanotubes in P3HT polymer -- The subclass H10K 30/352 covers inorganic nanostructures-specifically nanotubes or nanowires-incorporated into organic radiation-sensitive devices, such as CdTe nanotubes dispersed within a P3HT polymer matrix for photoconductive or photovoltaic functions.
[H10K 30/353] comprising blocking layers, e.g. exciton blocking layers -- The code H10K 30/353 classifies organic radiation-sensitive devices that include one or more blocking layers, such as exciton-blocking layers, to control charge or energy transfer within the structure. These layers are used in organic light-emitting diodes, photovoltaic cells and similar semiconductor components to improve efficiency and stability.
[H10K 30/354] comprising a metal-insulator-semiconductor [m-i-s] structure -- H10K 30/354 classifies organic radiation-sensitive devices that incorporate a metal-insulator-semiconductor (MIS) structure, where a metal electrode, an insulating layer, and an organic semiconductor are stacked to form the active region for light detection or imaging. This entry falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic solid-state devices.
[H10K 30/40] comprising a p-i-n structure, e.g. having a perovskite absorber between p-type and n-type charge transport layers -- The code H10K 30/40 designates organic radiation-sensitive devices that employ a p-i-n configuration, wherein a perovskite absorber layer is positioned between p-type and n-type charge-transport layers. This structure is used primarily in thin-film solar cells and photodetectors to improve charge separation and collection efficiency.
[H10K 30/451] comprising a metal-semiconductor-metal [m-s-m] structure -- H10K 30/451 covers organic radiation-sensitive devices that incorporate a metal-semiconductor-metal configuration, where two metal electrodes contact an organic semiconductor layer to form a planar diode. This structure is employed in photodetectors, solar cells and light-sensing components that rely on organic materials to convert photons into electrical signals.
[H10K 30/50] Photovoltaic [PV] devices -- Photovoltaic devices that employ organic semiconductor materials to convert light into electrical energy are classified under H10K 30/50. The entry covers organic solar cells, thin-film organic photovoltaic modules and related structures that harvest solar radiation within the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 30/53] in the form of fibres or tubes, e.g. photovoltaic fibres -- This subclass covers organic solid-state devices such as photovoltaic or photosensor structures that are manufactured as fibers or tubular elements, enabling light-to-electric conversion along a flexible strand or tube. It includes fiber-based solar cells and similar organic optoelectronic components.
[H10K 30/57] comprising multiple junctions, e.g. tandem PV cells -- H10K 30/57 covers organic radiation-sensitive devices that incorporate two or more stacked or series-connected p-n junctions, such as tandem photovoltaic cells, to increase light-to-electricity conversion efficiency. The classification applies to any organic solid-state architecture where multiple active layers form distinct junctions within a single device.
[H10K 30/60] in which radiation controls flow of current through the devices, e.g. photoresistors -- This subclass covers organic solid-state semiconductor devices where incident light or other radiation modulates the electrical conductivity, such as photoresistors and similar photoconductive elements. It is part of organic radiation-sensitive devices within the broader semiconductor and electricity categories.
[H10K 30/65] Light-sensitive field-effect devices, e.g. phototransistors -- H10K 30/65 covers organic light-sensitive field-effect devices, notably phototransistors, where illumination modulates the channel current of a field-effect structure. The classification applies to devices that combine an organic semiconductor layer with a gate-controlled conduction path, enabling direct conversion of light signals into electrical output.
[H10K 30/671] Organic radiation-sensitive molecular electronic devices -- H10K 30/671 covers organic semiconductor devices that detect or are modulated by ionizing or non-ionizing radiation, where the active layer consists of molecular electronic components such as organic dyes, polymers or small molecules. It includes photodiodes, phototransistors, image sensors and other structures that convert radiation into electrical signals.
[H10K 30/80] Constructional details -- H10K 30/80 concerns the constructional details of organic radiation-sensitive devices, such as organic light-emitting diodes, photodiodes and solar cells. It covers the arrangement and materials of layers, electrodes, substrates, encapsulation and other structural features that affect the device's electrical and optical performance. This sub-class falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the broader semiconductor and electricity categories.
[H10K 30/81] Electrodes -- H10K 30/81 refers to electrodes employed in organic radiation-sensitive devices such as organic photodiodes, solar cells and light-emitting diodes. It covers the design, materials and configuration of charge-collecting or injecting contacts that interface with organic semiconductor layers, including transparent conductive oxides, metal grids and hybrid structures. This subclass lies within electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices → organic radiation-sensitive devices.
[H10K 30/82] Transparent electrodes, e.g. indium tin oxide [ITO] electrodes -- The H10K 30/82 subclass concerns transparent conductive electrodes employed in organic semiconductor structures, particularly those made of indium tin oxide (ITO) and similar materials. These electrodes provide electrical contact while allowing light to pass, enabling their use in organic photodetectors, solar cells and related radiation-sensitive devices.
[H10K 30/821] comprising carbon nanotubes -- H10K 30/821 refers to organic radiation-sensitive solid-state devices that incorporate carbon nanotubes as part of their structure or functional layers. The classification covers devices such as organic photodetectors, solar cells, or light-emitting elements where carbon nanotubes are used to enhance charge transport or optical properties.
[H10K 30/83] comprising arrangements for extracting the current from the cell, e.g. metal finger grid systems to reduce the serial resistance of transparent electrodes -- This CPC subclass covers designs in organic radiation-sensitive solid-state devices that incorporate structures such as metal finger grids to draw current from the cell and lower the series resistance of transparent electrodes. It resides under organic electric solid-state devices within the semiconductor field.
[H10K 30/84] Layers having high charge carrier mobility -- H10K 30/84 relates to organic radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices in which the active layers are engineered for high charge-carrier mobility, enabling rapid electron or hole transport. The subclass covers material choices, doping methods, crystalline orientation and structural designs that boost mobility in organic thin-film transistors, photodiodes and similar components.
[H10K 30/85] Layers having high electron mobility, e.g. electron-transporting layers or hole-blocking layers -- H10K 30/85 covers layers in organic radiation-sensitive devices that provide very high electron mobility, such as electron-transporting or hole-blocking layers. These layers are used to improve charge transport and efficiency in organic semiconductor structures like OLEDs and other thin-film devices.
[H10K 30/86] Layers having high hole mobility, e.g. hole-transporting layers or electron-blocking layers -- This CPC subclass covers layers in organic solid-state devices that are designed to provide high hole mobility, such as hole-transporting or electron-blocking layers. These layers improve charge transport and reduce recombination in organic radiation-sensitive devices like OLEDs and solar cells.
[H10K 30/865] Intermediate layers comprising a mixture of materials of the adjoining active layers -- H10K 30/865 relates to organic radiation-sensitive devices where an intermediate layer is formed from a mixture of the materials used in the neighboring active layers. The mixed layer is positioned between the active layers to improve charge transport, recombination or adhesion, and is characteristic of organic photodetectors, solar cells and similar devices.
[H10K 30/87] Light-trapping means -- H10K 30/87 refers to light-trapping means used in organic radiation-sensitive semiconductor devices. It covers structures or layers that increase the optical path within the active material, such as textured surfaces, micro-cavities, or reflective coatings, to improve the device's light absorption and efficiency.
[H10K 30/88] Passivation -- H10K 30/88 covers passivation methods for organic radiation-sensitive solid-state devices such as organic LEDs and photodiodes. It includes barrier layers, encapsulation and surface treatments that protect the organic semiconductor from moisture, oxygen and mechanical damage, thereby enhancing device stability and performance.
[H10K 30/89] Terminals, e.g. bond pads -- H10K 30/89 covers the design and arrangement of electrical terminals such as bond pads used to connect organic radiation-sensitive solid-state devices to external circuitry. It includes configurations, materials and fabrication methods that enable reliable electrical contact while preserving the device's optical performance.
[H10K 39/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one organic radiation-sensitive element covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Such as:
[H10K 39/10] Organic photovoltaic [PV] modules -- H10K 39/10 covers integrated devices or assemblies that include at least one organic radiation-sensitive element used to convert sunlight into electricity. These are organic photovoltaic (PV) modules in which the organic semiconductor layer is part of a broader electronic system, such as panels or power-generation units.
[H10K 39/12] Electrical configurations of PV cells, e.g. series connections or parallel connections -- H10K 39/12 refers to the CPC class for electrical configurations of photovoltaic cells, such as series or parallel connections, within integrated organic semiconductor devices that include at least one organic radiation-sensitive element. This falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 39/15] comprising both organic PV cells and inorganic PV cells -- This subclass covers integrated photovoltaic assemblies that combine at least one organic solar cell with one or more inorganic solar cells. Such devices are intended to exploit the complementary properties of organic and inorganic PV technologies within a single module or package. It falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 39/18] Interconnections, e.g. terminals -- H10K 39/18 defines the features related to interconnections such as terminals, bonding pads, and conductive pathways that link the components of integrated organic electronic devices or assemblies containing at least one organic radiation-sensitive element. It addresses how these elements are electrically connected within the overall semiconductor module.
[H10K 39/30] Devices controlled by radiation -- H10K 39/30 covers integrated semiconductor devices or assemblies that include at least one organic radiation-sensitive component and are operated or switched by incident radiation. The classification applies to circuits, displays, sensors or other electronic modules where the radiation directly controls functionality.
[H10K 39/32] Organic image sensors -- Organic image sensors are integrated or assembled semiconductor devices that include at least one organic radiation-sensitive element capable of converting light into electrical signals. The classification covers structures where the organic photodetector is combined with supporting circuitry or other components to form a functional imaging unit. These devices are used for applications such as digital cameras, optical sensors and light-detection systems that benefit from the flexibility and tunable properties of organic materials.
[H10K 39/34] integrated with organic light-emitting diodes [OLED] -- This subgroup covers integrated circuits or assemblies that incorporate at least one organic light-emitting diode as a functional element. It includes devices where OLEDs are combined with other semiconductor or electronic components to form a single package, such as displays, sensors, or lighting modules.
[H10K 39/36] Devices specially adapted for detecting X-ray radiation -- Devices that incorporate at least one organic radiation-sensitive element and are designed specifically to detect X-ray radiation, often as part of integrated assemblies that may include additional semiconductor components. The code covers organic solid-state detectors and related structures optimized for X-ray sensing applications.
[H10K 39/38] Interconnections, e.g. terminals -- The H10K 39/38 class concerns the electrical interconnections-such as terminals, wiring, or contact pads-used to link the components of integrated organic radiation-sensitive devices or assemblies that contain at least one such element. It addresses how these devices are connected to external circuits or to each other within the broader group of organic solid-state semiconductor assemblies.
[H10K 39/401] Integrated devices having a three-dimensional layout, e.g. 3D ICs -- H10K 39/401 covers integrated semiconductor structures that incorporate three-dimensional layouts, such as stacked or vertically interconnected organic radiation-sensitive components within a single device. These 3D ICs fall under the organic electric solid-state devices subclass of the broader electricity and semiconductor categories.
[H10K 39/501] Integrated devices comprising a common active layer -- H10K 39/501 covers integrated organic electronic devices in which two or more functional elements share a single, common active layer. Examples include circuits, sensors or display modules where the same organic semiconductor film performs multiple roles, such as light emission, detection, or charge transport, and the components are assembled together without separate active layers for each function.
[H10K 39/601] Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one organic radiation-sensitive element -- Groups H10K39/601 and H10K39/621 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10W90/00 . Groups H10W90/00 , H10K39/601 and H10K39/621 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10K 39/621] comprising only organic radiation-sensitive elements -- This CPC subclass H10K 39/621 covers integrated or assembled semiconductor devices in which all radiation-sensitive elements are organic, such as organic photodiodes, photovoltaic cells or phototransistors, and no inorganic radiation-sensitive components are present. The entry pertains to organic electric solid-state devices used for detection or conversion of electromagnetic radiation.
[H10K 50/00] Organic light-emitting devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Organic electrical-light transducers wherein an electrical input is converted to a light output, such as organic light-emitting diodes [OLED] or organic light-emitting transistors.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Organic integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices -> H10K59/00 , H10K65/00 - Organic semiconductor lasers -> H01S5/36
[H10K 50/10] OLEDs or polymer light-emitting diodes [PLED] -- H10K 50/10 relates to organic light-emitting devices, specifically OLEDs and polymer light-emitting diodes, covering their structures, organic materials and fabrication methods for use in displays and lighting.
[H10K 50/11] characterised by the electroluminescent [EL] layers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10K 50/11 pertains to organic light-emitting devices, specifically focusing on characteristics of the electroluminescent layers. This classification is used for patents detailing the composition, structure, and properties of these layers that emit light when an electric current passes through them.
[H10K 50/115] comprising active inorganic nanostructures, e.g. luminescent quantum dots -- This subclass covers organic light-emitting devices that incorporate active inorganic nanostructures, such as luminescent quantum dots, to modify or boost emission characteristics. It is positioned under electricity, semiconductor devices and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 50/12] comprising dopants -- The H10K 50/12 subclass covers organic light-emitting devices that contain dopants, such as guest molecules or inorganic additives, to modify electrical or optical performance. It falls under organic solid-state devices within the broader categories of semiconductor devices and electricity.
[H10K 50/121] for assisting energy transfer, e.g. sensitization -- The CPC subclass H10K 50/121 covers organic light-emitting devices that employ a component to assist energy transfer, such as sensitizers that absorb excitation energy and pass it to the emissive layer. It includes structures, materials and methods that improve device efficiency by facilitating exciton or charge transfer.
[H10K 50/125] specially adapted for multicolour light emission, e.g. for emitting white light -- This subclass covers organic light-emitting devices that are specially adapted to emit multiple colours, for example by mixing different emissive layers or structures to produce white light. It lies within the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices → organic light-emitting devices.
[H10K 50/13] comprising stacked EL layers within one EL unit -- The H10K 50/13 subclass covers organic light-emitting devices in which multiple electroluminescent layers are stacked together to form a single emitting unit, allowing combined or sequential emission from different organic layers. This structure is used to enhance colour balance, efficiency or lifetime in OLED panels and similar solid-state lighting components.
[H10K 50/131] with spacer layers between the electroluminescent layers -- H10K 50/131 covers organic light-emitting devices that incorporate one or more spacer layers separating the electroluminescent layers. The spacer layers are used to control charge transport, prevent quenching, and improve device uniformity and lifetime. This subclass falls under organic electric solid-state devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10K 50/135] comprising mobile ions -- This subclass covers organic light-emitting devices that contain mobile ions within the emissive layer or adjacent charge-transport layers. The presence of mobile ions allows ion migration under bias, which can adjust internal electric fields, improve charge injection, and modify emission characteristics. It is used for technologies such as ion-conducting organic LEDs and displays where ion-assisted electroluminescence is a key feature.
[H10K 50/14] Carrier transporting layers -- Carrier transporting layers are thin films within organic light-emitting devices that move electric charge-electrons or holes-toward the emissive zone, enabling efficient recombination and light generation. They are composed of organic or inorganic materials selected for high mobility, appropriate energy levels, and stability, and are positioned between the electrode and the emissive layer in the device stack.
[H10K 50/15] Hole transporting layers -- H10K 50/15 covers materials and structures that facilitate the movement of positive charge carriers (holes) from the anode toward the emissive layer in organic light-emitting diodes. These layers are typically organic semiconductors positioned between the anode and the emissive zone to improve charge balance, reduce driving voltage, and enhance overall device efficiency.
[H10K 50/155] comprising dopants -- This subclass concerns organic light-emitting devices whose organic layers contain one or more dopant substances to alter charge transport, emission wavelength, or efficiency, such as doped emissive or charge-transport layers in OLED structures.
[H10K 50/156] comprising a multilayered structure -- H10K 50/156 relates to organic light-emitting devices that include a multilayered structure, where distinct organic layers perform functions such as charge transport, emission, and blocking. The classification is applied to patents describing stacked organic films that enhance efficiency, colour stability or lifetime of OLEDs.
[H10K 50/157] between the light-emitting layer and the cathode -- H10K 50/157 pertains to organic light-emitting devices and specifically to the region or interface located between the light-emitting layer and the cathode. It includes structures, materials, treatments and methods that modify or utilize this interfacial zone to improve charge injection, stability or efficiency. The code is used for patents dealing with electrodes, buffer layers or interlayers positioned directly adjacent to the cathode in an OLED.
[H10K 50/16] Electron transporting layers -- Electron transporting layers are functional thin films used in organic light-emitting devices to conduct electrons from the cathode toward the emissive zone, improving charge balance and device efficiency. They are typically composed of low-LUMO organic semiconductors and may be doped to enhance conductivity, forming a key subcategory of organic solid-state devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10K 50/165] comprising dopants -- H10K 50/165 covers organic solid-state electroluminescent devices-such as organic light-emitting diodes-in which one or more dopants are incorporated to modify the material's electrical or optical properties, for example to adjust colour, boost efficiency or improve stability.
[H10K 50/166] comprising a multilayered structure -- H10K 50/166 pertains to organic light-emitting devices that employ a multilayered structure, wherein distinct functional layers such as emissive, charge-transport, and electrode layers are stacked to enhance device performance, efficiency, and stability.
[H10K 50/167] between the light-emitting layer and the anode -- This subgroup covers features, materials or structures positioned between the light-emitting layer and the anode in organic solid-state light-emitting devices. It includes hole-injection or hole-transport layers, interfacial modifiers and barrier films that affect charge injection, balance, or device stability.
[H10K 50/17] Carrier injection layers -- The CPC subclass H10K 50/17 covers carrier injection layers used in organic light-emitting devices, which belong to organic electric solid-state semiconductor technology. These layers facilitate the movement of electrons or holes from electrodes into the organic emissive material, improving device efficiency.
[H10K 50/171] Electron injection layers -- Electron injection layers are thin films that facilitate the flow of electrons from an electrode into the emissive region of organic light-emitting devices, improving charge balance and device efficiency. They are typically composed of low-work-function metals, conductive oxides, or doped organic materials and are positioned between the cathode and the organic active layer. This layer crucially reduces injection barriers and enhances the performance of solid-state organic semiconductor structures.
[H10K 50/18] Carrier blocking layers -- The CPC entry H10K 50/18 covers carrier-blocking layers used in organic light-emitting devices, such as OLEDs. These layers are positioned within the device structure to impede the flow of either electrons or holes, thereby confining charge carriers to the emissive region, enhancing recombination efficiency and prolonging device lifetime.
[H10K 50/181] Electron blocking layers -- Electron blocking layers are thin organic or inorganic films placed between the emissive layer and the cathode in an organic light-emitting device to impede the flow of electrons toward the anode, improve charge balance, and boost luminous efficiency by confining recombination within the emissive zone.
[H10K 50/182] OLED comprising a fiber structure -- The CPC subclass H10K 50/182 covers organic light-emitting diodes that incorporate a fiber structure such as fibers or textile substrates to create flexible or wearable OLEDs. It is located in the organic electric solid-state device group of semiconductor devices within the electricity field.
[H10K 50/19] Tandem OLEDs -- Tandem OLEDs are multilayer organic light-emitting devices where two or more emissive units are stacked and linked electrically through intermediate charge-generation layers to raise brightness, efficiency, and lifetime. The classification includes the structures, materials, and production methods that enable series-connected sub-cells within a single organic light-emitting package.
[H10K 50/30] Organic light-emitting transistors -- H10K 50/30 designates organic light-emitting transistors, devices that combine the switching function of a field-effect transistor with an organic electroluminescent layer that produces light during operation. The classification covers structures, fabrication methods, and performance characteristics of transistors where the channel or other active region is composed of organic light-emitting materials, used in displays, sensors, and optoelectronic circuits.
[H10K 50/401] Organic light-emitting molecular electronic devices -- A subclass of organic electric solid-state devices, H10K 50/401 covers molecular electronic structures that emit light when electrically excited. It includes devices such as organic light-emitting diodes used for displays and illumination, where organic compounds form the active light-generating layer. This entry sits within the broader categories of semiconductor devices and electricity.
[H10K 50/80] Constructional details -- H10K 50/80 relates to the constructional details of organic light-emitting devices, covering the physical arrangement of layers, substrates, electrodes, encapsulation methods and overall device architecture used to produce electroluminescence in organic semiconductors. It specifies structural features that affect performance, stability and integration of OLEDs.
[H10K 50/805] Electrodes -- H10K 50/805 relates to electrodes used in organic light-emitting devices, covering the materials, structures and configurations that inject or collect charge in OLEDs. It includes transparent conductive layers, work-function-adjusted compounds and electrode architectures that affect device efficiency and stability.
[H10K 50/81] Anodes -- H10K 50/81 refers to the anode component in organic light-emitting devices, covering its structure, materials and fabrication methods. It includes conductive layers such as transparent oxides or metal films that inject holes into the organic emissive layer, as well as related surface treatments and adhesion layers.
[H10K 50/813] characterised by their shape -- H10K 50/813 classifies organic light-emitting devices whose defining feature is the shape of the emitting structure or overall package, such as planar, spiral, pixelated or three-dimensional forms. The subclass groups patents that claim performance or manufacturing advantages resulting from these geometric configurations within the broader field of organic solid-state semiconductor devices.
[H10K 50/814] combined with auxiliary electrodes, e.g. ITO layer combined with metal lines -- H10K 50/814 covers organic light-emitting devices that are combined with auxiliary electrodes, such as an indium-tin-oxide (ITO) transparent layer integrated with metal lines. The classification applies to structures where the ITO layer serves as a primary electrode while metal lines provide additional routing or contact functions, improving current distribution and device performance.
[H10K 50/816] Multilayers, e.g. transparent multilayers -- H10K 50/816 covers multilayer constructions used in organic light-emitting devices, including transparent multilayers that combine different functional layers such as electrodes, emission layers, and charge-transport layers. The classification addresses the design and integration of stacked thin films to achieve desired optical and electrical properties in organic solid-state light sources.
[H10K 50/818] Reflective anodes, e.g. ITO combined with thick metallic layers -- In CPC subclass H10K 50/818, reflective anodes for organic solid-state light-emitting devices are defined; they combine a transparent conductive oxide such as indium tin oxide with a thick metallic layer to provide high conductivity and optical reflection, enhancing light out-coupling in OLEDs.
[H10K 50/82] Cathodes -- The H10K 50/82 subclass defines cathodes employed in organic light-emitting devices, covering the electrode that injects electrons into the organic emissive layer of OLEDs. It includes the materials, structures, and manufacturing methods of such cathodes within the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 50/822] characterised by their shape -- The H10K 50/822 subclass covers organic light-emitting devices that are defined primarily by their physical shape, such as planar, stacked, pixelated or other geometrical configurations that affect performance or integration. It groups OLED structures where the device's form factor, rather than material composition or electrical architecture, is the distinguishing feature.
[H10K 50/824] combined with auxiliary electrodes -- H10K 50/824 covers organic light-emitting devices that incorporate one or more auxiliary electrodes in addition to the primary anode and cathode, such as electrodes used for charge-balancing, modulation, or sensing functions, which can improve device efficiency, stability or enable additional control features.
[H10K 50/826] Multilayers, e.g. opaque multilayers -- This CPC subclass covers organic light-emitting devices that incorporate a stack of two or more layers, including opaque layers, to provide functions such as charge transport, light confinement, or barrier protection. It addresses the design and use of multilayer structures as integral parts of OLED architectures.
[H10K 50/828] Transparent cathodes, e.g. comprising thin metal layers -- H10K 50/828 covers transparent cathodes employed in organic light-emitting devices, such as OLEDs. It includes configurations where thin metal layers serve as the cathode while allowing light to pass through, enabling transparent or semi-transparent display and lighting applications. This subclass falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the broader field of semiconductor devices.
[H10K 50/84] Passivation -- H10K 50/84 - Passivation covers methods and materials for protecting organic light-emitting devices from degradation, typically by applying barrier or encapsulation layers that shield the organic semiconductor from moisture, oxygen, and other environmental factors, thereby enhancing device lifetime and performance.
[H10K 50/841] Self-supporting sealing arrangements -- Self-supporting sealing arrangements for organic light-emitting devices provide a protective barrier that maintains the device's integrity without needing an additional frame or substrate. The seal is formed from a solid-state material that adheres to the OLED's encapsulation surfaces and remains mechanically stable, preventing ingress of moisture or oxygen while preserving optical performance. This subclass covers designs, compositions and methods for such autonomous seals in organic solid-state semiconductor packages.
[H10K 50/842] Containers -- The H10K 50/842 entry covers containers specifically designed for organic light-emitting devices, such as OLEDs. It includes housings, packaging, or storage units that protect the organic semiconductor layers and maintain their performance during handling, transport, or integration into electronic systems.
[H10K 50/8423] Metallic sealing arrangements -- Metallic sealing arrangements for organic light-emitting devices comprise metal-based methods that hermetically enclose the organic semiconductor layers, preventing ingress of moisture and oxygen that degrade performance. Such seals may involve metal films, clamps, pins or soldered joints applied to the device package to maintain long-term stability and reliability.
[H10K 50/8426] Peripheral sealing arrangements, e.g. adhesives, sealants -- H10K 50/8426 covers peripheral sealing arrangements such as adhesives and sealants used in organic light-emitting devices. It falls under electricity->semiconductor devices->organic electric solid-state devices->organic light-emitting devices and addresses protecting the edges of OLED panels from moisture and oxygen ingress.
[H10K 50/8428] Vertical spacers, e.g. arranged between the sealing arrangement and the OLED -- H10K 50/8428 covers vertical spacer elements used in organic light-emitting devices. These spacers are positioned between the sealing arrangement and the OLED to maintain a defined gap and mechanical stability.
[H10K 50/844] Encapsulations -- H10K 50/844 covers encapsulation techniques for organic light-emitting devices. It includes the application of barrier layers, sealing structures and protective coatings that shield the organic emissive material from moisture, oxygen and mechanical damage, thereby extending device lifetime and performance.
[H10K 50/8445] multilayered coatings having a repetitive structure, e.g. having multiple organic-inorganic bilayers -- H10K 50/8445 covers multilayered coatings that consist of alternating organic and inorganic layers forming a repetitive bilayer structure, used especially in organic light-emitting devices. Such coatings provide tailored optical, electrical and barrier properties by stacking thin films in a controlled sequence.
[H10K 50/846] comprising getter material or desiccants -- H10K 50/846 pertains to organic light-emitting devices that incorporate getter materials or desiccants within the structure to absorb moisture or unwanted gases, thereby enhancing device stability and longevity. This subclass falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices → organic light-emitting devices.
[H10K 50/85] Arrangements for extracting light from the devices -- H10K 50/85 concerns structures and methods that improve or direct the emission of light from organic light-emitting devices, including lenses, outcoupling layers, scattering films, waveguide or prism arrangements that guide photons out of the organic stack.
[H10K 50/852] comprising a resonant cavity structure, e.g. Bragg reflector pair -- H10K 50/852 covers organic light-emitting devices that incorporate a resonant cavity structure, such as a pair of Bragg reflectors, to enhance light extraction or spectral control within the OLED architecture. This sub-class falls under organic electric solid-state devices in the broader electricity and semiconductor device categories.
[H10K 50/854] comprising scattering means -- H10K 50/854 designates organic light-emitting devices that incorporate scattering means to redirect or diffuse the emitted light, improving extraction efficiency or uniformity. The scattering elements may be particles, microstructures or patterned layers integrated within or on the device, and are applied to OLEDs and related solid-state emitters.
[H10K 50/856] comprising reflective means -- H10K 50/856 designates organic light-emitting devices that include a reflective element, such as a mirror or metal layer, positioned to redirect emitted light toward the viewer and enhance extraction efficiency. The classification applies to OLED structures where the reflective means is an integral part of the device architecture.
[H10K 50/858] comprising refractive means, e.g. lenses -- H10K 50/858 relates to organic light-emitting devices that include refractive elements such as lenses to shape, direct or focus the emitted light. The subclass addresses solid-state organic LEDs where optical components are integrated to improve brightness, beam pattern, coupling efficiency or other light-management functions.
[H10K 50/86] Arrangements for improving contrast, e.g. preventing reflection of ambient light -- The H10K 50/86 subclass covers design features in organic LEDs that enhance display contrast by reducing ambient light reflections, such as anti-reflective coatings, surface texturing, or optical structures. It pertains to organic solid-state light-emitting devices.
[H10K 50/865] comprising light absorbing layers, e.g. light-blocking layers -- H10K 50/865 concerns organic light-emitting devices that incorporate one or more light-absorbing (light-blocking) layers, typically positioned to shield the emissive region from external illumination, improve contrast, or regulate internal light distribution within the device. This subclass falls under organic electric solid-state devices in the broader semiconductor and electricity categories.
[H10K 50/868] Arrangements for polarized light emission  H10K50/86  takes precedence -- Arrangements for polarized light emission concern OLED structures designed to emit light with a defined polarization state, using specialized electrode patterns, anisotropic organic layers, or integrated optical elements such as gratings or polarizers. These configurations improve display contrast, efficiency, or enable sensing applications.
[H10K 50/87] Arrangements for heating or cooling -- The subclass H10K 50/87 covers methods and structures that provide heating or cooling for organic light-emitting devices. It includes thermal management features such as integrated heaters, coolers, heat-spreading layers or temperature-control circuits designed to maintain optimal operation and extend the lifetime of OLED or similar solid-state emitters.
[H10K 50/88] Terminals, e.g. bond pads -- The CPC entry H10K 50/88 covers the terminals that provide electrical connections to organic light-emitting devices, such as the bond pads or contact pads on OLED or similar organic solid-state displays. It includes structures and layouts used to attach leads, wires or interconnects to the active organic layers.
[H10K 59/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one organic light-emitting element covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrated devices that comprise at least one organic light-emitting component;  Assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one organic light-emitting device.  Examples include:  H10K59/10 covers only OLED displays, whereas H10K59/30 - H10K59/90 cover both displays and non-displays.  **Glossary:** - OLED display: Organic light emitting diode display - TOLED display: Transparent OLED display - AMOLED display: Active matrix OLED display - PMOLED display: Passive matrix OLED display - OTFT array: Organic thin film transistor array - TFT array: Thin film transistor array - CCM: Colour changing medium - RGB: Red Green Blue - RGBW: Red Green Blue White
[H10K 59/10] OLED displays -- H10K 59/10 pertains to integrated organic light-emitting diode (OLED) display modules that combine one or more OLED elements into a single device or assembly for visual output. The entry belongs to the organic electric solid-state devices subgroup of semiconductor technology.
[H10K 59/12] Active-matrix OLED [AMOLED] displays -- H10K 59/12 covers active-matrix organic light-emitting diode (AMOLED) displays, which are integrated semiconductor assemblies that use an active matrix of thin-film transistors to drive individual organic light-emitting pixels. The classification falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the broader electricity and semiconductor device categories.
[H10K 59/1201] Manufacture or treatment -- H10K 59/1201 relates to processes for producing or treating integrated organic electric solid-state devices that contain at least one organic light-emitting component. It includes methods such as material deposition, patterning, encapsulation, annealing and other post-fabrication treatments used to build or improve OLED modules and similar assemblies.
[H10K 59/121] characterised by the geometry or disposition of pixel elements -- This subclass concerns integrated organic light-emitting devices whose defining feature is the geometry or disposition of the pixel elements, such as shape, spacing, pattern or arrangement within the display. It applies to solid-state organic devices that incorporate at least one OLED element and are classified under H10K 59/121.
[H10K 59/1213] the pixel elements being TFTs -- The CPC subclass H10K 59/1213 pertains to integrated organic solid-state devices in which each pixel incorporates a thin-film transistor that drives at least one organic light-emitting element, such as an OLED. It covers the configuration of the pixel circuitry and the TFT-based driving arrangement within organic semiconductor display assemblies.
[H10K 59/1216] the pixel elements being capacitors -- H10K 59/1216 refers to pixel elements that function as capacitors within integrated organic light-emitting devices. It falls under the CPC hierarchy ELECTRICITY → SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES → ORGANIC ELECTRIC SOLID-STATE DEVICES, specifically for assemblies containing at least one organic LED element where the pixel's capacitive structure is part of the device.
[H10K 59/122] Pixel-defining structures or layers, e.g. banks -- H10K 59/122 covers pixel-defining structures or layers, such as banks, employed in integrated organic light-emitting devices to separate, confine or shape individual display pixels.
[H10K 59/123] Connection of the pixel electrodes to the thin film transistors [TFT] -- This CPC entry H10K 59/123 covers methods and structures for linking the pixel electrode of an organic light-emitting display to its driving thin-film transistor. It belongs to the hierarchy electricity, semiconductor devices, organic electric solid-state devices and concerns integrated assemblies that incorporate at least one organic light-emitting element. The classification addresses the electrical interconnect layout, contact formation and routing between the electrode and TFT.
[H10K 59/124] Insulating layers formed between TFT elements and OLED elements -- The class covers insulating layers that are placed between thin-film-transistor (TFT) structures and organic light-emitting diode (OLED) elements in integrated organic solid-state devices. These layers electrically separate the TFT driving circuitry from the light-emitting pixels, reducing crosstalk, protecting components, and enhancing device stability.
[H10K 59/125] including organic TFTs [OTFT] -- The class H10K 59/125 covers integrated organic solid-state assemblies that include at least one organic thin-film transistor (OTFT). It is located under electricity, semiconductor devices, organic electric solid-state devices and pertains to circuits or modules where OTFTs are combined with other organic optoelectronic elements such as OLEDs.
[H10K 59/126] Shielding, e.g. light-blocking means over the TFTs -- H10K 59/126 covers devices in which the thin-film transistors of an organic solid-state assembly are protected by a shielding or light-blocking layer, such as a mask or opaque coating, to prevent unwanted illumination or electrical interference. This subgroup falls under organic light-emitting devices that integrate multiple semiconductor components.
[H10K 59/127] comprising two substrates, e.g. display comprising OLED array and TFT driving circuitry on different substrates -- H10K 59/127 refers to integrated organic light-emitting devices in which the light-emitting layer and the thin-film transistor driving circuitry are formed on two separate substrates, such as a display that combines an OLED array on one panel with TFT drivers on another.
[H10K 59/1275] Electrical connections of the two substrates -- This subclass covers structures, methods, or materials that provide an electrical link between the two substrates of an organic solid-state device such as an organic light-emitting panel. It includes conductive adhesives, vias, interconnection layers, and other means for joining the front and back plates to supply power or signals.
[H10K 59/128] comprising two independent displays, e.g. for emitting information from two major sides of the display -- H10K 59/128 covers integrated organic light-emitting devices that contain two independent displays, allowing information to be shown on opposite faces of the same panel. The classification applies to organic solid-state assemblies where each side functions as a separate visual output, such as dual-sided OLED screens used in signage or wearable displays.
[H10K 59/129] Chiplets -- H10K 59/129 covers chiplets, which are small, discrete semiconductor blocks that can be assembled into larger integrated or heterogeneous modules and may incorporate at least one organic light-emitting element, enabling compact organic solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/13] comprising photosensors that control luminance -- Subgroup H10K 59/13 covers integrated organic solid-state assemblies that contain at least one OLED element together with one or more photosensors that automatically adjust the device's luminance in response to ambient light.
[H10K 59/131] Interconnections, e.g. wiring lines or terminals -- H10K 59/131 covers the interconnections - wiring lines, terminals and related contact structures used to link the individual elements of integrated organic light-emitting devices or assemblies thereof. The subclass addresses the design, layout and material aspects of these connections within the broader group of organic solid-state semiconductor devices.
[H10K 59/1315] comprising structures specially adapted for lowering the resistance -- This CPC group covers integrated organic solid-state devices, especially OLED assemblies, that incorporate design features such as conductive pathways, doped layers or specialised contacts intended to lower the overall electrical resistance. It addresses structures specifically adapted to reduce resistance within the device.
[H10K 59/17] Passive-matrix OLED displays -- Passive-matrix OLED displays are flat-panel display assemblies that incorporate at least one organic light-emitting element and use a matrix of intersecting row and column conductors to address pixels without active switching devices. The classification covers the design, fabrication and operation of these integrated organic semiconductor devices for visual output.
[H10K 59/173] comprising banks or shadow masks -- The CPC subclass H10K 59/173 relates to integrated or assembled organic light-emitting devices that employ banks or shadow masks to define, isolate or pattern the emitting regions. These structures are used to control the placement of organic LEDs within a module or panel.
[H10K 59/176] comprising two independent displays, e.g. for emitting information from two major sides of the display -- H10K 59/176 classifies integrated organic solid-state devices that incorporate two independent display sections, each capable of emitting information from opposite or multiple major sides of the unit, such as dual-sided OLED panels used for bidirectional visual output.
[H10K 59/179] Interconnections, e.g. wiring lines or terminals -- This subclass addresses the wiring and terminal connections that join organic light-emitting elements to each other or to external circuitry in integrated organic solid-state devices. It includes conductive lines, busbars, bond pads and related interconnection structures that provide electrical continuity and routing.
[H10K 59/1795] comprising structures specially adapted for lowering the resistance -- H10K 59/1795 classifies integrated organic light-emitting devices or assemblies that include specially designed structures to lower electrical resistance, thereby improving efficiency and performance of the organic semiconductor components. The subclass applies to configurations where resistance-reducing features are adapted to the organic light-emitting elements.
[H10K 59/18] Tiled displays -- The H10K 59/18 class covers integrated assemblies of organic electronic devices that include at least one organic light-emitting element arranged as tiled display modules. It addresses the configuration, interconnection and packaging of multiple OLED tiles that together form a larger, seamless visual display, including the driving electronics and methods for aligning the individual tiles.
[H10K 59/19] Segment displays -- H10K 59/19 covers segment displays that incorporate at least one organic light-emitting element within an integrated or assembled device. Each segment can be individually energized to form alphanumeric characters or simple graphics, and the devices are solid-state organic semiconductor components. The entry resides under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/221] Static displays, e.g. displaying permanent logos -- H10K 59/221 covers organic-light-emitting integrated modules or assemblies that function as static displays, such as panels showing permanent logos or symbols. The claim is limited to devices where at least one organic electroluminescent element is incorporated and the displayed image does not change dynamically. This falls under CPC sections for electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/30] Devices specially adapted for multicolour light emission -- H10K 59/30 concerns integrated organic semiconductor devices that are specially adapted to emit light in more than one colour, such as OLED panels or lighting modules that combine different organic emitters or structural zones to produce multiple wavelengths.
[H10K 59/32] Stacked devices having two or more layers, each emitting at different wavelengths -- A stacked organic light-emitting device comprises at least two distinct emissive layers, each designed to emit light at a different wavelength, enabling multi-color or white emission from a single package. The layers are typically arranged in a sequence that allows independent electrical excitation while sharing common electrodes, improving color balance and efficiency without the need for separate chips. This structure belongs to the H10K 59/32 subclass of organic solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/35] comprising red-green-blue [RGB] subpixels -- The CPC subclass H10K 59/35 relates to integrated or assembled organic solid-state devices that incorporate at least one organic light-emitting element and include distinct red, green and blue (RGB) subpixels. It covers OLED or similar organic display structures where the three primary colour subpixels are combined within a single device or module to produce full-colour images.
[H10K 59/351] comprising more than three subpixels, e.g. red-green-blue-white [RGBW] -- This classification covers integrated organic solid-state devices that contain more than three subpixels, such as red-green-blue-white (RGBW) arrangements, where at least one subpixel is an organic light-emitting element.
[H10K 59/352] the areas of the RGB subpixels being different -- H10K 59/352 covers integrated organic light-emitting devices in which the red, green and blue subpixels have differing areas, a design used to balance colour brightness and efficiency in OLED displays. It is classified under electricity, semiconductor devices and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/353] characterised by the geometrical arrangement of the RGB subpixels -- The H10K 59/353 entry pertains to organic light-emitting device assemblies in which the defining feature is the specific geometric layout of red, green and blue subpixels. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices → integrated devices containing at least one OLED element, and focuses on how the RGB pixel arrangement is configured.
[H10K 59/38] comprising colour filters or colour changing media [CCM] -- H10K 59/38 covers organic solid-state light-emitting devices, such as OLED modules, that incorporate colour-filter or colour-changing media within the integrated assembly to modify or select the emitted hue. It pertains to designs where the filtering or shifting layer is part of the device structure rather than a separate external element.
[H10K 59/40] OLEDs integrated with touch screens -- The H10K 59/40 class covers integrated organic light-emitting diode (OLED) devices that incorporate a touch-sensing function, such as OLED display panels combined with capacitive or resistive touch layers. It applies to assemblies where the OLED light-emitting element and the touch-screen circuitry are fabricated as a single unit.
[H10K 59/50] OLEDs integrated with light modulating elements, e.g. with electrochromic elements, photochromic elements or liquid crystal elements -- H10K 59/50 covers integrated organic light-emitting diode (OLED) assemblies that combine the light-emitting element with one or more light-modulating components, such as electrochromic, photochromic or liquid-crystal elements, enabling dynamic control of colour, transparency or brightness. This subclass falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the broader electricity-semiconductor hierarchy.
[H10K 59/60] OLEDs integrated with inorganic light-sensitive elements, e.g. with inorganic solar cells or inorganic photodiodes -- H10K 59/60 covers organic light-emitting diode (OLED) modules that incorporate inorganic light-sensitive components such as solar cells or photodiodes, forming integrated optoelectronic devices. The entry falls under the hierarchy electricity - semiconductor devices - organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/65] OLEDs integrated with inorganic image sensors -- H10K 59/65 covers integrated devices or assemblies that combine at least one organic light-emitting diode (OLED) element with an inorganic image-sensor component, forming a single functional unit within the broader fields of electricity, semiconductor devices, and organic electric solid-state devices.
[H10K 59/70] OLEDs integrated with inorganic light-emitting elements, e.g. with inorganic electroluminescent elements -- This classification covers integrated devices that combine at least one organic light-emitting diode with inorganic electroluminescent components such as LEDs or other inorganic light-emitting elements, forming a hybrid light-emitting assembly. It falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the semiconductor device hierarchy.
[H10K 59/751] Integrated devices having a three-dimensional layout, e.g. 3D ICs -- This CPC subclass H10K 59/751 covers integrated semiconductor assemblies that incorporate a three-dimensional architecture, such as stacked or vertically interconnected chips, and include at least one organic light-emitting component. The classification falls under organic electric solid-state devices within the broader semiconductor and electricity categories.
[H10K 59/771] Integrated devices comprising a common active layer -- H10K 59/771 covers integrated devices or assemblies that contain multiple organic semiconductor components sharing a single active layer, such as a common organic light-emitting layer that also functions as a charge-transport or photodetector region. The classification applies to structures where the same organic film performs at least two distinct electronic or optoelectronic functions within one package.
[H10K 59/80] Constructional details -- This subclass relates to the structural and packaging aspects of integrated devices or assemblies that include at least one organic light-emitting element, such as OLED modules, within organic electric solid-state devices. It covers features like substrate arrangement, interconnections, encapsulation, and other constructional details that enable the operation of the organic light-emitting components.
[H10K 59/805] Electrodes -- The H10K 59/805 subclass concerns the electrodes employed in integrated organic light-emitting devices, covering anodes, cathodes and any contact layers that interface with the organic semiconductor. It addresses electrode materials, structures, deposition methods and patterning techniques used to optimise electrical connection and light-extraction in OLED assemblies.
[H10K 59/8051] Anodes -- H10K 59/8051 covers anodes used in integrated organic solid-state devices, particularly assemblies that include at least one organic light-emitting element. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices, and addresses the design and function of the electrode that injects charge carriers into the organic layers.
[H10K 59/80515] characterised by their shape -- H10K 59/80515 is in the CPC class for organic electric solid-state devices and pertains to integrated or assembled devices that contain at least one organic light-emitting element, where the distinguishing feature of the claim is the shape of the component or assembly.
[H10K 59/80516] combined with auxiliary electrodes, e.g. ITO layer combined with metal lines -- This sub-group covers integrated organic light-emitting assemblies that incorporate auxiliary electrodes, such as a transparent indium-tin-oxide (ITO) layer together with metal interconnects. The classification applies to devices where the ITO and metal lines are combined to provide electrical contact or routing within the organic solid-state component.
[H10K 59/80517] Multilayers, e.g. transparent multilayers -- The H10K 59/80517 entry covers multilayer structures, including transparent multilayers, used in integrated organic solid-state devices such as organic light-emitting components. It addresses the design and arrangement of stacked layers that improve optical or electrical performance in these semiconductor assemblies.
[H10K 59/80518] Reflective anodes, e.g. ITO combined with thick metallic layers -- The H10K 59/80518 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers reflective anodes used in organic light-emitting devices, where a transparent conductive material such as indium-tin-oxide is combined with a thick metallic layer to provide both high electrical conductivity and enhanced reflectivity, typically for bottom-emitting OLED assemblies.
[H10K 59/8052] Cathodes -- H10K 59/8052 covers cathodes used in integrated organic electric solid-state devices that contain at least one organic light-emitting element. It pertains to the design, structure, materials or fabrication of the negative electrode within such semiconductor assemblies, addressing functions such as charge injection, transport or extraction.
[H10K 59/80521] characterised by their shape -- This CPC subgroup concerns integrated or assembled organic solid-state devices that include at least one organic light-emitting element and are distinguished chiefly by their geometric configuration, such as specific curvature, layering, or three-dimensional form, rather than by material composition or electrical function.
[H10K 59/80522] combined with auxiliary electrodes -- H10K 59/80522 covers integrated or multi-device assemblies that contain at least one organic light-emitting element and also incorporate auxiliary electrodes for functions such as driving, sensing or charge balance. The classification falls within the CPC hierarchy for electricity, semiconductor devices, organic electric solid-state devices, and integrated devices.
[H10K 59/80523] Multilayers, e.g. opaque multilayers -- This subclass covers multilayer constructions, including opaque multilayers, employed in integrated organic light-emitting devices and other organic solid-state components. It includes stacked combinations of organic semiconductor layers, electrodes, barrier or encapsulation layers that together form the functional stack of an OLED assembly.
[H10K 59/80524] Transparent cathodes, e.g. comprising thin metal layers -- The H10K 59/80524 entry covers organic light-emitting assemblies that employ a transparent cathode, typically formed from one or more ultra-thin metal layers that let light pass through the electrode. Such cathodes enable top-emitting OLED structures used in displays and illumination panels where light must exit the device via the cathode side.
[H10K 59/82] Interconnections, e.g. terminals -- H10K 59/82 covers the interconnections such as terminals used in integrated semiconductor assemblies that include at least one organic light-emitting element. The code falls under the hierarchy electricity - semiconductor devices - organic electric solid-state devices. It addresses how components are linked electrically within these organic optoelectronic modules.
[H10K 59/84] Parallel electrical configurations of multiple OLEDs -- The code H10K 59/84 covers configurations where two or more organic light-emitting diodes are electrically connected in parallel, allowing them to be driven by the same voltage while sharing current. It relates to integrated organic solid-state devices such as displays or lighting modules that use parallel OLED arrays to improve brightness, uniformity or redundancy.
[H10K 59/86] Series electrical configurations of multiple OLEDs -- H10K 59/86 pertains to the arrangement of several organic light-emitting diodes connected in series within an integrated device. It includes the design of series-linked electrode structures, voltage distribution and drive circuitry that ensure uniform current flow across multiple OLED elements. The classification is used for patents on series electrical configurations of OLED arrays in solid-state lighting or display modules.
[H10K 59/87] Passivation; Containers; Encapsulations -- H10K 59/87 covers the protective measures applied to organic solid-state devices such as OLEDs, including passivation layers, packaging methods, and encapsulation techniques that shield the organic light-emitting elements from moisture, oxygen, and mechanical damage. It addresses materials, structures, and processes used to create sealed or barriered containers that maintain device performance and longevity. This subclass falls under integrated organic electric solid-state devices in the broader electricity-semiconductor hierarchy.
[H10K 59/871] Self-supporting sealing arrangements -- H10K 59/871 covers self-supporting sealing arrangements used in integrated organic light-emitting devices, where the seal itself provides structural rigidity and hermetic protection for the organic components. Such arrangements are designed to maintain device performance while simplifying assembly.
[H10K 59/872] Containers -- The H10K 59/872 class refers to containers that house or protect organic solid-state devices, such as OLEDs or other organic light-emitting elements, within integrated assemblies. It includes packaging, casings, modules or other enclosures designed to support, seal or transport these devices.
[H10K 59/8721] Metallic sealing arrangements -- H10K 59/8721 covers metallic sealing arrangements for integrated organic light-emitting devices. The code includes methods and structures that use metal components such as frames, caps or bonded edges to provide hermetic protection and mechanical stability for the organic layers within assemblies.
[H10K 59/8722] Peripheral sealing arrangements, e.g. adhesives, sealants -- H10K 59/8722 covers peripheral sealing arrangements such as adhesives and sealants used to protect integrated organic light-emitting devices or assemblies. The sealing protects device edges from moisture, oxygen and mechanical stress, ensuring stable operation and longevity. This falls within organic electric solid-state devices in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10K 59/8723] Vertical spacers, e.g. arranged between the sealing arrangement and the OLED -- H10K 59/8723 relates to organic light-emitting diode (OLED) modules that incorporate vertical spacer elements positioned between the sealing arrangement and the OLED layer to maintain a defined gap, improve mechanical stability, and prevent deformation of the encapsulation.
[H10K 59/873] Encapsulations -- H10K 59/873 covers the encapsulation of integrated organic semiconductor assemblies that include at least one organic light-emitting element. It includes protective layers, barrier films, sealing structures or methods that shield the devices from moisture, oxygen or mechanical damage. The classification is used for patents describing packaging, encapsulants or coating techniques for organic LED modules.
[H10K 59/8731] multilayered coatings having a repetitive structure, e.g. having multiple organic-inorganic bilayers -- The code H10K 59/8731 refers to multilayered coatings with a repetitive structure, typically composed of alternating organic-inorganic bilayers, used in integrated semiconductor assemblies that contain at least one organic light-emitting element. These repeated layers can enhance charge transport, barrier performance or optical properties in organic solid-state devices such as OLEDs.
[H10K 59/874] including getter material or desiccant -- The CPC subclass H10K 59/874 relates to integrated organic light-emitting devices or assemblies that incorporate a getter material or desiccant to absorb moisture or gases, thereby improving reliability and lifespan. It belongs to the hierarchy electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices → integrated devices with at least one OLED element.
[H10K 59/875] Arrangements for extracting light from the devices -- H10K 59/875 covers structures and methods that guide or emit light out of organic solid-state devices such as OLEDs, including lenses, waveguides, scattering layers, reflective or diffusive components, and other optical arrangements designed to improve light extraction efficiency from the device.
[H10K 59/876] comprising a resonant cavity structure, e.g. Bragg reflector pair -- The CPC subclass H10K 59/876 covers integrated organic light-emitting assemblies that incorporate a resonant cavity structure, such as a pair of Bragg reflectors, to improve optical performance. It applies to devices where the cavity forms part of the electrical and optical architecture, enabling enhanced emission control and efficiency.
[H10K 59/877] comprising scattering means -- H10K 59/877 concerns integrated organic light-emitting devices that incorporate scattering means, such as diffusers, gratings, particulate layers or other structures that redirect or spread emitted light. The subclass is used for devices where these scattering elements are part of the assembly to improve light distribution or viewing angle.
[H10K 59/878] comprising reflective means -- The CPC class H10K 59/878 covers integrated or multi-device assemblies that include at least one organic light-emitting element and incorporate reflective means, such as mirrors or reflective layers, to enhance light extraction or direction. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices, focusing on structures that combine organic LEDs with built-in reflectors for improved performance.
[H10K 59/879] comprising refractive means, e.g. lenses -- H10K 59/879 pertains to integrated organic electroluminescent devices that incorporate optical components such as lenses or other refractive elements to shape, focus or direct the emitted light. The subclass covers assemblies where at least one OLED element is combined with such refractive means, improving light distribution or coupling to external optics. This falls under the broader hierarchy of electricity-based semiconductor devices and organic solid-state light sources.
[H10K 59/8791] Arrangements for improving contrast, e.g. preventing reflection of ambient light -- H10K 59/8791 pertains to organic solid-state devices, especially OLED assemblies, that incorporate features to enhance visual contrast by reducing ambient-light reflection. Such arrangements include anti-reflection coatings, light-shield layers, diffusers or structures that block or redirect external light, thereby improving display readability.
[H10K 59/8792] comprising light absorbing layers, e.g. black layers -- The CPC subclass H10K 59/8792 belongs to the hierarchy electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices → integrated devices or assemblies of multiple devices with at least one organic light-emitting element. It specifically pertains to configurations that incorporate light-absorbing, for example black, layers within the organic optoelectronic structure.
[H10K 59/8793] Arrangements for polarized light emission  H10K59/8791  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code H10K 59/8793 pertains to arrangements for polarized light emission in organic electric solid-state devices, specifically integrated devices or assemblies that include at least one organic light-emitting element. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on semiconductor and organic device technologies.
[H10K 59/8794] Arrangements for heating and cooling -- H10K 59/8794 pertains to the design of heating and cooling systems integrated with organic light-emitting elements in semiconductor assemblies, specifically within the broader category of organic electric solid-state devices. This subclass addresses thermal management solutions for combined organic LED structures used in electronic applications.
[H10K 59/88] Dummy elements, i.e. elements having non-functional features -- H10K 59/88 covers dummy elements, which are parts incorporated into organic light-emitting assemblies that do not perform an active electrical function but are used for mechanical support, alignment, heat sinking or aesthetic purposes.
[H10K 59/90] Assemblies of multiple devices comprising at least one organic light-emitting element -- Groups H10K59/90 and H10K59/95 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10W90/00 . Groups H10W90/00 , H10K59/90 and H10K59/95 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10K 59/95] wherein all light-emitting elements are organic, e.g. assembled OLED displays -- H10K 59/95 classifies integrated or assembled semiconductor devices in which every light-emitting element is organic, covering configurations such as OLED displays that combine multiple organic light-emitting components into a single unit.
[H10K 65/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one organic light-emitting element and at least one organic radiation-sensitive element, e.g. organic opto-couplers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrated devices comprising at least one organic light-emitting component and at least one organic radiation-sensitive component, e.g. organic optocouplers;  Assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one organic light-emitting device and at least one organic radiation-sensitive device, e.g. organic optocouplers  Example:  The example shows a combination of an organic phototransistor with an OLED.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Organic image sensors integrated with organic light-emitting diodes -> H10K39/34 - OLED displays integrated with photosensors -> H10K59/13
[H10K 71/00] Manufacture or treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus specially adapted for the formation of organic solid-state devices, including the formation, patterning or treatment of the organic materials used in active layers, in layers having high carrier mobility or in electrodes of an organic solid-state device.
[H10K 71/10] Deposition of organic active material -- H10K 71/10 covers processes for depositing organic active materials onto semiconductor substrates, typically used in organic solid-state devices such as OLEDs and organic solar cells. The classification includes methods such as spin coating, inkjet printing, vapor deposition, and other techniques that form the active layers of the device.
[H10K 71/12] using liquid deposition, e.g. spin coating -- H10K 71/12 covers manufacturing or treatment methods for organic solid-state electronic devices in which the functional layers are applied by liquid-deposition techniques such as spin coating. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → organic electric solid-state devices, focusing on the deposition step that creates the active organic film.
[H10K 71/125] using electrolytic deposition e.g. in-situ electropolymerisation -- The code H10K 71/125 covers manufacturing or treatment steps for organic solid-state electric devices that employ electrolytic deposition, such as in-situ electropolymerisation, to form polymer layers directly on a substrate. It pertains to processes where an electric current induces polymer formation, enabling the creation or modification of semiconductor structures within organic electronic components.
[H10K 71/13] using printing techniques, e.g. ink-jet printing or screen printing -- H10K 71/13 covers manufacturing of organic solid-state electric devices by applying printing methods such as ink-jet or screen printing to deposit the organic semiconductor layers. The approach enables patterned formation of functional films for components like organic LEDs, solar cells or transistors.
[H10K 71/135] using ink-jet printing -- This subclass covers methods of producing or treating organic solid-state electronic devices-such as organic LEDs, solar cells, or transistors-by using ink-jet printing to deposit functional organic inks onto a substrate. It includes processes that pattern, layer or otherwise form organic semiconductor structures with an ink-jet head, enabling precise, material-efficient fabrication of organic electronic components.
[H10K 71/15] characterised by the solvent used -- H10K 71/15 covers manufacturing or treatment steps for organic electric solid-state devices in which the specific solvent used is the defining feature, such as in film formation, cleaning, or doping processes. It resides under Electricity → Semiconductor Devices → Organic Electric Solid-State Devices → Manufacture or treatment.
[H10K 71/16] using physical vapour deposition [PVD], e.g. vacuum deposition or sputtering -- This CPC subclass H10K 71/16 falls under electricity, semiconductor devices and organic electric solid-state devices, specifically the manufacture or treatment of such devices. It covers the use of physical vapour deposition methods such as vacuum deposition or sputtering to form organic solid-state layers.
[H10K 71/162] using laser ablation -- The H10K 71/162 subclass covers the manufacture or treatment of organic solid-state electronic devices in which laser ablation is employed to remove material, pattern, or shape organic semiconductor layers.
