[H10N 60/00] Superconducting devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Superconducting devices, such as:  This place also covers aspects such as the structure, active materials and fabrication of these devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Integrated devices or assemblies comprising multiple superconducting elements -> H10N69/00  **Glossary:** - Abrikosov vortex: vortex of supercurrent in a superconductor circulating around the normal conducting core of the vortex - cryotron: device that uses externally induced switching of a current carrying element between superconductive and normal states by electric, magnetic or heating means, e.g. using a gate conductor, coil, resistive heater - Tc: critical temperature; Curie temperature; Curie point - high Tc: Tc above 30 K; more frequently Tc above 90 K, may be cooled by liquid nitrogen
[H10N 60/01] Manufacture or treatment -- H10N 60/01 covers processes for producing or modifying superconducting solid-state devices, including fabrication steps such as material deposition, annealing, patterning, doping and surface treatment that create or improve the superconducting properties of the device. It is a sub-category of superconducting devices within electric solid-state devices, semiconductor devices, and the broader electricity field.
[H10N 60/0128] of composite superconductor filaments  comprising copper oxide  H10N60/0268 -- This CPC subclass H10N 60/0128 covers composite superconducting filaments that incorporate copper-oxide materials, such as high-temperature oxide superconductors used in wires or tapes for power and magnetic applications. It is nested under electricity → semiconductor devices → electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for → superconducting devices.
[H10N 60/0156] of devices comprising Nb or an alloy of Nb with one or more of the elements of group IVB, e.g. titanium, zirconium or hafnium -- This subclass pertains to superconducting solid-state devices built from niobium or niobium alloys that incorporate one or more group-IVB elements such as titanium, zirconium or hafnium. It covers any device in which the superconducting material is based on Nb or its specified alloys.
[H10N 60/0184] of devices comprising intermetallic compounds of type A-15, e.g. Nb 3 Sn -- The CPC subclass H10N 60/0184 covers superconducting devices that incorporate intermetallic A-15-type compounds such as niobium-tin (Nb₃Sn). These materials are used for their high critical temperature and current-carrying capability in applications like magnets, transmission lines and other solid-state superconducting components.
[H10N 60/0212] of devices comprising molybdenum chalcogenides -- H10N 60/0212 designates superconducting devices that incorporate molybdenum chalcogenide materials. These devices exploit the high-temperature superconductivity of compounds such as MoS2 or MoSe2 for applications like quantum circuits, magnetic shielding, or low-loss interconnects. The classification falls under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for.
[H10N 60/0241] of devices comprising nitrides or carbonitrides -- H10N 60/0241 refers to superconducting solid-state devices that incorporate nitride or carbonitride materials as the active element. These compounds, such as nitride-based high-temperature superconductors, are used to fabricate components like Josephson junctions, microwave resonators or power-handling devices that operate at cryogenic temperatures.
[H10N 60/0268] of devices comprising copper oxide -- This subclass includes superconducting devices whose active material is a copper-oxide compound, such as cuprate high-temperature superconductors used in wires, thin-film elements, magnets and related components. It covers structures and assemblies where copper oxide provides the superconducting property.
[H10N 60/0296] Processes for depositing or forming copper oxide superconductor layers -- H10N 60/0296 covers methods for depositing or forming copper-oxide superconductor layers, such as thin-film fabrication, chemical-vapor deposition, sputtering, or annealing processes, used to create superconducting components within electric solid-state devices. These processes enable the manufacture of high-temperature superconducting circuits and interconnects.
[H10N 60/0324] from a solution -- The CPC subclass H10N 60/0324 covers superconducting devices that are produced from a solution, meaning the superconducting material is formed by processes such as solution casting, coating, printing or precipitation of a liquid precursor onto a substrate to create the active element. This includes methods where the superconducting film or component originates from a soluble compound that is later converted to the superconducting phase.
[H10N 60/0352] from a suspension or slurry, e.g. screen printing or doctor blade casting -- H10N 60/0352 covers the formation of superconducting solid-state devices by depositing the active material from a suspension or slurry, such as using screen-printing or doctor-blade casting techniques, to create patterned conductive or functional layers.
[H10N 60/0381] by evaporation, e.g. MBE -- This subclass covers superconducting devices produced by evaporation techniques, especially molecular-beam epitaxy, where material is vaporised and deposited to form thin superconducting layers, multilayers or patterned structures. It includes the use of evaporative growth for fabricating superconducting films and related components.
[H10N 60/0408] by sputtering -- H10N 60/0408 covers superconducting solid-state devices that are manufactured by sputtering, a physical-vapor-deposition technique that creates thin-film superconducting layers on a substrate. This subclass specifies the use of sputtered films in the construction of electric semiconductor devices requiring superconductivity.
[H10N 60/0436] by chemical vapour deposition [CVD] -- The H10N 60/0436 subclass classifies superconducting devices that are produced by chemical vapour deposition. It pertains to electric solid-state devices not elsewhere specified, within the broader categories of semiconductor devices and electricity, and focuses on CVD-fabricated superconductors.
[H10N 60/0464] by metalloorganic chemical vapour deposition [MOCVD] -- This CPC subclass H10N 60/0464 falls under electric solid-state devices, specifically superconducting devices, and is limited to those fabricated using metalloorganic chemical vapour deposition (MOCVD). It addresses manufacturing methods that employ metal-organic precursors to deposit superconducting thin films or structures. The code is used for patent documents describing such processes.
[H10N 60/0492] by thermal spraying, e.g. plasma deposition -- The H10N 60/0492 subclass covers superconducting devices that are produced by thermal-spraying methods, such as plasma deposition, where a molten or semi-molten material is sprayed onto a substrate to form the superconducting layer. It pertains to solid-state superconductors made using these coating techniques.
[H10N 60/0521] by pulsed laser deposition, e.g. laser sputtering -- H10N 60/0521 includes the preparation of superconducting components by means of pulsed laser deposition, including laser sputtering, where a high-energy laser ablates a target material that then condenses as a thin superconducting layer on a substrate. The subclass falls within the CPC hierarchy for electric solid-state devices, specifically superconducting devices.
[H10N 60/0548] by deposition and subsequent treatment, e.g. oxidation of pre-deposited material -- The subclass H10N 60/0548 covers superconducting solid-state devices that are formed by first depositing a material on a substrate and then altering it-such as by oxidising the pre-deposited layer-to achieve the desired superconducting properties. It relates to manufacturing processes that combine deposition and subsequent treatment steps.
[H10N 60/0576] characterised by the substrate -- The H10N 60/0576 subclass covers superconducting solid-state devices whose defining feature is the substrate on which the superconducting material is formed or mounted. It includes devices that rely on particular substrate materials, orientations, or structures to achieve desired superconducting properties, such as thin-film superconductors grown on specific crystal lattices.
[H10N 60/0604] Monocrystalline substrates, e.g. epitaxial growth -- The CPC subclass H10N 60/0604 covers monocrystalline substrates used for superconducting devices, particularly those prepared by epitaxial growth techniques. It includes single-crystal base materials on which thin superconducting layers are deposited to achieve high performance and low loss.
[H10N 60/0632] Intermediate layers, e.g. for growth control -- H10N 60/0632 pertains to intermediate layers used in the manufacturing of superconducting devices, such as buffer or seed layers that control crystal growth and interface properties. These layers are employed to improve film quality, adhesion, and performance in solid-state superconducting components.
[H10N 60/0661] Processes performed after copper oxide formation, e.g. patterning -- H10N 60/0661 relates to post-copper-oxide formation steps in superconducting device manufacture, covering processes such as patterning, etching or other treatments applied after the oxide layer is created to define or modify device structures. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for → superconducting devices.
[H10N 60/0688] Etching -- H10N 60/0688 designates etching techniques applied to superconducting devices. It includes processes for selectively removing material to form patterns or structures on superconducting components such as thin films, wires, and Josephson junctions, enabling the fabrication and modification of solid-state superconducting circuits.
[H10N 60/0716] Passivating -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10N 60/0716, titled "Passivating," pertains to methods and materials used to protect superconducting devices from environmental degradation, enhancing their stability and performance. This classification is part of a detailed system for categorizing patent information related to semiconductor and electric solid-state devices.
[H10N 60/0744] Manufacture or deposition of electrodes -- H10N 60/0744 covers methods for manufacturing or depositing electrodes on superconducting solid-state devices. It includes processes such as sputtering, evaporation, electro-plating and other thin-film techniques used to form conductive contacts for superconducting components.
[H10N 60/0772] Processes including the use of non-gaseous precursors -- H10N 60/0772 covers manufacturing steps for superconducting solid-state devices that use solid or liquid precursors instead of gaseous ones, including deposition, annealing, patterning or other processes where the precursor is introduced in a non-vapor form.
[H10N 60/0801] Manufacture or treatment of filaments or composite wires -- H10N 60/0801 covers processes for producing or modifying superconducting filaments and composite wires, including drawing, heat-treating, coating, and other treatments that enhance electrical or mechanical properties. It pertains to solid-state semiconductor devices where superconductivity is harnessed, focusing on the manufacturing steps that define wire geometry and material structure.
[H10N 60/0828] Introducing flux pinning centres -- Entry H10N 60/0828 covers techniques for adding flux-pinning centres to superconducting devices, aimed at improving critical current and magnetic field stability. It includes the incorporation of impurities, defects, nanoinclusions or other engineered structures that trap magnetic vortices within type-II superconductors.
[H10N 60/0856] of devices comprising metal borides, e.g. MgB 2 -- H10N 60/0856 covers superconducting solid-state devices that incorporate metal-boride compounds, notably magnesium diboride (MgB₂), as the active superconducting material. The subclass addresses the design, fabrication and application of such devices within the broader field of electricity and semiconductor technology.
[H10N 60/0884] Treatment of superconductor layers by irradiation, e.g. ion-beam, electron-beam, laser beam or X-rays -- The CPC class H10N 60/0884 covers processes that treat superconducting layers by irradiation, including ion-beam, electron-beam, laser-beam or X-ray exposure. These techniques are used to modify material properties, repair defects or enhance performance of solid-state superconducting devices. It is a subcategory of electric solid-state devices, specifically superconducting devices.
[H10N 60/0912] of Josephson-effect devices -- The H10N 60/0912 entry classifies solid-state superconducting components that employ the Josephson effect, including Josephson junctions, superconducting quantum interference devices and related circuits used for high-frequency, quantum-logic and precision-measurement applications. It falls under Electricity → Semiconductor devices → Electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for → Superconducting devices.
[H10N 60/0941] comprising high-T c  ceramic materials -- The H10N 60/0941 subclass covers superconducting devices that incorporate high-critical-temperature ceramic materials, typically cuprate-based compounds operating above liquid nitrogen temperature, and includes elements such as wires, tapes, films, and other components that exploit the ceramic's superconducting properties.
[H10N 60/10] Junction-based devices -- H10N 60/10 covers superconducting devices whose operation relies on a junction between two superconductors or between a superconductor and a normal conductor. Examples are Josephson junctions, superconducting tunnel and weak-link devices, and SQUID sensors that exploit the quantum tunneling or phase-coherence properties of the junction.
[H10N 60/11] Single-electron tunnelling devices -- Single-electron tunnelling devices are solid-state components that control the flow of individual electrons through a quantum-tunnel barrier, often employing Coulomb blockade or superconducting effects. They include single-electron transistors and quantum-dot structures used for ultra-low-power logic, charge sensing and quantum-information processing.
[H10N 60/12] Josephson-effect devices -- Josephson-effect devices are superconducting components that use the quantum tunneling of Cooper pairs across a thin insulating barrier to generate or control very high-frequency signals, voltage standards, mixers, detectors and quantum bits. They are employed in precision metrology, superconducting digital circuits and research on quantum computing.
[H10N 60/124] comprising high-T c  ceramic materials -- H10N 60/124 covers superconducting devices that incorporate high-critical-temperature ceramic materials. The class includes solid-state components such as wires, tapes, films, or bulk elements made from ceramic superconductors operating above the temperature of liquid nitrogen. These devices are classified under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for.
[H10N 60/126] comprising metal borides, e.g. MgB 2 -- The H10N 60/126 subclass pertains to superconducting devices that incorporate metal-boride compounds, most notably magnesium diboride (MgB₂). These materials are valued for their relatively high critical temperatures and are employed in components such as wires, magnets and other solid-state superconducting applications.
[H10N 60/128] having three or more electrodes, e.g. transistor-like structures -- The subclass H10N 60/128 covers superconducting solid-state devices that incorporate three or more electrodes, typically in transistor-like configurations for controlling supercurrents. It includes structures where multiple contacts enable active modulation of superconducting behavior in devices such as superconducting field-effect transistors and related multi-terminal components.
[H10N 60/20] Permanent superconducting devices -- H10N 60/20 refers to permanent superconducting devices, i.e., components that exploit the zero-resistance property of superconductors to sustain persistent currents without external power. The class includes items such as superconducting magnets, storage coils, flux-trapping circuits and other devices designed for long-term, loss-free operation.
[H10N 60/202] comprising metal borides, e.g. MgB 2 -- H10N 60/202 covers superconducting devices that incorporate metal borides such as magnesium diboride (MgB2). These devices exploit the high critical temperature and current-carrying capacity of metal-boride superconductors for applications in electronics and power systems. The classification falls under the CPC subgroup for electric solid-state devices.
[H10N 60/203] comprising high-T c  ceramic materials -- This subclass covers solid-state devices that incorporate high-Tc ceramic superconductors, addressing their composition, fabrication methods, and use in applications such as magnets, filters, or power transmission components.
[H10N 60/205] having three or more electrodes, e.g. transistor-like structures   H10N60/128  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry H10N 60/205 pertains to superconducting devices with three or more electrodes, such as transistor-like structures, where the classification H10N 60/128 takes precedence for specific aspects.
[H10N 60/207] Field effect devices -- H10N 60/207 covers field-effect devices that use an electric field to control charge transport in solid-state components, including MOSFETs, JFETs and related semiconductor structures, particularly those employing superconducting materials. These inventions focus on the design of gates, channels and insulating layers that enable voltage-controlled switching or amplification in integrated circuits.
[H10N 60/208] based on Abrikosov vortices -- H10N 60/208 covers superconducting devices that exploit Abrikosov vortices, the quantized magnetic flux lines that form in type-II superconductors under a magnetic field. It includes structures, circuits or systems that manipulate, control or detect the motion or arrangement of these vortices for functions such as memory, logic, sensing or flux-pinning enhancement.
[H10N 60/30] Devices switchable between superconducting and normal states -- The classification covers devices that can be deliberately toggled between a superconducting state, where electrical resistance is essentially zero, and a normal conductive state with finite resistance. It includes switches, relays, or other components that exploit the transition for control, protection, or modulation of electric currents.
[H10N 60/35] Cryotrons -- H10N 60/35 covers cryotrons, which are superconducting devices that act as switches by locally heating a segment of a superconducting element to drive it into the normal state and interrupt current flow. They are used in low-temperature electronic circuits, cryogenic switching, and specialized sensing applications.
[H10N 60/355] Power cryotrons -- H10N 60/355 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, categorizes power cryotrons, which are superconducting devices designed for controlling high-power electrical circuits at very low temperatures.
[H10N 60/80] Constructional details -- H10N 60/80 relates to the constructional details of superconducting devices, covering design features such as geometry, material choices, interconnections, packaging, and cooling arrangements that enable the device to maintain superconductivity. It falls under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10N 60/805] for Josephson-effect devices -- H10N 60/805 covers superconducting devices that exploit the Josephson effect, such as Josephson junctions, voltage standards, superconducting quantum interference devices and related circuit elements. The classification applies to structures where a thin insulating barrier separates two superconductors, enabling tunneling of Cooper pairs and rapid switching. It is used for patents on quantum-level superconducting electronics and related measurement or signal-processing components.
[H10N 60/81] Containers -- The H10N 60/81 subclass pertains to containers used with superconducting devices, including vessels, housings or enclosures that maintain the low-temperature or magnetic-shielding conditions required for superconductors. It covers designs for storage, transport or integration of superconducting components in solid-state electric apparatus.
[H10N 60/815] for Josephson-effect devices -- H10N 60/815 designates superconducting devices that employ the Josephson effect, covering elements such as Josephson junctions, superconducting quantum-interference devices, rapid-single-flux-quantum circuits and related structures that use the tunnelling of Cooper pairs for ultra-fast or highly sensitive electronic functions.
[H10N 60/82] Current path -- The H10N 60/82 "Current path" subgroup deals with the layout and characteristics of the supercurrent's trajectory within superconducting devices, such as the geometry of superconducting wires, films, or circuit elements that guide the flow of electric current without resistance. It covers design aspects that define how the current is directed, confined, or distributed in solid-state superconducting components.
[H10N 60/83] Element shape -- The classification H10N 60/83 covers the geometric form of superconducting components, including wires, tapes, films, bulk pieces and other shapes that influence current-carrying capacity, magnetic properties and thermal behavior. It pertains to how the element's shape is defined or modified for use in solid-state superconducting devices.
[H10N 60/84] Switching means for devices switchable between superconducting and normal states -- The entry covers switching mechanisms that allow a device to be toggled between its superconducting state and a normal conductive state, including cryogenic relays, thermal, magnetic or electronic switches designed for use in superconducting circuits and systems.
[H10N 60/85] Superconducting active materials -- H10N 60/85 covers superconducting active materials, i.e., the substances that exhibit superconductivity and are employed in devices to achieve zero-resistance current flow. It includes elemental, alloy, ceramic and composite superconductors, as well as doped or processed forms used as wires, thin films, bulk bodies, or other functional components in superconducting circuits.
[H10N 60/851] Organic superconductors -- H10N 60/851 designates organic superconductors, a subcategory of electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for within the broader fields of electricity and semiconductor devices. These materials are carbon-based compounds that exhibit superconductivity at low temperatures, enabling loss-free electrical conduction for specialized applications.
[H10N 60/853] Fullerene superconductors, e.g. soccer ball-shaped allotropes of carbon, e.g. C 60  or C 94 -- This classification covers superconducting materials based on fullerene molecules such as C60 or C94, which have a spherical soccer-ball carbon structure and exhibit superconductivity when doped or cooled. It pertains to solid-state devices that employ these carbon allotropes as superconductors.
[H10N 60/855] Ceramic superconductors -- H10N 60/855 designates ceramic superconductors, which are high-temperature superconducting devices based on ceramic compounds such as cuprates. The class covers the materials, their fabrication, shaping, and incorporation into electrical components, circuits, magnets and power applications.
[H10N 60/857] comprising copper oxide -- H10N 60/857 covers superconducting devices that employ copper-oxide materials as the active superconducting element. These include solid-state components such as cables, magnets, or fault-current limiters where copper-oxide ceramics provide the superconducting behavior.
[H10N 60/858] having multilayered structures, e.g. superlattices -- H10N 60/858 covers superconducting solid-state devices that incorporate multilayered constructions such as superlattices, where alternating layers of superconducting and other materials are stacked to modify and enhance the device's electrical characteristics.
[H10N 60/99] Alleged superconductivity -- H10N 60/99 classifies patent documents that claim a material or device exhibits superconductivity but the superconducting behavior is not yet demonstrably verified. It covers alleged or tentative superconducting performance, including proposed compositions, structures or operating conditions said to achieve zero resistance. The subclass is used when the superconducting nature is asserted rather than experimentally confirmed.
[H10N 69/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one superconducting element covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrated devices comprising components that are individually covered by main group H10N60/00 , e.g. Josephson junction devices either with components of the same kind, (e.g. Josephson junction arrays), or with components of a different kind, (e.g. semiconductor diodes, transistors).  Assemblies of multiple devices comprising devices that are individually covered by group H10N60/00 that are not provided for elsewhere in the IPC.
[H10N 70/00] Other electric solid-state devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices wherein the electrical conductivity within a bulk solid-state body is directly influenced by an electrical current flowing through the body or by an electrical field that is applied to the body. This place also covers the active materials and fabrication of such devices.  These include devices based on:  The group H10N70/00 itself covers conductor-insulator-conductor devices, e.g. metal-insulator-metal [MIM] diodes, having one branch in their current-voltage characteristics only; the insulator (I) may comprise multiple different insulator layers. It extends also to transistor like MIMIM devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Integrated devices or assemblies comprising solid-state elements without a potent-jump barrier or surface barrier -> H10N79/00
[H10N 70/011] Manufacture or treatment of multistable switching devices -- H10N 70/011 concerns processes for making or treating multistable switching devices, a type of solid-state semiconductor component that can remain in two or more stable states without power. It falls under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for, covering fabrication steps, surface treatments, doping, encapsulation and related post-manufacture modifications.
[H10N 70/021] Formation of switching materials, e.g. deposition of layers -- The code H10N 70/021 covers processes for creating the active switching material in solid-state electric devices, such as the deposition of thin films or layers that form the functional part of a switch. It includes techniques for forming, coating, or otherwise producing the material that enables the device to change its conductive state.
[H10N 70/023] by chemical vapor deposition, e.g. MOCVD, ALD -- H10N 70/023 classifies electric solid-state devices that are produced using chemical vapor deposition techniques, including metal-organic chemical vapor deposition and atomic layer deposition, as methods for forming semiconductor structures not covered by other specific categories. It falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for → other electric solid-state devices.
[H10N 70/026] by physical vapor deposition, e.g. sputtering -- The CPC subclass H10N 70/026 falls under electricity → semiconductor devices → electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for, and pertains to other electric solid-state devices that are made by physical vapor deposition methods such as sputtering.
[H10N 70/028] by conversion of electrode material, e.g. oxidation -- H10N 70/028 covers solid-state devices whose operation relies on chemically converting the electrode material, such as by oxidation or reduction. It falls within electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for, a sub-group of semiconductor devices under the broader electricity class.
[H10N 70/041] Modification of switching materials after formation, e.g. doping  shaping  H10N70/061 -- H10N 70/041 covers post-formation modification of switching materials in electric solid-state devices, such as doping, reshaping or other treatments that alter the material's properties after it has been fabricated. This subclass falls under "Other electric solid-state devices" within the semiconductor devices group.
[H10N 70/043] by implantation -- The code H10N 70/043 covers semiconductor devices that are manufactured using ion implantation techniques. It falls under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for, indicating that the defining feature of the device is the incorporation of dopants by implantation rather than other doping methods. This classification is used for patents describing structures or processes where implantation is the primary step in creating the device's electrical characteristics.
[H10N 70/046] by diffusion, e.g. photo-dissolution -- H10N 70/046 pertains to electric solid-state devices whose semiconductor materials are formed or altered by diffusion processes, such as photo-dissolution where light induces the diffusion of dopants or removal of material.
[H10N 70/061] Shaping switching materials -- H10N 70/061 covers methods for shaping or structuring switching materials used in solid-state devices, including patterning, forming, or depositing phase-change, ferroelectric, or memristive materials to define functional regions. The classification applies to processes that modify the geometry or layout of these materials in devices such as memory cells, selectors, or other switching elements.
[H10N 70/063] by etching of pre-deposited switching material layers, e.g. lithography -- H10N 70/063 designates solid-state electric devices that are formed by etching pre-deposited switching-material layers, for example using lithographic processes. The subclass covers manufacturing steps where patterning of the active material defines the device's switching regions and is applied to devices not specifically classified elsewhere.
[H10N 70/066] by filling of openings, e.g. damascene method -- This subclass covers processes where pre-formed openings in a semiconductor substrate or dielectric are filled with conductive material to create interconnects, exemplified by damascene techniques. It includes steps for depositing, planarising and patterning the fill to form reliable circuit pathways in integrated circuits.
[H10N 70/068] by processes specially adapted for achieving sub-lithographic dimensions, e.g. using spacers -- H10N 70/68 covers manufacturing methods for semiconductor devices that employ specially adapted techniques to create structures smaller than the lithographic limit, such as using spacer-defined features. It is categorized under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for in the CPC hierarchy.
[H10N 70/10] Solid-state travelling-wave devices -- H10N 70/10 designates solid-state travelling-wave devices, which are semiconductor components that generate, amplify or control microwave and millimetre-wave signals by means of a wave that propagates along a transmission structure. The class includes solid-state travelling-wave amplifiers, oscillators and related circuit elements used in radar, communications and sensing.
[H10N 70/151] Charge density wave transport devices -- Charge density wave transport devices are solid-state components that use the collective motion of electrons in a periodic charge-density modulation to convey electrical signals, often in quasi-one-dimensional semiconductor or metallic crystals. They exploit the nonlinear conductivity of charge-density-wave phases for switching, amplification or signal processing.
[H10N 70/20] Multistable switching devices, e.g. memristors -- H10N 70/20 covers multistable switching devices such as memristors, which are solid-state components capable of retaining multiple resistance states without power, enabling non-volatile memory and neuromorphic computing applications. These devices belong to the electric solid-state devices subclass within semiconductor device classifications.
[H10N 70/231] based on solid-state phase change, e.g. between amorphous and crystalline phases, Ovshinsky effect -- This subclass includes devices that operate by a reversible solid-state phase change, such as the Ovshinsky effect, where a material switches between amorphous and crystalline states to alter its electrical characteristics. It covers memory cells, switches and similar components that use this transition.
[H10N 70/235] between different crystalline phases, e.g. cubic and hexagonal -- H10N 70/235 covers electric solid-state devices that employ a junction or transition between two distinct crystal structures, such as cubic and hexagonal phases, within the semiconductor material. The classification includes designs, methods, and structures that exploit the interface or phase change to influence electrical or optical properties of the device.
[H10N 70/24] based on migration or redistribution of ionic species, e.g. anions, vacancies -- H10N 70/24 covers solid-state devices that operate by migration or redistribution of ionic species such as anions or vacancies. These devices rely on ion movement within the material to produce a functional effect, distinct from conventional electronic charge transport.
[H10N 70/245] the species being metal cations, e.g. programmable metallization cells -- The code H10N 70/245 covers solid-state devices that use mobile metal cations to form conductive filaments for switching, such as programmable metallization cells. It applies to resistive-memory or selector elements where metal-ion migration, not electronic charge transport, provides the functional change.
[H10N 70/25] based on bulk electronic defects, e.g. trapping of electrons -- The H10N 70/25 class covers solid-state devices whose operation depends on bulk electronic defects such as electron-trapping sites. It includes components that exploit these defect states for functions like memory, sensing, or control, without falling under more specific semiconductor categories.
[H10N 70/253] having three or more electrodes, e.g. transistor-like devices -- This CPC subgroup H10N 70/253 pertains to electric solid-state devices that incorporate three or more electrodes, such as transistor-like structures used to control or amplify electrical signals. It classifies solid-state components that are not otherwise covered by more specific semiconductor categories.
[H10N 70/257] having switching assisted by radiation or particle beam, e.g. optically controlled devices -- A solid-state device whose switching operation is aided by radiation or a particle beam, such as optically controlled switches. The classification covers devices that use light, electrons, ions or other particles to trigger or modulate electrical conduction. It is applied to semiconductor components where a beam-induced effect assists the transition between states.
[H10N 70/801] Constructional details of multistable switching devices -- H10N 70/801 concerns the structural features of multistable switching devices such as latches and flip-flops that retain one of several stable states without continuous power. The classification covers the arrangement of contacts, electrodes, insulating layers and other constructional elements that enable the device to change and hold distinct states.
[H10N 70/821] Device geometry -- H10N 70/821 covers the geometry of semiconductor devices, including the shape, dimensions, and spatial arrangement of components within electric solid-state devices such as transistors, diodes, and power modules. It is applied when the physical layout influences performance or manufacturing.
[H10N 70/823] adapted for essentially horizontal current flow, e.g. bridge type devices -- The CPC subclass H10N 70/823 includes solid-state semiconductor components that are constructed so that the main current flows essentially horizontally across the device, a configuration typical of bridge-type structures used in power-conversion and switching applications.
[H10N 70/826] adapted for essentially vertical current flow, e.g. sandwich or pillar type devices -- This subclass covers solid-state semiconductor components designed so that the main current path runs vertically through the structure, such as sandwich-type or pillar-type devices, used where vertical charge transport improves performance or integration density.
[H10N 70/8265] on sidewalls of dielectric structures, e.g. mesa-shaped or cup-shaped devices -- The subgroup H10N 70/8265 covers features, methods or structures that are situated on the sidewalls of dielectric elements, such as mesa-shaped or cup-shaped semiconductor devices, within electric solid-state components.
[H10N 70/828] Current flow limiting means within the switching material region, e.g. constrictions -- This class covers features that restrict current in the active switching region of solid-state devices, such as geometric constrictions or built-in current-limiting structures, used to control overcurrent, improve reliability, and shape current waveforms in transistors, thyristors, or similar components.
[H10N 70/841] Electrodes -- The CPC subclass H10N 70/841 covers electrodes employed in electric solid-state devices that are not otherwise specified, such as those used in diodes, transistors, and similar semiconductor components. It includes the design, materials, and configuration of the conductive contacts that enable charge transfer within these devices.
[H10N 70/8413] adapted for resistive heating -- This CPC subgroup covers solid-state electric devices, such as semiconductor components, that are specifically adapted for resistive heating applications. It falls under the hierarchy electricity → semiconductor devices → electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for → other electric solid-state devices.
[H10N 70/8416] adapted for supplying ionic species -- This subclass covers solid-state electric components designed to deliver or dispense ionic species, typically for applications like solid-state batteries, ion-conducting membranes, or electrochemical sensors. Devices are solid-state, not otherwise provided for, and the classification is for their function of supplying ions.
[H10N 70/8418] adapted for focusing electric field or current, e.g. tip-shaped -- The H10N 70/8418 subclass covers electric solid-state devices that are shaped or configured to focus the electric field or current, such as tip-shaped emitters or electrodes used in field-emission, plasma generation or micro-electrode applications, where the geometry concentrates the field for improved performance.
[H10N 70/861] Thermal details -- H10N 70/861 covers the thermal aspects of solid-state electric devices that are not otherwise classed, detailing heat-generation, dissipation, temperature control and cooling methods for semiconductor components. It includes descriptions of thermal design, materials, and structures used to manage device temperature during operation.
[H10N 70/8613] Heating or cooling means other than resistive heating electrodes, e.g. heater in parallel -- H10N 70/8613 pertains to the classification of heating or cooling mechanisms in electric solid-state devices that do not use resistive heating electrodes, such as heaters arranged in parallel configurations.
[H10N 70/8616] Thermal insulation means -- H10N 70/8616 covers thermal-insulation means employed in electric solid-state devices such as power semiconductors. The subclass addresses structures, materials or methods that isolate heat‐generating components to protect surrounding circuitry and improve reliability. It falls under the hierarchy of electricity → semiconductor devices → electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for.
[H10N 70/881] Switching materials -- H10N 70/881 covers materials used to enable switching action in solid-state devices such as transistors and diodes. It includes semiconductors, insulators and conductive layers that change state to control current flow. The subclass falls under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for within the CPC hierarchy.
[H10N 70/882] Compounds of sulfur, selenium or tellurium, e.g. chalcogenides -- H10N 70/882 covers solid-state electric devices that use chalcogenide compounds-materials containing sulfur, selenium or tellurium such as sulfides, selenides and tellurides. These compounds are employed for functions like phase-change memory, resistive switching and other semiconductor applications.
[H10N 70/8822] Sulfides, e.g. CuS -- H10N 70/8822 covers solid-state electric devices that incorporate sulfide compounds such as copper sulfide. These materials are used as active or functional layers in semiconductor or other solid-state components not specifically covered elsewhere. The class falls within the electricity > semiconductor devices > electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for hierarchy.
[H10N 70/8825] Selenides, e.g. GeSe -- This subclass covers solid-state semiconductor components that employ selenide compounds such as germanium selenide as the active material. It includes devices where the selenide layer forms the functional element, for example photodetectors, LEDs, or transistors built from these compounds, and related processing and structures.
[H10N 70/8828] Tellurides, e.g. GeSbTe -- The CPC subclass H10N 70/8828 refers to telluride compounds such as germanium-antimony-telluride (GeSbTe) used in electric solid-state devices that are not otherwise specified. These materials are employed primarily as phase-change or memory materials in semiconductor components and related electronic applications.
[H10N 70/883] Oxides or nitrides -- The CPC subclass H10N 70/883 groups electric solid-state devices that use oxide or nitride materials, such as power transistors, diodes or sensor elements where these compounds form the active or insulating layers. It covers solid-state components not classified elsewhere within the broader semiconductor and electricity sections.
[H10N 70/8833] Binary metal oxides, e.g. TaO x -- This subclass concerns binary metal oxides, such as tantalum oxide, used as insulating or functional layers in solid-state semiconductor components and related electric devices. It covers their composition, preparation, and application in thin films, dielectric layers, and other parts of integrated circuits.
[H10N 70/8836] Complex metal oxides, e.g. perovskites, spinels -- H10N 70/8836 designates solid-state devices that employ complex metal oxides such as perovskites and spinels, which are used for their electronic, dielectric, or catalytic properties. The code falls under electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for within the broader semiconductor-device hierarchy.
[H10N 70/884] based on at least one element of group IIIA, IVA or VA, e.g. elemental or compound semiconductors  compounds of sulfur, selenium or tellurium, e.g. chalcogenides  H10N70/882 ; oxides or nitrides  H10N70/883 -- H10N 70/884 classifies electric solid-state devices that employ at least one element from groups IIIA, IVA or VA, including elemental or compound semiconductors, chalcogenides such as sulfides, selenides or tellurides, and related oxides or nitrides. It falls under the broader category of other electric solid-state devices within the CPC hierarchy for semiconductor devices.
[H10N 70/8845] Carbon or carbides -- H10N 70/8845 covers solid-state semiconductor components in which the active material is carbon or a carbide, such as carbon-based transistors or silicon-carbide power devices. These devices are classified under other electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for in the CPC.
[H10N 79/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one solid-state element covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrated devices comprising components that are individually covered by main group H10N70/00 , e.g. devices comprising bulk switching components, such as memristors, integrated either with components of the same kind, (e.g. arrays), or with components of a different kind, (e.g. semiconductor diodes, transistors).  Assemblies of multiple devices comprising devices that are individually covered by group H10N70/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Resistance change memory [ReRAM] devices -> H10B63/00 - Phase change memory [PCRAM, PRAM] devices -> H10B63/10
[H10N 80/00] Bulk negative-resistance effect devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This place also covers the fabrication of such devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Integrated devices or assemblies comprising NDR elements -> H10N89/00
[H10N 80/01] Manufacture or treatment -- The H10N 80/01 subclass concerns processes for producing or modifying bulk devices that display a negative-resistance effect, such as tunnel or Gunn diodes. It includes steps like crystal growth, doping, annealing, surface treatment, and packaging that are specific to these solid-state semiconductor components.
[H10N 80/10] Gunn-effect devices -- Gunn-effect devices are semiconductor components that exploit the bulk negative-resistance phenomenon in certain III-V materials such as GaAs to generate high-frequency oscillations. They are commonly used in microwave oscillators, amplifiers and frequency-stable sources without requiring a p-n junction.
[H10N 80/103] controlled by electromagnetic radiation -- H10N 80/103 covers solid-state devices that exhibit a bulk negative-resistance effect and are actuated or controlled by electromagnetic radiation. Typical examples include Gunn or Ridley-type oscillators and switches whose operation is initiated or modulated by light, infrared, microwave or other radiation.
[H10N 80/107] Gunn diodes -- Gunn diodes are bulk semiconductor devices that generate microwave frequencies by exploiting a bulk negative-resistance effect; they operate without a p-n junction and are used as high-power oscillators in radar, communications and sensing systems. This technology falls under CPC class H10N 80/107, a subcategory of electric solid-state devices not otherwise provided for.
[H10N 89/00] Integrated devices, or assemblies of multiple devices, comprising at least one bulk negative resistance effect element covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[H10N 89/02] Gunn-effect integrated devices -- H10N 89/02 covers integrated semiconductor devices that incorporate at least one bulk negative-resistance element exploiting the Gunn effect. Such devices combine the Gunn-effect component with other circuitry on a single chip, enabling high-frequency oscillation or amplification.
[H10N 97/00] Electric solid-state thin-film or thick-film devices, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric solid-state devices formed by thick-film technology (e.g. printing and firing of conductive pastes) or thin-film technology (e.g. PVD or CVD) and not provided for in any other subclass (e.g. not based on semiconducting materials) and not provided for in groups H10N10/00 - H10N89/00 (e.g. not based on thermoelectric, piezoelectric, electrostrictive, magnetostrictive, galvanomagnetic or bulk negative differential resistance materials; e.g. not based on bulk switching effects in devices without potential barriers), based on thin-film or thick-film technology. These devices are based on other, potentially unknown, solid-state effects for rectifying, amplifying, oscillating or switching.  For example, devices based on metal-insulator transition (also called the Mott transition) or on plasmons.  This place also covers the fabrication of such devices.
[H10N 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric solid-state devices not provided for in any other subclass and not provided for in groups H10N10/00 - H10N97/00 .
[H10N 99/03] Devices using Mott metal-insulator transition, e.g. field-effect transistor-like devices -- This group covers devices that exploit the Mott metal-insulator transition to control electrical conductivity, typically in structures resembling field-effect transistors. The transition is triggered by electrical, thermal, or optical stimuli, allowing the device to switch between conductive and insulating states without relying on conventional doping.
[H10N 99/05] Devices based on quantum mechanical effects, e.g. quantum interference devices or metal single-electron transistors -- This subclass covers solid-state electric devices that operate by exploiting quantum-mechanical phenomena, such as quantum interference devices and metal single-electron transistors, where the functional principle relies on effects like tunnelling, quantised charge transport or phase coherence rather than conventional semiconductor behavior.
[H10P] GENERIC PROCESSES OR APPARATUS FOR THE MANUFACTURE OR TREATMENT OF DEVICES COVERED BY CLASS -- This subclass covers processes or apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of devices, or parts thereof, covered by class H10 , which are generically applicable to these devices. Attention is drawn to the following: a. processes or apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of devices, or parts thereof, which are covered by a single subclass of H10B - H10N , are classified in the subclass in question. For example, the manufacture of a transistor is classified in subclass H10D ; b. processes or apparatus specially adapted for the manufacture or treatment of generic packages, interconnections, connectors or other constructional details of devices, which are covered by subclass H10W , are classified in the subclass in question. For example, the formation of a copper pillar bump connector is classified in subclass H10W .
[H10P 10/00] Building up of layers, structures or materials -- This group covers bonding of wafers or substrates either (i) before the step of making of any interconnections or (ii) before the step of packaging of devices, whichever step comes first. Attention is drawn to the following: aspects of bonding involving chips, package parts or interconnections, e.g. chip-on-chip bonding or chip-on-wafer bonding, are classified in subclass H10W , e.g. in group H10W80/00 . Group H10P10/00 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10P14/00 and H10P95/00 . Groups H10P10/00 , H10P14/00 and H10P95/00 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 10/12] Bonding of semiconductor wafers or semiconductor substrates to semiconductor wafers or semiconductor substrates  preparing SOI wafers using bonding  H10P90/1914 -- Group H10P10/12 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10P90/00 . Group H10P10/12 is also impacted by reclassification into groups H10P10/126 , H10P10/128 - H10P10/1285 , H10P54/52 , H10P56/00 , H10P90/1914 and H10P90/1916 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 10/126] characterised by the composition of the bonding layer, e.g. dopant concentration or stoichiometry -- Group H10P10/126 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10P10/12 , H10P90/1914 and H10P90/1916 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 10/128] by direct semiconductor to semiconductor bonding -- Groups H10P10/128 and H10P10/1285 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10P10/12 , H10P90/1914 and H10P90/1916 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 10/1285] by bonding laterally separated doped regions to each other -- This CPC subclass deals with semiconductor manufacturing processes that create structures by bonding doped regions that are laterally separated. It covers techniques for joining such regions to form continuous conductive paths or device features, often used in integrated circuits and power devices.
[H10P 10/14] Bonding of semiconductor wafers to insulating substrates -- Group H10P10/14 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups H10P90/1914 and H10P90/1916 . Groups H10P90/1914 , H10P90/1916 and H10P10/14 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 14/00] Formation of materials, e.g. in the shape of layers or pillars -- Group H10P14/00 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group H10P10/00 . Groups H10P10/00 and H10P14/00 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 14/20] of semiconductor materials -- The CPC subgroup H10P 14/20 deals with processes and apparatus for forming semiconductor materials, especially when creating layered or pillar-type structures used in devices. It includes methods such as deposition, epitaxy and other techniques that build the material foundation of semiconductor components.
[H10P 14/203] using transformation of metal, e.g. oxidation or nitridation -- The CPC sub-group H10P 14/203 covers semiconductor-device manufacturing methods that create material layers or pillars by chemically transforming a metal, such as through oxidation or nitridation, to form oxides, nitrides or related compounds. It belongs to the generic processes for treating devices within the electricity and semiconductor-device hierarchy.
[H10P 14/22] using physical deposition, e.g. vacuum deposition or sputtering -- H10P 14/22 covers the use of physical deposition techniques, such as vacuum deposition and sputtering, to create material layers or pillars during the manufacturing or treatment of semiconductor devices. This entry falls under the electricity section, specifically semiconductor devices, and addresses generic processes for forming such structures.
[H10P 14/24] using chemical vapour deposition [CVD] -- H10P 14/24 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers the use of chemical vapour deposition (CVD) for forming semiconductor materials, such as layers or pillar structures, during the manufacturing or treatment of devices. It falls under the hierarchy ELECTRICITY → SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES → GENERIC PROCESSES OR APPARATUS FOR THE MANUFACTURE OR TREATMENT OF DEVICES COVERED BY CLASS, specifically addressing material formation techniques.
[H10P 14/26] using liquid deposition -- H10P 14/26 covers semiconductor manufacturing processes that create material structures such as layers or pillars by applying liquids to a substrate. It includes techniques like ink-jet, spray, or spin coating where a liquid precursor is deposited and then solidified to form the desired device features. This class is part of the broader group on generic processes for making or treating semiconductor devices.
[H10P 14/263] using melted materials -- The H10P 14/263 subclass covers semiconductor manufacturing steps that employ melted material to form device structures, such as depositing or shaping layers, pillars or other geometries from a molten semiconductor source. It pertains to generic processes for creating or treating semiconductor devices by using the material in its liquid state.
[H10P 14/265] using solutions -- H10P 14/265 covers methods that employ liquid solutions to form semiconductor materials, such as depositing layers or pillars by spin coating, dip coating or ink-jet printing. It pertains to solution-based processes used in the manufacture or treatment of semiconductor devices.
[H10P 14/27] using selective deposition, e.g. simultaneous growth of monocrystalline and non-monocrystalline semiconductor materials -- H10P 14/27 covers methods that deposit semiconductor layers selectively, allowing simultaneous growth of monocrystalline and polycrystalline materials within the same process. This enables the formation of mixed-structure devices such as layered or pillar structures with differing crystalline properties.
[H10P 14/271] characterised by the preparation of substrate for selective deposition -- H10P 14/271 belongs to the CPC section on electricity, specifically to semiconductor devices and the generic processes for making or treating those devices. It covers methods that form material structures such as layers or pillars and is defined by steps that prepare a substrate so that material can be deposited selectively onto it.
[H10P 14/272] using mask materials other than SiO 2  or SiN -- The H10P 14/272 subclass covers semiconductor manufacturing steps that employ mask materials other than silicon dioxide or silicon nitride to define patterns during layer formation, such as using metal oxides, polymers or other compounds for masking in deposition or etching of layered or pillar-type structures.
[H10P 14/274] using seed materials -- H10P 14/274 covers methods for forming semiconductor layers or structures that employ seed materials to initiate or control the growth of the desired material, such as using a pre-deposited seed layer or seed pillars to promote uniform deposition. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under electricity, semiconductor devices, and generic processes for manufacturing or treating those devices. This entry pertains to techniques that use seed materials to improve layer quality or pattern formation.
[H10P 14/276] Lateral overgrowth -- Lateral overgrowth refers to processes in semiconductor manufacturing where a crystalline layer is grown laterally from a seeded region across a substrate, forming continuous films or pillars that reduce dislocations and enable high-quality material on patterned templates.
[H10P 14/278] Pendeoepitaxy -- Pendeoepitaxy is a semiconductor growth technique in which a crystalline layer expands laterally from a pre-patterned seed region, allowing material to fill trenches or pillars and to coalesce with reduced dislocations. It is employed to create high-quality layers such as GaN, InP, or SiC for optoelectronic and power devices.
[H10P 14/279] Vapour-liquid-solid growth -- The H10P 14/279 entry covers the vapour-liquid-solid (VLS) method for forming semiconductor materials, where a vapor-phase precursor reacts with a liquid catalyst to precipitate solid crystalline structures such as layers, nanowires or pillars. This generic process is used in the manufacture or treatment of semiconductor devices and falls under the broader class for semiconductor device production.
[H10P 14/29] characterised by the substrates -- Group H10P14/29 is impacted by reclassification into groups H10P14/2924 - H10P14/2925 and H10P14/2926 . Groups H10P14/29 , H10P14/2924 - H10P14/2925 and H10P14/2926 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[H10P 14/2901] Materials -- H10P 14/2901 refers to the materials employed in forming semiconductor structures such as layers or pillars. It covers the selection, composition and properties of substances used in generic processes or apparatus for manufacturing or treating semiconductor devices, focusing on how these materials are shaped and integrated.
[H10P 14/2902] being Group IVA materials -- H10P 14/2902 covers processes for forming semiconductor materials that belong to Group IVA of the periodic table, such as silicon, germanium, tin or lead, in the shape of layers, pillars or other structures. The subclass is part of the CPC hierarchy for generic manufacturing or treatment techniques applied to semiconductor devices, focusing on the deposition, epitaxy or patterning of these specific elemental or compound materials.
[H10P 14/2903] Carbon, e.g. diamond-like carbon -- H10P 14/2903 covers methods for forming carbon materials, such as diamond-like carbon, as layers, pillars or other shapes in semiconductor devices. [H10P 14/2907] being Group IIIA-VA materials -- The subclass covers processes for forming semiconductor materials that combine Group IIIA elements (such as gallium, indium or aluminum) with Group VA elements (such as arsenic, phosphorus or antimony) into layers, pillars or similar structures. The classification covers deposition, epitaxy, growth and related treatment steps used to create these nitride-based devices.

[H10P 14/2912] Antimonides -- H10P 14/2912 relates to antimonides, semiconductor compounds that contain antimony and are used as active materials in electronic devices. It is situated within the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices and generic processes for material formation.

[H10P 14/2916] Selenides -- This subclass concerns semiconductor manufacturing methods that use selenide compounds to form material layers or pillar-like structures. [H10P 14/2925] Surface structures -- H10P 14/2925 covers the creation of surface structures on semiconductor materials during fabrication, such as texturing, patterning or sculpting of layers, pillars, or other formations. [H10P 14/32] characterised by intermediate layers between substrates and deposited layers -- The CPC subclass H10P 14/32 concerns semiconductor manufacturing steps in which one or more intermediate layers are inserted between the substrate and subsequently deposited layers. These interlayers-such as buffer, diffusion or insulating films-serve to adjust adhesion, stress, electrical isolation or other material properties during the formation of layered or pillar-type structures. It is part of the broader class for electricity-related semiconductor devices and generic processes for making or treating such devices.
[H10P 14/3202] Materials thereof -- This subclass covers the substances employed in forming semiconductor structures, including bulk crystal substrates, thin films, epitaxial layers, and pillar-forming materials. It addresses the composition, doping, and physical properties of these materials used in device manufacturing.
[H10P 14/3204] being Group IVA semiconducting materials -- H10P 14/3204 covers processes for forming semiconductor materials, such as layers or pillars, that are composed of Group IV elements like silicon, germanium or tin. [H10P 14/3206] Carbon, e.g. diamond-like carbon -- H10P 14/3206 pertains to processes for forming carbon materials, such as diamond-like carbon, on semiconductor devices. [H10P 14/3211] Silicon, silicon germanium or germanium -- The CPC subclass H10P 14/3211 encompasses processes and equipment for forming silicon, silicon-germanium or germanium materials, such as layers, pillars or other structures, used in semiconductor devices. [H10P 14/3212] including tin -- The CPC subclass H10P 14/3212 covers processes for forming semiconductor materials in layered or pillar structures where tin is incorporated, such as deposition, growth, or treatment steps that include tin as a component of the resulting device.

[H10P 14/3216] Nitrides -- H10P 14/3216 covers the use of nitrides-such as gallium nitride or silicon nitride-in semiconductor manufacturing processes that form material layers or pillar structures. It pertains to generic processes or apparatuses for producing or treating devices whose performance relies on nitride-based layers.
[H10P 14/3218] Phosphides -- H10P 14/3218 refers to processes that form phosphide materials, such as depositing or shaping phosphide layers, films or pillars, for use in semiconductor devices. This subclass covers the manufacture or treatment steps that create phosphorus-based compounds as functional parts of electronic components.
[H10P 14/3221] Arsenides -- H10P 14/3221 covers processes and apparatus for forming arsenide semiconductor materials, such as depositing, growing, or patterning arsenide layers, films or pillars that become part of electronic devices. It falls under the broader categories of electricity, semiconductor devices and generic manufacturing methods for device materials.
[H10P 14/3222] Antimonides -- The CPC subclass H10P 14/3222 concerns semiconductor manufacturing processes that involve antimonide compounds. It covers methods and apparatus for forming antimonide-based materials, such as layers, films or pillar structures, used in devices within the broader electric and semiconductor device categories.
[H10P 14/3224] being Group IIB-VIA semiconductors -- H10P 14/3224 covers methods for forming Group IIB-VIA semiconductor materials, such as creating layered or pillar structures, as part of generic processes for manufacturing semiconductor devices.
[H10P 14/3226] Oxides -- H10P 14/3226 designates processes and equipment for forming oxide materials in semiconductor device fabrication, including deposition, oxidation, and shaping of oxide layers or pillars. It covers steps that create or modify oxide films used as insulators, gate dielectrics, or other functional structures within semiconductor components.
[H10P 14/3228] Sulfides -- This CPC subclass covers processes for forming sulfide materials used in semiconductor devices, such as depositing or growing sulfide layers or pillars. It falls under electricity, then semiconductor devices, then generic manufacturing or treatment processes, specifically the formation of materials in layer or pillar form.

[H10P 14/3232] Tellurides -- H10P 14/3232 pertains to the formation of telluride semiconductor materials, encompassing processes and apparatus for depositing, growing, or shaping layers, films, pillars or similar structures composed of telluride compounds used in electronic devices.

It covers generic manufacturing steps used in devices within the electricity and semiconductor device subclasses.
[H10P 14/3236] being chalcogenide semiconducting materials not being oxides, e.g. ternary compounds -- The CPC entry H10P 14/3236 covers semiconductor materials that contain chalcogen elements but are not oxides, such as various ternary compounds. It relates to processes that form these materials in shapes like layers or pillars for use in semiconductor devices.
[H10P 14/3238] being insulating materials -- The CPC subclass H10P 14/3238 covers processes, apparatus and methods that involve the use of insulating materials when forming semiconductor structures, such as depositing, patterning or shaping insulating layers, films or pillars. It relates to the creation of dielectric regions that isolate or separate active elements in devices like transistors, capacitors and integrated circuits.
[H10P 14/3241] being conductive materials -- Code H10P 14/3241 pertains to processes or apparatus for forming conductive materials in semiconductor devices, such as depositing or shaping layers, pillars, or other structures that provide electrical conductivity. It includes techniques like plating, sputtering, epitaxial growth or other methods used to create conductive features within the device.
[H10P 14/3242] Structure -- The H10P 14/3242 subclass covers the physical structure of semiconductor devices that are created by forming materials in defined shapes such as layers, pillars or similar geometries, focusing on the arrangement, geometry and internal configuration of those formed elements within the device.
[H10P 14/3244] Layer structure -- H10P 14/3244 relates to semiconductor device manufacturing and covers methods or apparatus for forming material layers or pillar-like structures that become part of a device. It specifies the creation of multilayered or stacked configurations, including the geometry, composition and deposition techniques used to build the layer structure. This subclass falls under the broader processes for treating devices covered by the ELECTRICITY-SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES hierarchy.
[H10P 14/3246] Monolayers -- The H10P 14/3246 subclass deals with processes and equipment for creating monolayer-thick films-atomic or molecular-scale layers-on semiconductor substrates. It covers techniques that deposit, grow, or pattern such ultra-thin layers as part of device fabrication, especially where the material is formed as a layer or pillar. This entry falls under the broader category of semiconductor device manufacturing.
[H10P 14/3248] consisting of two layers -- H10P 14/3248 covers semiconductor manufacturing steps where the structure is built from two distinct material layers, such as thin films or stacked pillars, formed sequentially or simultaneously. It includes processes for depositing, patterning, bonding or treating each layer to achieve the desired electrical or mechanical properties in the final device.
[H10P 14/3251] consisting of three or more layers -- This CPC subgroup covers manufacturing processes that create semiconductor structures composed of three or more stacked layers, such as multilayer films, epitaxial stacks or pillar arrays. It includes methods for depositing, bonding, or treating successive layers to form functional devices.
[H10P 14/3252] Alternating layers, e.g. superlattice -- A CPC subclass in the electricity-semiconductor devices hierarchy, H10P 14/3252 covers processes for forming alternating layers, such as superlattices, in semiconductor structures. It pertains to manufacturing techniques that create layered or pillar-shaped material arrangements for device functionality.
[H10P 14/3254] Graded layers -- The H10P 14/3254 subclass covers techniques for producing semiconductor structures in which the material composition or physical properties change continuously across the thickness, forming graded layers or pillars. It includes processes and equipment that create such compositional gradients to tailor electrical or optical performance in devices.
[H10P 14/3256] Microstructure -- H10P 14/3256 (Microstructure) covers methods and apparatus for creating the micro-scale architecture of semiconductor materials, such as forming layered or pillar-shaped structures that define device performance. It falls under generic processes for producing semiconductor devices, emphasizing material formation techniques that shape the internal microstructure.
[H10P 14/3258] Crystal orientation -- The H10P 14/3258 subclass concerns techniques for controlling the crystallographic orientation of semiconductor layers or pillars during formation, including methods of epitaxial growth, substrate preparation and post-growth treatments that align grains or crystals to achieve desired electrical properties. It falls under generic processes for manufacture of devices.
[H10P 14/34] Deposited materials, e.g. layers -- H10P 14/34 concerns processes for creating deposited materials, such as thin-film layers, pillars or other structures, on semiconductor substrates. It includes the methods and apparatus used to form these layers by vapor, chemical or atomic layer deposition techniques for electronic devices.
[H10P 14/3402] characterised by the chemical composition -- H10P 14/3402 pertains to semiconductor manufacturing steps where the material being formed - typically layers or pillars - is distinguished by its specific chemical makeup, such as particular dopant ratios, alloy constituents, or compound formulations. The classification captures processes or apparatuses that rely on that composition to achieve the desired electrical or structural properties.
[H10P 14/3404] being Group IVA materials -- The H10P 14/3404 entry covers semiconductor manufacturing steps that form materials made from Group IVA elements-such as silicon, germanium, tin or lead-into structured forms like layers, films or pillars. It is used to classify processes that create or treat these Group IVA-based semiconductor structures during device fabrication.

[H10P 14/3411] Silicon, silicon germanium or germanium -- H10P 14/3411 relates to manufacturing steps that create semiconductor material such as silicon, silicon-germanium or germanium in forms like thin films, layers, pillars or other structures used in electronic devices. The subclass includes deposition, epitaxy, growth and patterning techniques specifically for these materials.

[H10P 14/3414] being group IIIA-VIA materials -- H10P 14/3414 covers processes for forming semiconductor materials that combine elements from group IIIA (such as gallium, indium, aluminium) with elements from group VIA (such as arsenic, phosphorus, antimony). The entry includes methods for depositing, growing or shaping these III-V compounds into layers, pillars or other structures used in electronic and optoelectronic devices.
[H10P 14/3416] Nitrides -- The H10P 14/3416 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to the formation of nitride semiconductor materials, such as gallium nitride, aluminium nitride, or indium nitride, in thin-film layers, pillars or other shaped structures. It covers processes such as epitaxial growth, deposition, and treatment used to produce devices like LEDs, laser diodes and power transistors.
[H10P 14/3418] Phosphides -- H10P 14/3418 pertains to the formation of phosphide materials, typically in specific shapes like layers or pillars, during the manufacturing processes of semiconductor devices. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on the electrical and material aspects of device fabrication.
